This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds, modest marginal per-capita return (1.8 multiplier split four ways) and a small, costly reward mechanism (3 coins cost to reward giver per unit, 2 coins benefit to recipient per unit). Typical dynamics: early rounds show moderate cooperation while players test the group; since players do not see others' contributions, early information is limited and signals come mainly from reward actions. Because reward is costly, it will be used sparingly and usually to reinforce consistent cooperators rather than to punish defectors. Expect some conditional cooperation: players who perceive reciprocation (via receiving rewards) will tend to sustain contributions, while those who appear to free-ride attract no rewards and may cause gradual declines. Toward the end (final rounds) expect an "end-game" drop in contributions as incentives to maintain reputation fade. Given the player profiles, SLOTH is more likely to favor lower effort and drift downward over time; DUCK and GORILLA are moderately cooperative early on; CHICKEN behaves strategically and may reduce contributions earlier than DUCK and GORILLA. Rewards will be occasional 1-unit gestures aimed at regular cooperators.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with binary contribution choices each round (contribute all 20 coins to the shared pot, or withdraw all 20 for private use). The public pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among four players, so contributions benefit the whole group but are individually costly. Typical dynamics: early rounds often see some attempts at cooperation (players testing each other's willingness to contribute), then a decline as free-riding pays off and trust erodes. Messages can sustain cooperation if credible reciprocity or social pressure emerges; without enforceable punishment, end-game defection is common (players withdraw in late rounds because of backward induction).

Given the players' profiles: GORILLA and DUCK (both older, highly educated, in stable employment) are more likely to try to sustain cooperation and to send conditional messages. SLOTH (younger, part-time employed) has a tendency to prioritize ease/comfort and may free-ride early. CHICKEN (younger, high-school education) may be inconsistent — sometimes cooperative, sometimes responsive to short-term incentives. I therefore predict initial cooperation attempts by GORILLA and DUCK, some early free-riding by SLOTH (and possibly CHICKEN), gradual erosion of cooperation with occasional brief recoveries triggered by messages, and final-round defection by most or all players.

game_explanation: This online public goods game (PGG) with 29 rounds pits private incentives (keep coins) against collective gain (contribute to the pot, which is multiplied by 1.8 and split). Each player faces the dominant short-term incentive to withdraw (free-ride) because each coin kept yields 1 privately while each coin contributed returns only 0.45 to the contributor (1.8/4). Over repeated rounds, however, players can use costly punishment and reward to encourage cooperation. Punishment is relatively efficient at deterring defection (cost 3 to the punisher, -4 to the target), while rewards are more costly and less powerful per unit (cost 3, benefit +2 to the recipient). Because players know the game horizon (29 rounds), we expect some cooperation early on from social preferences and uncertainty, with gradual erosion as self-interested incentives and learning take effect. Punishments and rewards will be used sparingly and strategically — mainly in response to clear defection or to reinforce reliable contributors — and retaliation is possible but limited because of the costliness of sanctions. Overall pattern: moderate-to-high contributions at the start, intermittent defections by one or two players, sparse targeted punishments to curb obvious free-riding, occasional small rewards to sustain cooperators, and a slow drift toward lower contributions by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 10-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round players decide how many of the 20 coins to withdraw privately (so contribution = 20 - withdrawn). The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. After payoffs are calculated, players may spend coins to punish (cost 1 each, deduct 3 from a target per unit) or reward (cost 1 each, give 1 to a target per unit) specific peers. Players see only simultaneous choices each round and can identify who punished/rewarded them.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: without history, modest cooperation is common (mid-range contributions). Players test others' tendencies.
- If someone free-rides strongly in an early round, a small number of peers will punish sparingly to signal norm enforcement. Punishment is costly, so it is used only occasionally and in small units.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly, typically by those who sustain cooperation (or by a consistent high contributor) to positively reinforce others near the start/mid game.
- Over rounds players adjust to each other's patterns: occasional punishments nudge free-riders back toward contributing, and occasional rewards encourage cooperators.
- Near the very end (rounds 9–10) some backward-induction pressure can reduce contributions as players anticipate fewer returns from cooperation; punishments still occur but remain limited.
- Overall expect moderate cooperation with some variation, a few directed punishments of low contributors, and a couple of occasional rewards.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 23 rounds, four players, and a simple binary contribution choice each round (contribute all 20 coins or withdraw all 20). If a player contributes, they forgo the 20 private coins but share the multiplied public pot; if they withdraw, they keep 20 plus still receive the shared return. Alone, withdrawing strictly dominates contributing in a single round, but with recurring interaction and a punishment stage (1 coin cost to punish, 2 coins deducted from the target per punishment unit), cooperators can deter free-riding and sustain contributions. Expect early experimentation: some players may test cooperation while others free-ride. Punishment will be used sparingly at first to signal norms (1-unit punishments are common). If punishers are consistent, cooperation will rise midgame. Near the final rounds, backward induction and declining incentives to punish lead to more defection: players are likelier to free-ride in the last few rounds, and many will fully withdraw in round 23. Chats will be sparse — short requests, warnings, and occasional complaints — and punishments will be targeted to repeat free-riders rather than used broadly.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game is a repeated 6-round interaction with binary contribution choices each round (either contribute all 20 coins to the public pot or withdraw all 20 for private use). The public pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so a lone defector immediately gains privately (20) plus their share of the multiplied pot, while cooperators boost the group return but forgo the private 20. Because players see only aggregate punish/reward totals (not who did them) and the game has a known finite horizon, we should expect:

- Early rounds: a mix of cooperation and isolated defections. Some players will test the waters; others will cooperate.
- Punishment and rewards will be used sparingly to enforce cooperation or signal approval; punishments typically follow visible defections and rewards follow consistent cooperation.
- Mid-game: cooperation tends to rise if punishments are credible; a few players who defect may be punished once or twice and then return to cooperating.
- Final round: incentives to cooperate weaken (no future consequences), so more defection is likely; players may still use a final reward to thank consistent cooperators.

Using the provided profiles as priors (SLOTH implies occasional shirking; DUCK is older and may be conformity- or norm-enforcing; CHICKEN and GORILLA are educated and likely conditional cooperators), I predict modest use of punishment early to correct defectors, a small amount of rewarding when cooperation stabilizes, and somewhat more defection in round 6.

The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally. After payoffs are distributed, players may spend costly reward units (cost 3 to sender, gives 4 to recipient). Players don't see others' choices before deciding, so early rounds are noisy tests of cooperation. Expect most high-education profiles (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) to start cooperative and use small, targeted rewards to support cooperators or withhold rewards from free-riders. SLOTH's profile suggests a higher prior for free-riding early on; after being ignored or not rewarded, SLOTH may shift toward cooperation midgame. Near the end (round 6) decline in contributions is typical, so expect more defections then. Rewarding will be sparse and targeted — mostly 1–2 units — used to signal approval or to shore up cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the communal pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so the group payoff depends on how many people contribute. A contributor's round payoff equals the equal share from the multiplied pot, while a free-rider also keeps the 20 they withdrew plus that same equal share. Incentives: individually, free-riding in a given round yields higher private payoff if others contribute; collectively, everyone is better off if all contribute. With a known finite horizon (23 rounds) and no binding contracts, cooperation tends to be fragile and can unravel toward the end (final-round defection). However, the chat channel can help sustain cooperation by allowing promises, appeals to fairness, or reputational signaling.

Given the player profiles: expect some polite cooperation attempts (SLOTH, DUCK), pragmatic tests and strategic moves (GORILLA), and occasional blunt, self-interested choices (CHICKEN). Chats will be sparse but used strategically to ask for cooperation or call out free-riding. Early rounds may see mixed cooperation as players test each other; occasional rounds of full cooperation are possible if trust builds. Toward the late rounds (final ~5 rounds) cooperation will likely collapse as the end approaches, unless strong reciprocity is repeatedly reinforced—but even then, rational backward induction makes full cooperation fragile in the last rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 3-player repeated public goods game over 6 rounds. Each round, players decide how much of their private 20-coin endowment to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because each contributed coin returns only 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to the individual, the individual incentive is to contribute 0, while the group optimum is to contribute everything. Typical dynamics: early rounds often see moderate contributions (trust-building, conditional cooperation, or pre-play promises). If some players appear to contribute less and reap higher payoffs, others tend to reduce contributions in later rounds. Messages are possible but usually scarce; they mainly serve to make promises, announce intentions, or react to observed payoffs. Since players know the game ends after round 6, defections tend to increase toward the final rounds (backloading of selfishness). Using the supplied profiles as priors: SLOTH (postgrad, employed) is slightly predisposed to conditional cooperation but may avoid being exploited; GORILLA (high school) is somewhat uncertain and responsive to payoffs; DUCK (college-educated) is likely to be pragmatic and may attempt to coordinate early but will punish persistent free-riding. Expect moderate cooperation early, then a decline as players respond to relative payoffs, with strong defection in the final round.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a tension between private and collective incentives. Each player can contribute any integer from 0–20; contributed coins are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so a dollar contributed yields 2.1/4 = 0.525 to each player (including the contributor). The private return to keeping a coin (not contributing) is 1, which is larger than the private share of the public good (0.525), so unilateral incentive is to free-ride. However, if everyone contributes a lot, the group benefit can be substantial.

Punishment and reward add strategic enforcement: a punishment unit costs the sender 1 and removes 3 from the target, so a single punishment is a costly deterrent to defecting; a reward unit costs 1 and gives 1 to the recipient, a mild positive reinforcement. Because punish/reward are costly, real players use them sparingly to reinforce norms or respond to clear free-riding. Players observe past contributions and who targeted whom after each round, so conditional cooperators tend to match others' past behavior: rewarding consistent contributors and occasionally punishing obvious low contributors. Expect moderate initial cooperation, occasional targeted punishment against persistent low contributors, some small rewards to recognize contributors, and a decline in cooperation toward the final round as endgame incentives push people to freeride.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players over 23 rounds. Each round each player either keeps their whole 20 coins (contribute 0) or leaves them all in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so an individual who keeps their 20 gets the private 20 plus whatever share results from contributions; a contributor gets no private 20 that round but receives the shared return. The dominant one-shot incentive is to withdraw the 20 (free-ride), because keeping 20 and still receiving an equal share is individually better whenever others contribute. However, social preferences and conditional cooperation often produce some contributions: some players may try to signal or pledge cooperation, others will respond to past payoffs, and inexperienced or altruistic players sometimes keep contributing even after being exploited.

Given the short finite horizon (23 rounds) and the simultaneous decision structure with only round payoffs visible afterward, I predict mostly defection (withdraw) across most rounds, with occasional cooperative moves by one or two players (especially DUCK and CHICKEN in their profiles) trying to restart cooperation. GORILLA may experiment with contributing occasionally; SLOTH — by name and profile — is more likely to free-ride consistently. Chat will be sparse and mostly short pledges or short reactions to being exploited or to call for coordination. Payoffs will therefore oscillate: high for free-riders in rounds where others contribute, low for contributors, and moderate when several cooperate.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits four players against a repeated social dilemma over 29 rounds. Each round everyone can withdraw some of 20 coins for private use; the rest is contributed to a shared pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players may pay to punish or reward others (punishment costly to sender and harsher for the target; rewards are cheaper to the target but still costly for the sender). Key incentives: contributing benefits the group but is individually costly; withdrawing (free-riding) yields immediate private gain but can elicit punishment or fewer rewards. Uncertainty stems from simultaneous decisions each round (you don't see others' moves before choosing) and from how much peers will invest in enforcement (punishment/reward). Typical dynamics in such games: modest-to-high cooperation early, occasional enforcement (mostly targeted at low contributors), gradual decline in contributions as enforcement is imperfect and future consequences wane, and sparse rewards used to sustain cooperators. Given the provided personality priors, I expect SLOTH to lean toward lower contributions (more private withdrawals), GORILLA and CHICKEN to be relatively cooperative and enforcement-prone, and DUCK to be moderate but responsive to punishment. Punishment/reward will occur but be used sparingly (single-unit actions are most common).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 3-player public goods game (16 rounds). Each round each player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. The pot is doubled and split evenly among the three players. Each coin a player contributes raises the group payoff by 1 coin (because 2 is returned and split among three, so sum of payoffs increases by 1), but it returns only 2/3 of a coin to the contributor personally. That creates a tension: socially it's best for everyone to contribute everything, but individually the best privately is to contribute 0 and free-ride.

Because players see the round-level net payoffs for everyone (their own and peers'), they can infer others' behavior and often act as conditional cooperators: they may contribute more if they believe others are contributing, and cut contributions if they detect free-riding. Given the supplied profiles, I expect SLOTH to favor low contributions (free-riding or minimal effort), GORILLA (young, educated) to start relatively cooperative and try to sustain cooperation, and DUCK (older, educated) to be somewhat cooperative but responsive. Over 16 rounds I predict initial moderate-to-high contributions from Gorilla and Duck, Sloth contributing little, producing high payoffs for Sloth and fueling a decline in cooperation. Periodic attempts by Gorilla and Duck to revive cooperation will lead to oscillations: spikes of contributions followed by drop-offs when Sloth continues to free-ride. The result is a pattern of conditional cooperation and strategic reduction — not full breakdown to zero, but sustained lower-than-social-optimal contributions.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game pits private incentives (take more coins for yourself) against group incentives (leave coins in the shared pot so everyone benefits). Each round players decide how many of the 20 coins to contribute; the total contribution is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly actions to punish (cost 3 to sender, -4 to target per unit) or reward (cost 3 to sender, +2 to target per unit). Because punishment and reward are costly, both are typically used sparingly — punishment to discipline clear free-riders or retaliation, reward to encourage high contributors. Players only see their own round summary (including who punished/rewarded them), not others' full histories, which creates uncertainty about motives. Over many rounds we expect initial conditional cooperation (moderate contributions), occasional short-run defections by one player, sparse punishment to enforce cooperation, occasional small rewards to reinforce steady contributors, and a gradual decline in contributions as the end of the known 29-round horizon approaches (end-game free-riding). Uncertainty about others' intentions and costliness of sanctions will keep sanctions rare and modest.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 rounds. Each round every player starts with 20 coins in the common pot and chooses how many to withdraw for private use; the remainder is their contribution. The total contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and shared equally, and afterward players can spend coins to punish (cost 1 to sender, −3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 1, +1 to target per unit) any peers. Because the game is finite (10 rounds), incentives change over time: early on, players have reason to build cooperation because future punishment and reward can enforce norms; toward the last rounds there is more incentive to free-ride because future retaliation is limited. Punishment and reward are costly to the sender, so they tend to be used sparingly and targeted—usually small amounts to signal norm enforcement or appreciation.

Given the provided profiles, I expect SLOTH (name and profile suggesting lower effort) to start below the group mean and adjust upward if punished or rewarded. GORILLA and DUCK (both educated, employed) will tend to contribute at or above the mid-point to sustain the public good. CHICKEN (older, norm-following) will start relatively high and often maintain high contributions. Punishment and reward will be occasional: a few small punishments targeted at repeated low contributors, and occasional small rewards for consistent high contributors or for late improvement. Late-round contributions will drift down somewhat as the end approaches.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round each player either contributes their entire 20 coins to the group pot or keeps all 20 (so contributions are all-or-nothing). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally; this creates a social incentive to contribute (everyone benefits) but a private incentive to free-ride (keep 20 and still get a share of the multiplied pot). Because contributions are revealed each round and punishment is allowed (costly to the punisher, doubly costly to the punished), the group can use punishment to enforce cooperation. Early rounds are likely to contain testing and information-gathering: some players may free-ride to see whether others will punish. Punishment tends to be sparse but decisive — a few units aimed at defectors can shift behavior toward contributing. Over repeated rounds with visible punishments and visible punishment spending by players (though punishments' targets are hidden to the punished only in the sense of the action being anonymous), we expect cooperation to rise after early enforcement and remain fairly high, with occasional late-game defections as players test the boundaries or try to opportunistically cash in near the end. Chat is likely to be brief and aimed at signaling willingness to punish or asking for coordination.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 3 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), 16 rounds, and a punishment stage each round. Each coin a player contributes is multiplied by 2 and split evenly: so each coin contributed returns 2/3 of a coin to the contributor (and 2/3 to each other player). That means contributions are socially beneficial (group gets more if everyone contributes) but individually costly (private marginal return < 1). Punishment lets players spend 1 coin to impose a 2-coin loss on another; punishment is therefore an instrument to discipline low contributors but is itself costly and imperfect.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds typically see exploration: players test others' tendencies. Some contribute moderately (signaling cooperation), while others may free-ride a bit. Because each player receives private information only about totals, there is uncertainty about why someone contributed little (strategy vs. mistake).
- Players who value the group outcome (or who are willing to pay to enforce norms) will punish persistent low contributors, but punishment is usually sparse because it costs the punisher and does not always restore full fairness.
- Endgame effects: contributions often drift down as the end approaches because future retaliation/punishment is less effective. Some players may maintain cooperation throughout; others exploit it.
- Using the provided profiles as priors: SLOTH (name and profile suggest lower motivation to actively enforce norms and some inconsistency) will often contribute less and be a target of a few punishments. DUCK (older, stable) is likelier to contribute steadily and to use punishment sparingly to enforce cooperation. GORILLA (young, mixed) is likely to be variable—sometimes cooperative, sometimes slack—and may both punish and be punished occasionally.
- Given the game's length and that punishment is visible in aggregate but not attributable to specific sources, expect mostly moderate contributions with occasional sharp drops and a few targeted punishments aimed at low contributors. Punishment will be used sparingly (small numbers of units, rarely large).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three anonymous players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone gets 20 coins and decides privately how many to contribute to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so any 1 coin you contribute returns only about 0.63 to yourself — individually it's rational to free-ride, but the group does best when everyone contributes. Punishment is available but costly: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and removes 12 coins from the target. Because punishment is costly, we expect it to be used sparingly and mainly to deter persistent free-riding or to signal norms. Early rounds often show moderate cooperation; a clear defection is likely to trigger limited punishment from others, which usually leads the defector to raise contributions in later rounds. In the final round some players may defect (end-game effect), while others may still spend on punishment to express disapproval even though future leverage is gone. Chats are typically sparse and used to signal intentions or complain about defections.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated four-player public goods game with 10 rounds. Each round players choose an integer contribution (0–20) from an initial 20; the group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. After payoffs are revealed each round, players can spend coins to reward (1 coin spent per reward unit; each unit gives 1 coin to the recipient). Rewards are visible and identified by source and target, so they serve as a limited reputational/incentive device across rounds. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Individual incentive each round: keeping a coin privately gives you 1 immediately; contributing a coin returns 2.1/4 = 0.525 to you via the public pot. So contribution is individually costly (private return <1) but benefits the group (social multiplier >1).
- With 10 rounds and observable rewards/contributions, conditional cooperators or those valuing reciprocity will tend to increase contributions after seeing others contribute and reward them; free riders may exploit early cooperation but can be selectively ignored (no reward) later.
- Rewards are costly and typically used sparingly: players reward perceived cooperators or to encourage undercontributors. Because rewards are visible, a small positive reciprocity loop can sustain or raise cooperation mid-game. In the final round, forward-looking motives drop, so contributions and rewards commonly fall.
- Uncertainties: players don't know others' initial types/preferences. Early rounds will reveal tendencies. Individuals' socioeconomic profiles suggest moderate to cooperative behavior from SLOTH, GORILLA, and DUCK; CHICKEN (younger, unemployed) might be more variable (initially freeriding), but observing consistent payoffs from contributing may shift CHICKEN toward contribution over rounds.

Given these forces, I predict moderate contributions at start, growing if players reward cooperators, with CHICKEN initially lower but increasing by imitation/response; rewards remain sparse (mostly 1 unit), concentrated among mutually cooperative players. Final round will show a drop in contributions and few or no rewards.

game_explanation: This is a 16-round public goods game with 3 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution between 0 and 20 from an endowment of 20. The sum of contributions is multiplied by 2 and split equally among the three players. Because each contributed coin returns on average 2/3 to the contributor (2 split among 3), the one-shot selfish incentive is to contribute 0. However, in a repeated 16-round setting players can be conditionally cooperative (rewarding contributors, punishing free-riders) and may experiment early to establish norms. Observability: at the end of each round players see every player's net payoff for that round (their own and their peers’). From those payoffs they can deduce the total contributions and infer individual contributions, so reputational learning and conditional responses are possible. Typical unfolding: some early cooperation (or experiments) by younger/more enthusiastic players, exploitation by a low-contributing player (SLOTH is a natural prior for low contributions), reactive reductions by those who were exploited, occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation, and a tendency toward lower contributions as the game approaches the final rounds (endgame free-riding).

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits four players against the temptation to free-ride. Each round, a player either keeps the full private endowment (withdraws 20 → contributes 0) or contributes the full 20 to the public pot (contributes 20 → retains 0). The public contributions are multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so cooperating helps the group but can be exploited. Punishment is costly to send (3 coins per unit) and harsh to receive (7 coins lost per unit); rewards are also costly (3 coins per unit) but give a moderate positive boost (4 coins per unit). Because players don't see others' choices before deciding and cannot identify who punished/rewarded them in the summary, dynamics are driven by prior beliefs, visible group outcomes, and sparse costly interventions (punishment/reward).

Expectations across six rounds:
- Early rounds often show moderate cooperation from at least some players; free-riders may appear immediately.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with three players. Each round everyone privately has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution between 0 and 20. Contributions go into a shared pot that's multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. That multiplier (1.9) is less than the number of players (3), so the individual monetary incentive is to contribute 0 each round (you lose 1 coin of private holding for every 1 coin you contribute but the group returns only 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 per coin to you). However, social preferences, reciprocity, and messages can sustain positive contributions early on. Typical dynamics: some players (conditional cooperators) start with moderate contributions and then reduce if they see free-riding; pure free-riders contribute little and benefit early, inducing a decline in cooperation over rounds. Because the game lasts 6 rounds and there's no future after round 6, contributions often drift down over time, with a pronounced drop in the final round. Given the player profiles: GORILLA (postgraduate, full-time) is the most likely conditional cooperator who will try to maintain contributions; DUCK (young postgrad) will likely be cooperative but responsive to exploitation; SLOTH (older, high-school) is the most likely low contributor/free-rider. Expect initial moderate contributions from GORILLA and DUCK, low from SLOTH, then gradual decline as cooperators adjust downward, with a final-round collapse to defection.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player effectively chooses a contribution (0–20) to the shared pot; the full pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so the private benefit from contributing is diluted across the group (individual return is 2.1/4 = 0.525 per unit contributed). The private incentive is therefore to free-ride (withdraw more, contribute less), while group-level welfare is maximized by high mutual contributions. Because players do not observe others' choices before deciding, decisions reflect priors, expectations about reciprocity, and responses to observed past behavior. After redistribution, players can send costly positive rewards to others; rewards are small, visible, and serve as a social incentive to reward cooperators and withhold praise from free-riders.

Predicted dynamics: early rounds will see moderate cooperation as players test each other. Positive rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors (particularly by players who appear reciprocally motivated, like CHICKEN and GORILLA). A persistent low contributor (likely SLOTH, given the profile hint of lower engagement) will get few rewards, which should reduce others' willingness to prop up that player. Occasional dips (one player experimenting with low contributions) will trigger a reduction in rewards toward that player and small retaliatory withholding. Toward the end (rounds 9–10) some slight decline in contributions is likely (endgame effect), but the presence of positive rewards maintains some cooperation. Rewards remain modest (mostly 1–2 units) and targeted rather than broad.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players and a binary choice each round: either withdraw the whole 20 (contribute 0) or leave it in the pot (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.4 to each player (a private return of 0.4) — that makes contribution individually costly (you lose 0.6 per coin you put in) but collectively beneficial. Because contributions are all-or-nothing, the tension is stark: defecting (withdraw 20) gives you the private 20 plus your share of the multiplied pot, so defecting tends to give higher immediate payoffs.

Punishment and reward give players tools to change incentives. Punishment is relatively potent (each unit costs sender 3 and imposes 7 on the target); reward is moderate (each unit costs sender 3 and gives target +4). Both are costly to the sender, so players punish/reward sparingly. Early rounds are uncertain — players test each other. If some players consistently cooperate and punish free-riders, a norm of cooperation can emerge; if punishers bear high costs without changing behavior, cooperation may not be sustainable. In the final round, the “endgame” prediction is that forward-looking incentives to enforce cooperation are weaker, so cooperation often falls. Social preferences and the small group mean interpersonal signals (messages, punish/reward patterns) will shape choices; we’ll use the provided profiles to bias plausible behavior (GORILLA and CHICKEN slightly more likely to enforce norms; SLOTH a bit more opportunistic; DUCK mixed and responsive to feedback).

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with four players. Each round each player's choice is binary: either leave the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw all 20 for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so the group's total contribution benefits everyone (each 1 coin contributed increases each player's payoff by 0.4). Because private withdrawal gives an immediate private gain plus the shared return, there is a temptation to free-ride by withdrawing all 20 while the others contribute.

Players can use costly positive rewards: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the receiver 4 coins. Rewards are a way to reinforce cooperation (reward cooperators) or signal disapproval (by withholding rewards). Rewards are expensive, so they are used sparingly. With a known finite horizon (6 rounds), standard incentives predict cooperation may be stronger early and erode toward the end; however, rewarding and reputation-building (through messages) can sustain cooperation for several rounds. Uncertainty arises from not observing others' choices before deciding and from imperfect identification of who rewarded whom (players see amounts sent and received but not which peer sent to whom).

I predict an initial cooperative norm among three players (DUCK, CHICKEN, and GORILLA), with SLOTH free-riding at first. Cooperators will use small positive rewards to reinforce each other and to withhold rewards from the defector. After a couple rounds of being unrewarded, SLOTH will switch to cooperation briefly; GORILLA will test opportunistic defection mid-game; cooperation will weaken near the final round, ending in widespread defection.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone decides privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Individually, each coin contributed returns only 0.633 to the contributor (1.9/3), so the short-term incentive is to free-ride. However, the group gets more if everyone contributes fully. After contributions are revealed, players can spend resources to punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and removes 12 coins from the target. Punishment is costly to use but can deter free-riding.

Given the player profiles, I expect GORILLA (postgraduate, younger) to be consistently pro-social and willing to use punishment to enforce cooperation. DUCK (older, college-educated) is moderately cooperative and will punish at times but less aggressively. SLOTH (self-described by name and profile) is likelier to under-contribute early and respond only after being punished. Punishment will be used sparingly, primarily to discipline clear under-contributors; once cooperation stabilizes, punishment will drop. In the final round, some players may defect because there's no future enforcement value, so last-round contributions may fall and punishment may be limited.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private incentives against collective benefit. Each coin left in the shared pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally; a single coin contributed yields 2.1/4 = 0.525 back to the contributor, so contributing is individually costly even though it's socially beneficial. Because players observe only past outcomes (not intentions) and the game lasts 10 rounds, I expect moderate cooperation early (conditional cooperation based on others' apparent generosity), occasional targeted punishment of clear free-riders (to enforce cooperation), and some rewarding of high contributors. Punishment is costly to the punisher (1 coin) but severe to the target (-3 coins), so players will use it sparingly — usually single-unit punishments directed at repeat low contributors. Rewards are cheaper and less powerful (1 coin cost → +1 to target) and will be used occasionally to acknowledge high contributors. As the finite horizon approaches, contributions typically drift downward (endgame effect), and punishment becomes rarer because retaliation no longer has future consequences. Heterogeneous priors from player profiles (education, age, likely norms) suggest moderate initial trust (e.g., DUCK and CHICKEN somewhat more inclined to contribute; GORILLA slightly more variable), which will shape gradual adjustments across rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with four players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the group pot or withdraws them (so contribution = 0 or 20). The group total is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so every player's share from the pot is proportional to total contributors but independent of their own contribution. In a single round, free-riding (withdrawing) strictly dominates cooperating, because defectors keep 20 and receive the same pot share as cooperators. However, the subsequent reward stage allows players to spend 3 coins to give 4 coins to another player; this can create incentives to sustain cooperation if cooperators are rewarded and free-riders are not.

Key uncertainties and dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often see some cooperation from conditional cooperators who test reciprocity. Free-riders get higher immediate payoffs and may keep exploiting cooperators unless rewards offset that advantage.
- Because rewards are costly to send and anonymous in recipients' summaries, rewarding will be sparse and targeted — usually small, reciprocal gestures between cooperators or occasional attempts to convert defectors.
- As the end approaches (final rounds), standard "endgame" logic predicts unraveling: cooperation tends to fall because future retaliation or reciprocity is less effective. Some players may still send a last round reward as a moral statement or to reward past cooperators.
- Given the provided profiles, expect DUCK and CHICKEN to lean more toward conditional cooperation at first, while SLOTH and GORILLA are more likely to free-ride initially. Rewards will be used sparingly by cooperators to reinforce reciprocity; defectors will rarely spend to reward others unless trying to nudge cooperation late in the game.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 29-round public goods game with 4 players. Each player decides how much of 20 coins to contribute (0–20); remaining coins are kept privately. Contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns 0.45 to each player overall — beneficial for the group, but costly to the individual (private return 0.45 < 1 withdrawn). That creates a strong temptation to free-ride (withdraw more). The reward stage allows positive transfers: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Rewards are expensive for senders and only partially efficient socially (net social change -1 per unit) but can sustain cooperation through targeted reciprocity: rewarding cooperators can encourage them to keep contributing, and being rewarded signals that one’s cooperation was noticed. Players don’t see others’ contributions before deciding and only learn after the round who rewarded them personally (not others’ full summaries), so information is limited and reciprocity tends to be local and cautious.

Given these incentives, I predict:
- Early rounds: tentative, moderate cooperation as players probe each other; a few small, targeted rewards to signal and reinforce cooperation (often between Gorilla and Chicken who appear more socially/invested).
- Middle rounds: patterns emerge. One or two players (Chicken and Gorilla) sustain higher contributions; others (Sloth, Duck) drift toward lower contributions or fluctuate. Rewards remain sparse and targeted, typically 1 unit at a time.
- Late rounds: contributions gradually decline as endgame effects kick in; rewards taper off further. In the final round(s) we expect widespread withdrawal (near zero contributions) and minimal rewarding.

Now the round-by-round transcript.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round is binary: a player either withdraws all 20 coins (contributes 0) or leaves them in the shared pot (contributes 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so a single contributor increases the group return but personally gets back less than they gave (contributing is costly unless others also contribute). Punishment is available: sending 1 punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the target. Players see only round summaries (their own net, how much they spent on punishing, and how much was deducted from them) but not who punished them.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: cooperation is fragile because unilateral contribution is costly. Some players (those with cooperative priors) will try contributing; others will free-ride. Contributors may use punishment to deter freeloaders.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is used selectively and not too costly, it can sustain higher cooperation: many players will switch to contributing to avoid being targeted.
- Late rounds (endgame): toward the last few rounds, incentives to punish decline and backward-induction pressure can lead to unraveling — more players withdraw for private gain in the final rounds.
- Messaging will be sparse and strategic (requests to cooperate, warnings after defection). Punishment will be used sparingly: enough to signal and enforce norms, not so much as to destroy the punisher's payoff.

I will simulate a plausible path consistent with those tendencies: two initial cooperators who punish free-riders; one free-rider who later switches after repeated punishment; a cautious free-rider who transitions to cooperation midgame; stable cooperation in the middle; a few unilateral defections mid-to-late game; and unraveling in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 23 rounds. Each round every player faces the same binary choice: withdraw the full 20 coins privately (contribute 0 to the public pot) or withdraw 0 (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally. So a contributor sacrifices the private 20 but increases everyone's payoff; a defector keeps 20 and still shares the multiplied pot. The social optimum is all contribute (each gets 50), but the short-term incentive is to defect when others contribute (you get 20 plus the shared return). Over repeated rounds with communication, common patterns are: initial cooperation (social norms / promises), occasional defections driven by temptation or impatience, punishments (reciprocal defection) or appeals to restore cooperation, and an endgame increase in defections as the horizon approaches.

Uncertainties here include: which players are more inclined to punish versus tolerate free-riding; whether promises or shaming in chat can sustain cooperation; and how strongly the finite horizon (23 rounds) will cause unraveling. Given the profiles and handles: GORILLA (older, steady) and CHICKEN (young, cooperative) are likely to lean toward cooperation and try to maintain it; DUCK may oscillate between cooperation and opportunism; SLOTH (by name and profile) is more likely to free-ride occasionally. Expect early cooperation, a few defection-triggered punishments mid-game, attempts to restore cooperation, and more widespread defection near the end.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 3 players. Each round everyone privately chooses an integer contribution between 0 and 20 from their 20-coin endowment. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the three players. That creates the classic tension: contributing helps the group (the social return 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 per coin to each player), but each coin you contribute gives you only 0.633 back individually while costing you 1 coin, so the private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). With messaging and repeated interaction, cooperation can start high and then collapse if some players defect. Because players know the game lasts exactly 6 rounds, there is an “end-game” pressure: cooperation often unravels toward the final rounds. Given the profiles: DUCK (not in paid work) may be somewhat communal and likely to propose cooperation early; GORILLA (strategic, postgraduate) may test others or exploit cooperation; SLOTH (younger employed) may initially cooperate but will retaliate if exploited. Expect an initial cooperative round (moderate contributions), a defection that provokes punishment, and then a collapse with a few attempts to rebuild that fail as the end approaches. Chat will be sparse and mainly used to propose cooperation or signal punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits individual immediate incentives against group benefit. Each player starts each round with 20 coins in a shared pot; each coin they withdraw becomes private and each coin left in the pot is a contribution. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and divided equally among the 4 players, so each contributed coin returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins to the contributor personally — less than the 1 coin they could have kept. That makes full withdrawal individually optimal, but full contribution maximizes group payoffs. Rewards are the only direct interpersonal action: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins (a costly, pro-social signal that benefits the recipient but reduces total group payoff by 1 coin per unit). Players do not see others' contributions directly; they only learn if someone rewarded them and their own round net payoffs and reward activity. That limited visibility makes targeting rewards difficult; rewards mainly serve as selective supportive signals when senders suspect someone contributed a lot or to encourage known cooperators.

Given the profiles and typical experimental dynamics:
- DUCK and CHICKEN are more likely to be conditional cooperators who start with relatively high contributions and use sparse positive rewards to maintain cooperation between them.
- SLOTH is moderate but somewhat less engaged, contributing less than the most cooperative players but not fully free-riding.
- GORILLA is more inclined to withdraw (free-ride) and responds little to rewards since negative incentives (punishment) are not available here.

Expectations across 29 rounds:
- Early rounds show moderate cooperation from DUCK and CHICKEN, SLOTH middling, and GORILLA low.
- Because players cannot observe contributions, reciprocity is mainly between those who exchange visible rewards; DUCK and CHICKEN will occasionally exchange 1 reward unit to signal approval.
- Rewards are used sparingly (1 unit most times), since they are costly. SLOTH may occasionally give single-unit rewards to DUCK. GORILLA rarely sends rewards.
- Gradual erosion of contributions occurs as the game proceeds and the end approaches, with a sharper drop in the final rounds (endgame effect).
- Overall, cooperation persists at a moderate level early on but declines toward the end as incentives to free-ride dominate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each player starts each round with a potential 20-coin contribution; withdrawing coins reduces the shared pot and increases private payoff. The group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so there is an individual incentive to withdraw (free-ride) while the group benefits if people contribute. The reward stage lets players give positive transfers to others (cost 3 to giver, +2 to receiver), so rewards can reinforce cooperation but are costly.

Given the player profiles and typical lab-game dynamics, I expect:
- One player (SLOTH) will tend to withdraw more (low contributions) because of low labor-market engagement and lower inclination toward costly cooperation.
- GORILLA, with higher education and full-time employment, will behave as a consistent cooperator early on (high contributions).
- DUCK (part-time) will be a conditional cooperator: moderately high early, responding to others' behavior.
- CHICKEN will start cooperative but respond to decline in others.
- Rewards will be used sparingly and mainly to acknowledge high contributors (mostly directed to GORILLA). Over the sequence, cooperation will gradually decay as endgame effects and short-run incentives encourage withdrawal; occasional rewards try to sustain cooperation but are rare because they're costly.

I will simulate plausible, conservative contributions and sparse reward actions round-by-round, with contributions listed in the exact avatar order (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN).

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with three players. Each round everyone privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split evenly. Individually, keeping money is always tempting (each coin you keep gives 1 to you; each coin you contribute returns only 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 to you), so free-riding is the dominant short-term incentive even though the group is better off if everyone contributes. Because contributions are revealed each round, social norms and retaliation can emerge: cooperative players may punish low contributors to enforce higher contributions in later rounds. Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) but very damaging to the punished (12 coins deducted per unit), so punishment is used sparingly in real experiments — enough to signal disapproval or deter repeated free-riding, but not so often that punishers ruin their own payoffs. Because the game lasts only 6 rounds, expect some cooperation early on, some targeted punishment when players defect noticeably, attempts by punished players to increase contributions afterward, and some unraveling (lower contributions) in the final round when future retaliation no longer matters. Chats, if present, will be sparse and aimed at asking others to contribute more or expressing frustration after punishment.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This public goods game is a standard social dilemma with a small group (4 players), a binary choice each round (contribute all 20 coins to the public pot or keep all 20 privately), and an opportunity to use costly rewards to support others. The public return is 1.6 times total contributions split equally; a single player’s private best-response is to keep the 20 (free-ride), because each coin contributed returns only 0.4 to the contributor (20 -> 8 per player share), so individual incentives favor defection while group payoff favors cooperation.

Given these payoffs and the 6-round finite horizon, I expect some mix of cooperation and defection. Early rounds often show testing: some players will try cooperating to see if others reciprocate, while some will defect immediately to exploit cooperators. Costly rewarding (3 coins cost to give 4 coins to a target per unit) can sustain cooperation when cooperators reward each other, but people reward sparingly because rewards themselves reduce the sender’s payoff. Messages tend to be sparse and strategic (requests to cooperate, brief gratitude, mild reproach). Toward the final round cooperation is likely to decline (end-game), though some players may still use rewards to “thank” past cooperators.

I’ll simulate plausible behavior based on the player profiles: SLOTH and DUCK are relatively young and likely to try cooperation and reciprocate with small rewards; GORILLA tends to defect early (taking advantage), and CHICKEN shows some variability (initial defection, then a brief experiment with cooperation, then reversion).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round simultaneous public goods game with cheap talk. Each round every player gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute; total contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. The tension: contributing benefits the group but costs the individual directly; free-riding (contributing less) can earn a higher individual payoff if others contribute. Because the horizon is finite and known (6 rounds), we expect some decay in cooperation over time: players who observe others free-riding will reduce their contributions (retaliation), while two or more cooperative players may try to sustain higher contributions. Cheap talk can help coordinate, but it is non-binding and some players may exploit it. Given the profiles: SLOTH's name and younger status suggests a tendency toward lower contributions (or opportunistic free-riding); GORILLA (young, college-educated) and DUCK (older, postgrad) are more likely to attempt cooperation but will respond to repeated free-riding by lowering contributions. Expect: initial attempt to coordinate, early partial cooperation, some punishment after free-riding, one short-lived recovery if two players coordinate, and a fall toward lower contributions by the final round (endgame effect).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with three players, six rounds, and full information about the game's length. Each round every player gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the three players. Because 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 < 1, every coin you contribute loses you money privately (you give up 1 and get back ~0.633), but it benefits the group. The one-shot Nash equilibrium is to contribute 0, yet many experiments show conditional cooperation: players may contribute positive amounts early on if they expect others to do the same, especially when communication is allowed. Communication can foster coordination (short-run cooperation) or be used cheaply to signal intentions; however, some players will exploit cooperation by free-riding, and others will punish by reducing future contributions. Because the game has a known finite horizon (6 rounds), we also expect contributions to fall toward the end (end-game effect), with the final round often showing strong defection.

Using the provided profiles as priors: DUCK (young, college-educated) is likely to be a conditional cooperator and may push for cooperation. SLOTH (not in paid work) might favor social preferences and stick with modest cooperation if peers reciprocate. GORILLA (full-time employed) may be pragmatic and somewhat more willing to reduce contributions if others do. Expect moderate cooperation early (with brief coordination via chat), some opportunistic decreases when someone free-rides, attempts to rebuild cooperation midgame, and a collapse toward low contributions in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round every player faces a simple binary choice: withdraw all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each contributing player helps everyone: if k players contribute 20, each participant gets 8k from the pot. With 4 players this creates a tension: defecting gives you an immediate private gain (you keep 20), but if enough others cooperate the shared return can still make cooperating worthwhile. After redistribution, players may pay to punish (cost 3 to sender, -7 to target per unit) or reward (cost 3 to sender, +4 to target per unit). Punishment and reward are costly to the sender and anonymous to recipients. The game ends after round 6, so backward induction and the shadow of future retaliation/reward are important: early rounds often see cooperation supported by threats of punishment or promises of reward; cooperation can collapse if punishment is too weak or if players start defecting in the final round when future consequences vanish.

Given the player profiles and typical online PGG behavior, I predict:
- Initial rounds will show some cooperation but one player (SLOTH) is somewhat more likely to defect early on. Other players (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) tend to cooperate and may use sparse punishment/reward to enforce cooperation.
- Punishments are applied sparingly but meaningfully: a few 1-unit punishments are likely when someone defects repeatedly. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperation.
- Because the game is short, players adjust quickly: a strong enough combined punishment (total -7 per unit) will tend to bring a repeat defector back into cooperation by round 3. In the final round, some players will defect knowing there is no future, and punishment there will be rarer and costlier to the punishers.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits individual incentives (take money privately) against group incentives (leave money in the pot to be multiplied by 1.8 and split among 4). Each coin contributed returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins to each player, so each contributor receives 0.45 from their own coin but sacrifices 1 coin privately — making free-riding individually attractive. Because contributions are simultaneous and private before choice, players must form beliefs about others' willingness to cooperate. Typical dynamics: initial testing/conditional cooperation (moderate contributions), followed by adaptation — sustained cooperation if players reward cooperators, decline if free-riding appears. The reward stage lets players give costly praise: paying 3 coins to give 2 coins to another. Rewards are thus used sparingly to encourage good contributors or to reciprocate past generosity; they are too costly for mass use. Knowing the game lasts 29 rounds creates endgame tension: cooperation likely erodes toward the final rounds. Uncertainties include heterogeneity in social preferences (some players may be consistently cooperative and reward, others forecastably selfish), and stochastic reciprocity (players see who rewarded them and may reciprocate). Expect moderate-high cooperation early, maintenance via occasional small rewards between reliable cooperators (especially those who are more trusting or civic-minded), and a gradual decline toward the last few rounds as endgame incentives dominate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This 3-player public goods game gives each player 20 coins per round and asks for an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is doubled and split equally among active players, so each 1-coin contribution costs the contributor 1 coin but raises everyone’s return by 2/3 coin. Rational self-interest leads to contributing 0, but many real players start with positive contributions (conditional cooperation), then reduce contributions when they see others free-ride. Because there is no communication, players only learn peers' past choices via the round summaries; this increases uncertainty early on and encourages tentative cooperation. Player priors: SLOTH (young adult) may test the group and shift toward self-interest earlier; GORILLA (older, experienced) is more likely to sustain cooperation longer; DUCK (similar background to Gorilla) is plausibly a conditional cooperator who follows observed norms. Over 16 rounds I predict an initial phase of moderate cooperation, followed by gradual decline as free-riding emerges, and eventual near-zero contributions toward the final rounds (end-game effect), with occasional small gestures of cooperation from the more trusting player.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round players start with 20 coins and choose how many to withdraw privately; the remainder is contributed to a common pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly across the four players. Because each contributed coin returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins to each contributor personally, contributing is socially beneficial (total group return 1.8) but privately costly (personal return 0.45 < 1). The one-shot dominant strategy is to withdraw everything (contribute 0), but with repetition players can sustain cooperation through reciprocity and indirect incentives.

The reward stage lets players send costly rewards: each reward unit costs 3 coins to the giver and gives 2 coins to the recipient. Rewards are an imperfect tool to sustain cooperation — they directly benefit recipients but are costly for the sender, so they tend to be used sparingly and targeted at those who appear to support the public good. Because the game ends after a known finite horizon (29 rounds), we expect a typical pattern: relatively higher contributions early on (trust and hope for reciprocity), occasional directed rewards to reinforce contributors, gradual erosion of contributions as free-riding becomes tempting, and a marked decline toward the final rounds (endgame effect). The four players differ in likely tendencies: CHICKEN (college-educated) is the most likely to be consistently pro-social and to use rewards; DUCK is moderately cooperative; GORILLA (younger, not in paid work) is variable and may reduce contributions as others free-ride; SLOTH (name suggests low effort) is the likeliest to contribute little from the start. Because rewards are visible to recipients, they provide social signals, but they will be used sparingly in a realistic prediction.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players over six rounds. Each round each player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. The dominant individual incentive in a single round is to contribute 0 (you keep all your 20 and still get a share of others' contributions), while the group optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20. Over repeated rounds, standard patterns often appear: early-round testing and signaling (a few players try to encourage cooperation), conditional cooperation (players who see others contributing stay cooperative), occasional punishment or retaliation (some players reduce contributions when others free-ride), and an end-game drop in contributions in the final round when reciprocity incentives vanish.

Uncertainties here include whether any player will consistently lead by contributing a lot (and absorb short-term losses to enforce cooperation), whether any player will exploit others by contributing little while still receiving the benefit of the multiplied pot, and how credible any chat commitments will be. Given the player profiles (GORILLA appears experienced and possibly cooperative/strategic; DUCK moderate and conditional; SLOTH younger and named suggestively "SLOTH"), I predict an initial attempt at moderate-to-high cooperation led by GORILLA, some exploitation by SLOTH in early rounds, some adjustments toward mid-game stability, and a notable drop in the final round.

The total contributed is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so a lone contributor loses relative to free riders, while full cooperation benefits everyone. Players cannot see who punished them, and the horizon is finite (23 rounds), so cooperation is more likely in early/mid rounds when punishment deters free-riding; toward the very end some players may defect more since future retaliation is limited. Chats will be sparse; punishments will be used selectively to signal enforcement (especially by players who appear willing to maintain cooperation). Expect initial cooperation with occasional defections that are lightly punished; sustained cooperation for many middle rounds; and some unraveling in the last few rounds as the end approaches.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-round public goods game with four players. Each round everyone starts with 20 coins in a shared pot and chooses how many coins to withdraw privately; the leftover is contributed to the public pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. After returns are distributed, players can spend coins to punish (cost 1 to sender, −3 to target) or reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target). Players observe contributions and the identity of any punishers/rewarders after each round, and they know the game ends after 10 rounds.

Likely dynamics: initial rounds typically show a mix of cooperation and some free-riding. Conditional cooperators will tend to match perceived group norms; low contributors are occasionally punished (sparingly) and high contributors sometimes rewarded (sparingly). Punishment can prompt short-term increases in contribution by the punished player, while rewards reinforce good behavior. Because punishment and rewards are costly, they will appear infrequently and in small units. Near the final rounds some players may test or relax cooperation, but given repeated interaction and visible IDs for sanctions, most players will maintain moderate-to-high contributions after early calibration.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round repeated public goods game with four players. Each round every player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot or withdraws them all (so contribution choices are binary: 0 or 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so the private incentive is often to withdraw (free-ride) because a defector keeps 20 plus gets a share of the pot, while a cooperator sacrifices 20 but can only recoup part of it through the equal split. The optional reward stage lets players spend 3 coins to give a 4-coin bonus to someone else; this is costly to rewarders but can be used sparingly to reward cooperators and sustain cooperation.

Given the short horizon (6 rounds) and the players' profiles, I expect some initial attempts at cooperation (especially from CHICKEN, who is most pro-social in the priors), mixed responses by others, and occasional reciprocal rewarding among cooperators. However, the strong individual incentive to free-ride will likely produce several rounds with partial or full defection; rewards will be used sparingly to encourage cooperation but won't fully eliminate strategic defection, and cooperation may briefly spike then decline toward the end (endgame effect). Chats will be short, with CHICKEN sometimes proposing cooperation or thanking others, and others replying minimally.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This 16-round public goods game places 3 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) each with 20 coins per round. Each chooses how much to contribute (0–20). The group pot is doubled and split equally, so everyone's payoff benefits from total contributions but each faces an incentive to free-ride (keep more privately while still enjoying the shared pot). After contributions are revealed, players can punish others: each punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and reduces the target's payoff by 2 coins. Punishment is anonymous in the players' summaries (they don't see who punished them), but they see how much each peer spent punishing and how much each lost to punishment.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some cooperation as players test each other. GORILLA (postgraduate, cooperative tendency) is likely to contribute relatively high amounts; DUCK (moderate) tends to contribute moderately; SLOTH (described as "sloth") is inclined toward lower contributions and may try to exploit cooperators.
- Punishment will be used sparingly, mainly by cooperative players to discipline persistent low contributors. Because punishment is costly, expect mostly small (1–2 unit) punishments when free-riding is judged repeated or blatant.
- After being punished, free-riders often raise contributions somewhat, producing oscillations: occasional dips followed by targeted punitive responses. Toward the end, contributions may drift down as the "endgame" effect reduces incentives to invest in future rounds; punishment also tends to be used less often but may still appear for retaliation or signaling.
- Uncertainties: players cannot identify punishers, so some anti-free-rider signaling is diffuse; individual responses depend on how each player weighs the cost of punishment vs. the benefit in future rounds.

Keeping these tendencies in mind, the transcript below simulates plausible contributions, sparse punishments targeted mainly at SLOTH when they under-contribute, and evolving cooperation across 16 rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 rounds and 4 players. Each round players choose how much of their 20 coins to leave in the public pot (contribution) by withdrawing coins for private use. The group pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (free-riding) while group payoffs favor contributing. Because players can punish (costly to sender, larger harm to target) and reward (costly to sender, small benefit to target) and identities are visible, we expect some enforcement of cooperation: higher contributors may be rewarded occasionally and low contributors punished sparingly. Punishment and reward are costly, so actors use them rarely and selectively — typically when others deviate noticeably from the group's norm. Over initial rounds there's uncertainty, so contributions will be heterogeneous; early punishments of clear low contributors should push some toward higher contributions. Toward the final rounds, endgame effects tend to reduce cooperation, so contributions likely decline somewhat in rounds 8–10. Given the player profiles, GORILLA and DUCK are more likely to sustain higher contributions and engage in occasional enforcement; SLOTH is more likely to under-contribute early but respond to sanctions; CHICKEN is variable and may oscillate between moderate cooperation and occasional defection.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round every player starts with 20 coins and decides how many to withdraw (their private take); the remainder is contributed to a shared pot which is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After payoffs are computed, players can use costly punishment (source pays 3 per unit, target loses 4 per unit) or costly reward (source pays 3 per unit, target gains 2 per unit). Players do not see others' contributions before deciding, and they only learn their own round payoff plus who punished/rewarded them.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high contributions from socially oriented players (GORILLA, DUCK) and lower contributions by more self-interested or "slothful" players (SLOTH). CHICKEN may start moderate and drift.
- Because contributions are not visible, direct contingent punishment/reward is noisy; players will use sanctions sparingly to avoid wasting coins, typically targeted when someone’s behavior is persistently low (as inferred from own payoffs across rounds) or to reward perceived cooperators.
- Punishment will be rare and usually small (1 unit) because it’s costly; rewards will also be used sparingly to encourage cooperation.
- Over time, without strong, visible coordination, contributions tend to drift downward (endgame effect). Occasional punishments or rewards may temporarily boost contribution from targeted players.
- Uncertainties: because players cannot observe others' contributions, sanctions may be mistargeted or delayed, and players must balance experimenting with strategies versus conserving coins for punishment/reward opportunities.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players, 23 rounds, and a simple binary choice each round: either withdraw all 20 coins from the shared pot (contribute 0) or leave them in (contribute 20). The group return is 2.5× the total contributions, split equally among the four players. Individual incentive: when others contribute, you gain more by defecting (taking your 20) while still getting a share of the multiplied pot. Collective incentive: everyone contributes, and each gets the largest possible group payoff (50). Because the game is finite and players see round payoffs of all peers, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: some players try cooperating (signal intent in chat), others free-ride and get higher payoffs, which discourages cooperators.
- Middle rounds: cooperation often collapses to mutual defection; occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation (via chat promises) can produce short-lived cooperation.
- Endgame: because there's no future after round 23, cooperation is fragile near the last few rounds; there may be a last attempt at coordination a few rounds before the end, but breakdown is common by round 23.

Given the player profiles (SLOTH: educated, possibly conditional cooperator; GORILLA: more self-interested/short-run; DUCK: young and initially cooperative but easily exploited; CHICKEN: pragmatic, willing to coordinate), I predict an early partial cooperation (some contribute, some free-ride), quick collapse into widespread defection, a midgame coordinated burst of full cooperation for a few rounds after explicit messaging, then reversion to defection, and a late attempt at cooperation that is partially successful. Chat will be sparse, used to signal intentions, protest exploitation, or try to coordinate brief cooperation bursts.

game_explanation:
This 10-round public goods game pits four identifiable players against the temptation to withdraw coins privately. Each round they choose how many of the 20 coins to leave as a contribution; the group pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, then players may reward others (cost 1 per unit sent, gives 1 to the recipient). Because rewards are costly and visible, they are best used sparingly to encourage cooperators or reciprocate generosity. Early rounds typically reveal players' basic dispositions (conditional cooperators will match others; free-riders will contribute little). Over rounds we expect some conditional cooperation: those who see high contributions and occasional rewards will tend to increase or sustain contributions, while those who see low returns or little reciprocity may reduce contributions. Uncertainties include individual risk preferences, how much each values short-term private withdrawal vs. longer-term shared returns, and whether players use rewards to signal reciprocity. Given the profiles, I expect GORILLA and DUCK to lean toward higher contributions (steady cooperators), SLOTH to be more variable and responsive to others, and CHICKEN to be cautious and somewhat lower in contribution. Rewards will be occasional and targeted at the more consistent contributors (not used as punishment).

After a low point, some players respond by increasing contributions to restore mutual gains (conditional cooperation).
