Mutual small rewards between the steady cooperators are possible as reciprocal reinforcement.

Approaching the final rounds, some players start to think about end-game effects (more private withdrawal), but reputation and recent reciprocity may sustain cooperation for a bit. GORILLA and DUCK stay somewhat high; SLOTH nudges up; CHICKEN trims back. Rewards are used selectively; one small acknowledgement from a cooperator is plausible.

As the final round approaches, players often begin to favor private payoffs more. GORILLA may still contribute relatively more out of norm adherence; DUCK may modestly reduce; SLOTH contributes moderately; CHICKEN contributes less. A single small reward toward the steady contributor is plausible as a final encouragement or reciprocal nod.

Final-round logic often reduces cooperation because future reciprocity is impossible. Expect lower contributions from those who were already cautious (CHICKEN, SLOTH), while GORILLA and DUCK may still give somewhat more out of consistency or social preference. Rewards in the final round are still possible but typically minimal — a single token reward to acknowledge a steady cooperator is realistic.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with three players for 16 rounds. Each round players privately choose how much of 20 coins to contribute; the pot is doubled and split equally, so there is a private incentive to free-ride while a group incentive favors contributions. Because punishment is available at cost to the punisher, we expect conditional cooperators (who contribute more initially) to punish clear, repeated free-riding to pressure low contributors upward. Given the player priors, GORILLA (full-time employed, steady) is likely to be a consistent contributor and occasional punisher of clear shirkers; DUCK (younger, conditional cooperator) will start cooperatively and punish occasionally. SLOTH (name suggests lower effort) is likely to contribute less on average, sometimes responding to punishment by increasing contributions but sometimes lapsing. Punishment will be used sparingly — just enough to signal norms and correct repeated low contributions — and will not be highly frequent because it is costly to send.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 23 rounds. Each round every player effectively chooses to either keep the entire 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so an individual who contributes 20 creates a group benefit but sacrifices the sure private 20. Punishment allows cooperators to deter free-riding: spending 1 coin to reduce a target by 2. However, punishment is costly to the punisher and anonymous to targets (they see the deduction but not who punished them). Messages are available but sparse in most experiments.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test each other. Some will contribute out of pro-social preference or reciprocity (Gorilla, Chicken), others may try free-riding early (Sloth, conditional Duck). Expect a few targeted punishments early from committed cooperators to establish norms.
- Middle rounds: if punishment deters free-riding, stable cooperation (most contribute 20) will emerge; punishments become rare and used only if someone deviates.
- Late rounds (approaching 23): the incentive to sustain cooperation falls (fewer future rounds to enforce norms), so we often see more defection in the final rounds. Punishment declines because it is costly and less effective in the final rounds.
- Uncertainty: because punishment is anonymous, players must infer who punished them. Messages can be used to justify moves or signal intentions, but are typically sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), six rounds, and costly, anonymous punishment available after contributions each round. Each round each player has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so contributing helps the group but is individually costly (the marginal per-capita return is 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 < 1). Punishment is expensive for the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) but punishes the target heavily (-12 coins per unit), and punishers are anonymous in the reports.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: without established norms some players may test lower contributions; others may punish small defections to signal that free-riding is costly. Punishment is usually used sparingly because it costs the punisher.
- Middle rounds: if punishments successfully deter free-riding, contributions tend to rise and stabilize at moderate levels (not full cooperation because of the <1 return). If punishments are perceived as unfair, retaliation and lower contributions may follow.
- Endgame (final rounds): the incentive to free-ride increases because punishment can't be retaliated against in future rounds for the last round; we often see some drop in contributions, though prior social enforcement may limit the drop.
- Communication: messages are typically few and strategic (requests, apologies, short threats or promises). Players may confess to punishments or explain their motives.

Given the player profiles, expect GORILLA and DUCK (both employed, mid-age) to favor stability and moderate cooperation; SLOTH might be slower to cooperate initially but will respond to punishment (the name suggests a lower initial contribution, but the profile can also adapt). Punishment will be used occasionally to steer behavior, not every round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round each player either withdraws all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leaves all 20 in the pot (contribute 20). The shared pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so each player's share from the public fund is 0.625 × (total contributions). Keeping all 20 privately gives an immediate personal advantage because the private return (20) exceeds the individual marginal benefit of contributing (0.625 × 20 = 12.5). That makes withdrawing (free-riding) the dominant strategy in a one-shot setting. Over repeated rounds, cooperation can still appear by reciprocity, promises, or social preferences, but with a known finite horizon (23 rounds) we expect cooperation to be fragile and to decline as players anticipate endgame defection.

Given the player profiles: SLOTH (college-educated, full-time) is somewhat likely to try conditional cooperation early on; CHICKEN (younger, part-time) is relatively likely to attempt cooperation and to send encouraging messages; GORILLA and DUCK (both completed high school, full-time or non-specified) are more likely to prioritize private payoffs and free-ride. Messaging will be sparse but purposeful: a few early commitments to contribute, a few complaints when cooperation is exploited, occasional last-ditch pleas to restart cooperation. Overall prediction: some initial cooperation attempts (one or two players), quick exploitation by free-riders, then a fall to widespread defection for most of the game, with a couple of isolated cooperative attempts midgame and late in the game that fail to re-establish group cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player decides how much of their 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) versus withdraw for private use; the shared total is multiplied by 2.1 and split evenly. After payoffs from the public pot are distributed, players may spend coins to reward others (1 coin spent gives the target +1). Players see round summaries (including who rewarded them), so reputation and conditional cooperation can matter across rounds.

Key dynamics I expect:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' cooperativeness. Some players (with higher education or pro-social priors) are likelier to contribute more and to use rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- Conditional cooperation: players who see high contributions and receive rewards are likely to sustain or increase contributions; free-riders tend to emerge and provoke reduced support over time.
- Rewards are costly and typically used sparingly and selectively to reinforce high contributors rather than to punish (there is no negative transfer).
- Over time, if free-riding persists and reward spending is limited, contributions usually drift downward, with an additional end-game decline in the final rounds because players anticipate no future reciprocation.

I will simulate a plausible unfolding where GORILLA is consistently cooperative and uses rewards early to sustain reciprocity with SLOTH; DUCK and CHICKEN trend toward freeriding, producing a gradual decline in group contributions and fewer rewards later in the game.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods setting with 29 rounds and persistent identities. Each round each player effectively decides how much of 20 coins to leave in the public pot (contribution) versus keep privately. The public pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally among the four players, so each coin contributed returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins to each individual — less than the private value of 1. That creates a strong individual incentive to withdraw (free-ride). Repeated interaction, however, allows conditional strategies: players may contribute to sustain mutual benefit, or punish/reward others to encourage cooperation.

Typical dynamics I expect here:
- Early rounds: cautious cooperation (moderate contributions) as players test one another.
- Mid game: occasional defections by one or two players. Conditional cooperators (players who care about group outcomes or about reputation) may respond with sparse punishments or selective rewards. Punishment is costly: each punishment unit costs the punisher 3 coins and removes 4 coins from the target, so players punish sparingly and typically only when they perceive repeated low contributions.
- Rewards are also possible (each reward unit costs 3 and gives +2 to receiver) and tend to be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors.
- Because players only see their own round payoff and who punished/rewarded them (but do not see others' contributions), learning is imperfect: players infer patterns slowly from targeted feedback. That typically leads to mixed cooperation levels and occasional targeted sanctions rather than wide-scale retaliation.
- End game: with a known finite horizon, contributions tend to decline toward the last rounds as incentives to punish/reward and to sustain cooperation weaken.

I will simulate a plausible unfolding of this game: moderate cooperation at start, a few defections by the less cooperative players, intermittent targeted punishment primarily by the most cooperative player(s), and occasional small rewards for consistent contributors. Punishment and reward actions will be sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), 16 rounds. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution between 0 and 20 from a personal endowment of 20. All contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2, and split equally among the three players. Individually, each coin contributed returns 2/3 coin to its contributor (because 2/3 = 2/3 of the multiplied pot per contributor) which is less than 1, so the strictly self-interested short-run incentive is to contribute 0. However, the group-level payoff is maximized if everyone contributes more (the socially efficient outcome is all contribute 20).

Given the finite 16-round horizon and no communication, typical dynamics are: some initial cooperation (players test one another), followed by gradual decline as players respond to perceived free-riding (conditional cooperation), and a sharper end-game drop as future reciprocity becomes irrelevant. Uncertainty about peers’ types and motives (how quickly they reduce contributions) makes the early rounds exploratory. Player profiles provide priors: SLOTH (younger, college-educated, possibly idealistic/conditional cooperator) may start relatively high but can become disappointed and reduce contributions; GORILLA (older, pragmatic) may contribute moderately but lean toward lower contributions when others pull back; DUCK (college-educated, strategic) may start cooperative but respond to trends and eventually free-ride near the end. Expect overall group contributions to decline over the 16 rounds, with small fluctuations when players briefly reciprocate or test cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players and costly punishment. Each round players decide whether to leave the full 20 coins in the public pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them all for private use (contribute 0). The public pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so cooperation benefits the group but an individual can earn more in the short run by free-riding. Because punishment is possible (cost 1 per unit to sender and -2 per unit to target), cooperative contributions can be enforced, but punishment is costly and used sparingly in real experiments. Early rounds will typically show cooperation and occasional “tests” (single defections) that are met with targeted punishment by at least one pro-social actor. Punishment usage tends to stabilize cooperation in the middle of the game but is costly; as the end approaches, rational incentives to punish decline and defection increases (end-game effect). Players are anonymous with respect to punishers, so retaliation is imprecise and punishment frequency will be moderate rather than heavy. Chat messages are likely rare and strategic (warnings, requests, brief reproaches).

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game creates a tension between private incentive (withdrawing the full 20 coins each round) and group benefit (contributing all 20 so the shared pot grows). With four players and a multiplication factor of 1.6, every 20 contributed yields 8 extra coins to be split (20 * 1.6 / 4 = 8 per player when one player contributes 20 and others maybe too). Because the game has a known finite horizon (6 rounds), we expect some cooperation early as players test each other and try to build reciprocity through costly rewards; as the end approaches, some players may defect more often. Rewards are the only sanction: sending a reward unit costs 3 coins and gives the target 4 coins, so players can reward cooperation (or withhold rewards to implicitly punish defection). Players cannot see others' choices before deciding, but they observe round summaries afterward, which will drive responses in subsequent rounds.

Given the profiles:
- DUCK (highly educated, older) is likely pro-social and consistent in contributing and rewarding cooperators.
- CHICKEN and GORILLA are reasonably cooperative but conditional: they tend to reward cooperators and withhold for defectors.
- SLOTH (named suggestively) is the likeliest early freerider and may switch to cooperation if rewarded or socially pressured.

Expectations: early rounds show cooperative contributions from most players with one or two free-rides by SLOTH; cooperators reward each other modestly and withhold from obvious free-riders; if a player switches to cooperation mid-game they will be rewarded. Near the final rounds, defection increases, and rewards decline.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), 23 rounds. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws the full 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The total contributed is multiplied by 2.5 and then split equally among the four players. That means each contributor increases the group's shared return by 50 coins, which gives each player +12.5 coins in that round. Withdrawing privately gives an immediate private gain of 20 but reduces the group share by 12.5 for everyone else.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Short-term incentive: withhold (take 20) to gain private 20 while still receiving the group's return. If others contribute, withholding yields a higher payoff that round.
- Long-term/reciprocal motive: contributions can sustain higher group payoffs if many contribute repeatedly. But because choices are binary and players see round summaries (including peers' payoffs), defections are observed and can trigger retaliation (withdrawals) or attempts to coordinate.
- Uncertainty: players don't see choices before deciding each round, only afterward. Messages are allowed and can be used to coordinate or shame/promise; in practice chat tends to be sparse and used for occasional coordination or complaints.
- Given profiles: expect some initial goodwill (GORILLA and CHICKEN may try to encourage cooperation), DUCK (job-seeking) may be tempted to free-ride intermittently, SLOTH (employed, "sloth" name) may be generally cooperative but not strongly punitive — cooperation likely to fluctuate, with periodic full-cooperation attempts and several rounds of mutual defection.

Expected overall pattern: early rounds see partial cooperation, a few persistent free-rides, subsequent rounds show reciprocal punishment and occasional recovery attempts via chat. Over 23 rounds cooperation will vary but tends to decline, though late-round attempts to re-establish cooperation or a final “end-game” burst of cooperation are plausible.

Withdrawing privately gives an immediate private gain of 20 but reduces the group share by 12.5 for everyone else.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Short-term incentive: withhold (take 20) to gain private 20 while still receiving the group's return. If others contribute, withholding yields a higher payoff that round.
- Long-term/reciprocal motive: contributions can sustain higher group payoffs if many contribute repeatedly. But because choices are binary and players see round summaries (including peers' payoffs), defections are observed and can trigger retaliation (withdrawals) or attempts to coordinate.
- Uncertainty: players don't see choices before deciding each round, only afterward. Messages are allowed and can be used to coordinate or shame/promise; in practice chat tends to be sparse and used for occasional coordination or complaints.
- Given profiles: expect some initial goodwill (GORILLA and CHICKEN may try to encourage cooperation), DUCK (job-seeking) may be tempted to free-ride intermittently, SLOTH (employed, "sloth" name) may be generally cooperative but not strongly punitive — cooperation likely to fluctuate, with periodic full-cooperation attempts and several rounds of mutual defection.

Expected overall pattern: early rounds see partial cooperation, a few persistent free-rides, subsequent rounds show reciprocal punishment and occasional recovery attempts via chat. Over 23 rounds cooperation will vary but tends to decline, though late-round attempts to re-establish cooperation or a final “end-game” burst of cooperation are plausible.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the public pot (contribute 20) or withdraws the full 20 for private use (contribute 0). The total contributed is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally. After payoffs are computed, players can spend coins to punish (cost 3 to source, −7 to target) or reward (cost 3 to source, +4 to target) others; players see totals in the summary but not who targeted whom. Because the game has multiple rounds and identities are stable but actions are anonymous, we expect:

- Early rounds: some players try cooperating; free-riders may appear. Conditional cooperators (who see others contributing) will respond to punishment and rewards by increasing cooperation.
- Punishment is used sparingly to discipline free-riders (often 1 unit), and rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperation.
- Once punished or rewarded, many players switch to contributing in subsequent rounds.
- In the final round, forward-looking incentives disappear and defection tends to rise (more withdraws), so cooperation often collapses and costly punishment/reward declines.

Given the player profiles, we expect CHICKEN to be an enforcer (punishes free-riders), DUCK and GORILLA to be cooperative or supportive, and SLOTH to be more inclined to test defection early on. The transcript below simulates these dynamics while keeping chat and sanctioning sparse and plausible.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) lasting six rounds. Each round players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can punish others at a cost (4 coins per punishment unit) that imposes a larger penalty on the target (12 coins per unit). Players also can send short messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: motivated contributors (especially DUCK, given profile) will contribute relatively high amounts; SLOTH (profile suggests lower effort/more free-riding) may contribute less. GORILLA may be moderate, responsive to others' behavior.
- Punishment will be used sparingly to discipline clear free-riding: cooperators may punish a low contributor to raise future contributions. Because punishment is costly, players usually apply small punishments (1–2 units) rather than large ones.
- Once punished, a typical free-rider increases contributions in subsequent rounds to avoid further punishments. Cooperation tends to rise for the middle rounds.
- Near the final round, the incentive to defect rises (no future punishment benefit), so contributions often fall again; punishment also tends to decline in the final round.

I'll simulate plausible choices and a few targeted chat messages. Punishments will be limited and only applied when someone clearly defects relative to group norms.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with four players. Each round each player either contributes their whole endowment (20) to the group pot or keeps it all (contribute = 20 or 0). Contributions are multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each additional full contribution benefits every player by +8 but costs the contributor 20 — so a single contribution is individually costly (net -12) but collectively beneficial. Because players move simultaneously and cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, there is uncertainty about others' motives. Punishment (cost 3 to sender, -7 to recipient) and reward (cost 3 to sender, +4 to recipient) allow players to sanction free-riders or reinforce cooperators, but these instruments are costly to use, so they tend to be used sparingly.

Typical dynamics here: early rounds often show some cooperation mixed with a few defections. Punishment is used occasionally to discourage free-riding; punished players often switch to contributing in subsequent rounds. Rewards sometimes reinforce cooperation. Toward the final round, forward-looking incentives disappear and some players may defect to grab private payoff — punishments tend to decline in the last round because there is no future to enforce. Expect modest, targeted punishment of repeat defectors early on, a rise in cooperation after punishment, some reciprocal small rewards, and some endgame defection.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a six-round binary public goods game where each player can either contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to the group pot or keep it privately. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and shared equally by all 10 players, so each coin contributed returns 0.62 coins to each player. That means any single contributor sacrifices 20 coins privately but increases everyone's payoff by 12.4 coins total (20 * 0.62), giving each player +12.4 coins; the contributor bears the private cost entirely, so contributors earn less than free riders whenever some players do not contribute. The dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (keep 20), but repeated interaction and messaging can support cooperation if enough players coordinate on contributing.

Given the profiles, I expect some initially motivated cooperators (players with postgraduate degrees or stated principled tendencies like PARROT, OWL, DUCK) to try contributing early. Because contributions are binary and players see only round payoff information (their own and peers' net payoffs) rather than others' intentions beforehand, early cooperators will likely be "punished" by higher payoffs for free riders in the first round, which typically causes cooperation to decline. A small number of persistent cooperators might remain for a few rounds, but by the final rounds, especially the last round, most players will prefer to free-ride. Chat will be sparse and used mainly to make short promises or to express disappointment.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 6 players, private endowment 20 each round, a group multiplier of 3.8, and a punishment/reward stage after contributions. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- The individual marginal return from contributing one coin is 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to oneself — less than the private cost of 1 — so unilateral free-riding is privately attractive, but collective payoff is maximized by higher contributions.
- In early rounds players are uncertain about how others behave and will test cooperation levels. Some will start at moderate contributions (around 10–12), others may test free-riding (0–8).
- With punishment/reward available, norm enforcement commonly emerges: occasional punishments of clear low contributors and occasional rewards for high contributors. Punishment is costly (3 to sender per unit) but deducts heavily (12 per unit) from targets, so it’s used sparingly to deter persistent free-riding.
- Messages (chat) tend to be sparse and persuasive: reminders to cooperate, short calls-out after clear defections, or thanks for high contributions.
- Over rounds we expect a movement toward a stable moderate-to-high cooperation equilibrium (many players contributing around 12) with infrequent defections that are met by limited punishment and occasional small rewards to reinforce cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either keeps the whole 20 (withdraw = contribution 0) or leaves it all in the common pot (contribution 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so when many people contribute the group return per person rises, but an individual who withholds 20 gets a private 20 plus the shared return — creating a temptation to free-ride. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early rounds are exploratory: some will test cooperation; others may free-ride to exploit cooperators. The separate, costly reward stage lets players strengthen cooperation by sending small positive transfers to those they value; rewards are costly to the giver (2 coins per unit) and equally beneficial to the receiver (2 coins per unit), so they redistribute payoffs without changing group total. Expect a mix early on (some conditional cooperators, some free-riders), then increasing cooperation as cooperators reward each other and newcomers are encouraged—SLOTH (profile: disengaged, not in paid work) is the most likely consistent free-rider, while GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, MOOSE are more likely to cooperate; DOG and PARROT are more responsive and may join cooperation after being rewarded. Chat will be sparse and used to thank or signal intentions. Rewards will be scarce and targeted mainly at cooperators or new cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players where, each round, a player either keeps all 20 coins (contributes 0) or leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so each additional contributor raises payoffs for everyone, but an individual who withdraws the 20 keeps a private gain while still receiving the group share — creating a tension between private incentive to free-ride and collective incentive to contribute. After the public return is distributed, players can send costly positive rewards to others (each reward unit costs 2 coins to the sender and gives 2 coins to the recipient). Because rewards are costly, they will be used sparingly and mostly to reinforce cooperative behavior or reciprocate cooperation.

Given the players' profiles, I expect a small core of prosocial players (PARROT, MOOSE, DUCK) to contribute early and to reward other contributors; a few players (SLOTH, CHICKEN) are more likely to free-ride initially; others (GORILLA, DOG) may respond conditionally and shift toward cooperation if they see reciprocal rewarding. Chats will be few and directive (e.g., PARROT signalling reward intentions); rewards will be modest (1–2 units) and aimed at contributors, with occasional reciprocation. Over eight rounds we should see cooperation stabilizing among most players, occasional free-riding by one or two, and rewards used to encourage contributors.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives (keep your 20) against group benefits (every 20 contributed becomes 124 total, split so each player gets 12.4 times the contributor's 20 contribution, i.e. 0.62 of total contributions). Because the private return on contributing (you forgo 20 but everyone—including you—gets 0.62*20 = 12.4) is smaller than the private cost, contributing is individually dominated, so free-riding is likely. However punishment is available: players can spend 2 coins per punishment unit to subtract 2 coins from a target. This creates a costly way to sanction defectors and can sustain cooperation if players are willing to pay the enforcement cost.

Given the ten heterogeneous players, I expect an early mix of cooperation and defection (some stronger pro-social types contribute, others free-ride). Initial non-punishment will let defectors earn more, prompting some cooperators to begin targeted punishments in rounds 2–4 to deter repeat defection. Punishment will be sparse (a few units, targeted at persistent defectors) because punishment itself is costly. If punishments succeed, cooperation can rise to near-full in mid rounds; in the final round the usual endgame effect will cause some players to revert to free-riding, and punishment declines as future enforcement is worthless.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately chooses to contribute either 0 or 20 of their 20 coins to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players, so each player's share equals 6.2 * (total contributions) / 10. Contributing is socially efficient (it raises the group pot a lot) but privately costly: a contributor gives up 20 but benefits from the shared pot only in a fraction of that. Because decisions are simultaneous, free-riding (contributing 0) often gives higher immediate payoff unless there is credible punishment.

Punishment is costly to both sides: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and deducts 2 coins from the target. Players see the round summary (who contributed, who punished whom, how much was spent/received) so punishment can serve as a deterrent across rounds. Expect early rounds to show mixed cooperation; cooperators will try to discipline repeat free-riders with a small number of targeted punishment actions. Punishment is used sparingly — only a few players will pay to punish obvious free-riders. If punishment effectively targets persistent defectors, cooperation can rise in mid-game. However, in the last round many players often defect because there's no future enforcement; some spiteful punishments can still occur in the final round.

I will simulate a plausible sequence: modest cooperation in round 1 with a few punishments targeted at the most conspicuous free-riders, punishments shape behavior so cooperation increases in the middle rounds, full cooperation briefly in round 5, and some unraveling in round 6 with a couple of last-round punishments.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round binary public goods game. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute 20 coins (full contribution) or 0 (keep all). Total contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among the 10 players. Contributing is socially efficient (if everyone contributed, each would get a large share) but individually costly in any single round because a contributor reduces their private holding by 20 while the benefit from their contribution is only (20 * 6.2)/10 = 12.4 to each player, so the contributor's personal return from contributing is 12.4 while they lose 20 — a net private loss. This creates a strong incentive to free-ride (keep your 20) and still enjoy others' contributions.

Given the provided player profiles and typical experimental patterns:
- Some players (often with higher education and/or prosocial priors) tend to start by contributing, but many quickly reduce contributions after observing that free-riders earn more within a round.
- Messages can sustain cooperation early if people pledge to contribute; however, because players never see others' choices before deciding, the immediate material incentive to defect is strong and contributions usually decline over rounds (conditional cooperators respond to others' perceived behavior).
- Toward the final rounds, backward-induction and the observed advantage of free-riding typically drive contributions down further, though a few participants may continue to contribute from principle.

Expect an initial moderate level of contributions from some players, then a gradual decline across rounds, with sparse chat (short promises/complaints) and occasional stubborn cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, each getting 20 coins per round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 3.8 and split evenly, so every contributed coin returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to each player. Contributing is individually costly (you lose 1 coin but only get ~0.63 back), so a selfish best response is to contribute 0. However, social incentives (communication, rewards, and punishments) can sustain higher contributions. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) and very costly to the target (−12 coins per unit), so players will use punishment sparingly but it can powerfully deter free-riding. Rewards are cheaper for the recipient (+2 per unit) than they are for the giver (−3 per unit), so rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce cooperation.

Given the player profiles (mixed ages, employment, education and countries), I predict many rounds will show moderate cooperative contributions with occasional low contributions (free-riding) by one or two players. Punishments will be rare and targeted (usually one unit) and used to discipline persistent low contributors. Rewards will be occasional, used to reinforce particularly cooperative behavior. Chat will be sparse and purposeful (greetings, short appeals to cooperate, brief reprimands after clear free-riding). Over 26 rounds we should see cooperation that fluctuates — rises after enforcement or rewards, dips when players experiment with lower contributions — but no extreme or continuous punishment spree.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round everyone decides whether to contribute the whole 20 coins (contribute = 20) or withdraw it all (contribute = 0). The total contributed pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so an individual who defects (withdraws 20) gets the private 20 plus the shared return, which usually gives defecting a short-run advantage if many others contribute. Because the number of rounds is unknown, reputation and conditional cooperation through rewards and messages can sustain cooperation early on; toward the (unknown) end some players may try to exploit by defecting. Rewards are costly to the sender (2 coins per reward unit) and beneficial to the receiver (+2 coins per unit) — effectively transfers used mainly to acknowledge cooperation. In practice we expect: a few steady cooperators (academically/employment-stable profiles) will contribute consistently and selectively reward other cooperators; a small subset (incl. "SLOTH") will free-ride persistently; some younger or more opportunistic players may switch between strategies depending on social signals (chat and rewards). Chats are generally sparse and used to call out defection or to thank cooperators. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation and rarely to reward defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated online public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone gets 20 private coins and chooses how many to contribute (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and split equally: a single coin contributed increases the group pot by 3.8 but returns only 3.8/6 ≈ 0.63 to each person, so the private payoff from contributing 1 coin is -0.37 (you lose 1, get ≈0.63 back). That makes free-riding (contributing 0) the individually dominant choice, while full contribution is socially optimal.

The reward stage lets players transfer positive incentives after seeing contributions: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the target 2 coins. Rewards are therefore costly to the sender and (net) costly for total group payoff (-1 per unit) but can reallocate payoffs to reward cooperators and support conditional cooperation. Players do not know how many rounds remain, which increases the value of steady reciprocity and makes endgame collapsing less predictable.

Likely dynamics: early rounds tend to show some conditional cooperation—many players contribute moderately (10–18) as a test. Free-riders or low contributions (often a couple of players) will appear and be met by omission of rewards (with any positive rewards selectively given to higher contributors). If some players consistently reward cooperators (even at cost), that can sustain or revive cooperation and push the group toward higher contributions. Because rewards are costly, they will be used sparingly and targeted at reliable cooperators; chat will be infrequent and mostly used to encourage or call out behavior.

The group pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so each additional cooperator increases everyone's share but benefits defectors more in the short run (defectors keep 20 plus the shared return). Because players do not know how many rounds remain, early behavior is exploratory: some players may test cooperation, others may free-ride. When cooperation is expected, small rewards are a low-cost way to reinforce it; when defection appears, a few players may pay to punish defectors to enforce the norm. Punishment is relatively costly to the source (2 coin per unit) and hits targets by 3 coins per unit; rewards cost the source 2 coins and give the target 2 coins — so rewards are less damaging than punishments but also symmetric in cost.

Given the profiles, I predict: a few players (CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE) start cooperative, some (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) test defection early. Moderately pro-social players will use sparse punishment early to deter free-riding; rewards will be used occasionally to encourage cooperative switches. Over rounds we should see defections decline and cooperation become the norm, with occasional one-shot defections and brief punishment responses.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round every player either keeps the 20 coins for themselves (contribute 0) or leaves them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so contributing increases everyone’s shared return but is individually costly: a defector who keeps 20 benefits from others' cooperation while avoiding the contribution cost. The punishment and reward stage gives players direct tools to sanction or praise peers: each unit of punishment costs the sender 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target; each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the target +2 coins. Because rounds are anonymous and players do not know how many rounds will be played, we should expect:

- Early rounds: a fair amount of cooperation by default (many players start by cooperating), with a minority of opportunistic defections.
- Punishments are usually rare and targeted rather than indiscriminate. When defectors are punished, they often switch to cooperating in subsequent rounds; some may retaliate once if they were punished first.
- Rewards are used sparingly to encourage consistent cooperators or to repair relationships after punishment.
- Over time the group tends toward a cooperative norm reinforced by occasional targeted punishments; near the (unknown) end players may test the norm, producing a few last-round defections.

Below is a simulated transcript of 8 rounds that follows these patterns. I show the reasoning for each round briefly before the events, and include the contribution vector (in the exact avatar order) and all non-zero punishment/reward actions as tuples (source, target, unit). Payoffs and summaries are calculated after redistribution and sanctions/rewards. Numeric payoffs are rounded to two decimals.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, CHICK, RABBIT, OWL </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with costly punishment and full identification of punishers. Each round players choose between contributing 20 (cooperate) or 0 (free-ride). Contributions are amplified (×6.2) and split equally, so every contribution benefits the whole group more than it benefits the contributor individually, creating a social dilemma: contributors earn less than free-riders in the same round unless punishment changes incentives. Because punishment is costly (2 coins per unit sent) but directly lowers a target's payoff (2 coins per unit), players who care about fairness or future retaliation can use it to discipline free-riders.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior as players test others. Some contributors punish obvious free-riders to signal intolerance for defection.
- Mid game: punishment typically raises cooperation; many players switch to contributing after being punished or after seeing punishments targeting defectors.
- Late rounds: once cooperation looks stable, punishment frequency often falls; occasional opportunistic defections may be punished sharply to protect cooperation.
- Chats are sparse and strategic: pledges to cooperate, short calls-out of free-riding, or brief thanks/comments after enforcement.

I will simulate plausible choices consistent with those patterns: some initial free-riding in round 1, targeted punishments, an increase in contributions by round 2–4, a brief lapse by one player in round 5 that is punished, and general cooperation in the final round. Punishment is used sparingly but visibly to enforce norms.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players. Each round everyone privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute; contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After payoffs are revealed, players can punish (costly to punishers and very harmful to targets) or reward (costly to givers and moderately beneficial to receivers). Players do not know the total number of rounds, so behavior is driven by conditional cooperation, fairness concerns, and learning over time. Expect:
- A mix of cooperators (contribute a lot) and a few freeriders early on. Freeriding initially raises interim payoffs for defectors, which can prompt targeted punishment from cooperators.
- Punishment and reward will be used sparingly: punishments mainly to sanction persistent low contributors; rewards occasionally to reinforce high contributors.
- Contribution levels will converge toward moderate-to-high cooperation as sanctions and praise shape behavior, but with occasional lapses and experiments by some players.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful (calling out behavior, short apologies, thanks), and players will typically punish only when they feel it’s worth the cost.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses either to contribute the full 20 to the group pot or keep 0 (i.e., keep their 20). The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players. Because the individual return from contributing (20 * 6.2 / 10 = 12.4) is less than keeping the 20, the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Typical dynamics in repeated play with cheap-talk: some players try to signal or pledge contributions early; conditional cooperators may contribute early but often reduce contributions after seeing they earn less than free riders; a few persistent cooperators might keep contributing out of principle, while most rational players drift toward zero contributions over time. Messages can modestly coordinate contributions, but with the strong individual incentive to keep 20 each round, contributions usually fall across rounds, often ending with everyone free-riding in later rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 10 players, binary contributions (0 or 20), a strong group multiplier (6.2) but one that still leaves individual incentives to free-ride (your private return from contributing is 12.4 < 20). Without enforcement, the dominant action is to defect (contribute 0), but costly punishment allows cooperators to discipline defectors and sustain higher group-level contributions. Early rounds are uncertain: some players experiment to find a stable norm. Punishment is costly to the punisher and typically used sparingly — often targeted at clear, repeated free-riders. Over the six rounds we expect an initial mix of contributions, some targeted punishment that raises cooperation in middle rounds, and some unraveling toward the final round as future consequences disappear. Chat messages will be infrequent and used to signal intentions or to protest punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 identifiable players, 6 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20), and costly punishment (2 coins per punishment unit, which also deducts 2 coins from the target). Each coin contributed returns 0.62 to each player (6.2 total to the group split over 10), so contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly unless enough others contribute. Because punishment is costly to the punisher but can deter free-riding by making defectors worse off, we should expect a mix of behavior: some players will contribute to sustain the public good; some will free-ride early on to exploit cooperators. A few players (those who value reciprocity or norm enforcement) will use punishment selectively against repeated defectors. Over rounds, punishment should increase compliance for some, producing more contributions after early rounds; toward the final round, the incentive to punish or contribute weakens (endgame effect), so contributions may drop slightly and punishments become sparser. Chat messages will be sparse and used to signal intent, complain about punishment, or coordinate norms.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment to the group pot or to keep it. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split evenly across all 10 players. Because the private benefit to keeping 20 coins (immediate +20) exceeds the private loss from contributing (−20) while everyone gets an equal share of the multiplied pot, the one-shot dominant action is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, repeated interaction, simple messages, conditional cooperation, and reputation concerns can sustain contributions. Expect some early contributions from people inclined to cooperate or to signal goodwill, some free-riding by more selfish players, a decline if cooperators feel exploited, occasional attempts to coordinate by messaging, and possibly modest recovery in later rounds as players try to encourage cooperation—or a final push in the last round when punishment/retribution is no longer relevant.

Players see each round’s net payoffs for themselves and all peers at the summary stage, so contributions can be inferred (there are only two possible payoff values per round: contributor vs noncontributor). That transparency tends to produce conditional responses (if many defect, cooperators reduce contributions; if many contribute, some return). Chat is available and sparse; expect short coordination appeals, mild admonishments, and occasional promises or threats.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players and an unusual contribution rule: each player either keeps the 20 coins (contribute 0) or leaves them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so the more people who contribute, the bigger everyone’s shared payoff — but defecting (withdrawing the 20) yields an immediate private gain plus the shared return. After contribution outcomes are revealed, players can use costly, positive rewards (each reward unit costs the giver 2 coins and gives the receiver 2 coins) to reward cooperative behavior or signal approval.

Given the player profiles and typical PGG dynamics, I expect some initial uncertainty: a small core of players (those inclined to be cooperative or socially aware) will contribute early, others will test whether they can exploit the group by withdrawing. Cooperators will use sparse rewards to signal and reinforce cooperation; defectors will be largely ignored (no direct punishment is possible, only withholding rewards). Over rounds the group may converge toward higher cooperation if rewards succeed in encouraging contributors, but some players (especially those who gain consistently by withdrawing) may keep free-riding. Chat will be sparse and used chiefly to encourage cooperation or call out choices.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with seven players. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contributes 20) or withdraws them for private use (contributes 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so individual incentives push toward withdrawing (free-riding) while group welfare is highest when everyone contributes. After payoffs are revealed, players may punish (cost 2 to sender, -3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target per unit). Players can see who punished/rewarded them, which supports targeted sanctioning and reputation-building even though they don't know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics: early rounds show some mix of cooperation and defection as players test others. Targeted punishments for obvious free-riding will be used sparingly by a few players to deter persistent defectors. Small, infrequent rewards can reinforce cooperators. Players who are punished will often switch to cooperation in subsequent rounds, but occasional defections reappear (testing or retaliation). Chat is sparse: short normative appeals, complaints after punishments, and occasional promises or acknowledgements.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with a binary contribution choice each round (0 or 20). Contributing costs 20 up-front but increases the public pot: total contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally. Punishment is available after contributions: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and deducts 2 coins from the target. The game lasts 6 rounds and players see round-level summaries (including who punished whom).

Likely dynamics:
- There is a temptation to free-ride (contribute 0) because a single defector can still receive the multiplied share and often earn more in a round than contributors, especially early on.
- Some players (those with postgraduate education or stated profiles more pro-social) are more likely to contribute consistently and to use punishment sparingly to enforce cooperation.
- Punishment tends to be used selectively against persistent free-riders. Because punishment is costly to the punisher, players will punish sparingly; punishments may escalate only if defection persists.
- Messages are used occasionally to request cooperation or to call out defectors.
- Over rounds we expect some back-and-forth: initial cooperation from many, occasional free-riding by a few, targeted punishment by several contributors, and gradual adjustment by at least some defectors — but not necessarily full convergence to all contributing, since punishment is costly and defectors still often earn more each round.

Given the player profiles: DUCK, PARROT, RABBIT, OWL, MOOSE, and some others are likely to be pro-social and punish free-riders sparingly. SLOTH (unemployed) and CHICKEN / CHICK (mixed education) are more likely to test free-riding. GORILLA, DOG are steady contributors or conditional cooperators. Expect sparse chat, sparse punishment, and slow movement toward more cooperation as the game progresses.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round every player faces a binary choice: withdraw the 20 coins for immediate private use (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so each player's return from the public pot depends on how many others contributed. If many contribute, everyone benefits more from the pot; if few contribute, contributors get a smaller share while withdrawers keep their private 20 plus a share.

Key dynamics and incentives:
- Individual incentive: withdraw (free-ride) because you keep 20 plus share of the public pot; this usually yields a higher round payoff than unilaterally contributing.
- Collective incentive: if many contribute, everyone gets a larger group return (the multiplied pot shared across all).
- Uncertainty/learning: players do not know how many rounds total are played, so early rounds emphasize reputation- and reciprocity-building. Players see summary information after each round (including who rewarded them), allowing conditional strategies (reward cooperators, ignore or withhold reward from free-riders).
- Reward stage: rewards are pure transfers (each reward unit costs 2 to the sender and adds 2 to the receiver). Rewards do not change total group wealth, but can reallocate payoffs and signal approval/praise. Because rewards are sparse and costly to the sender, expect few, targeted reward moves—mainly to encourage perceived cooperators or reciprocate previous rewards.
- Communication: chats are available, which can encourage cooperation or call out free-riding, but messages are cheap and not binding. Expect occasional concise appeals or thanks.

Overall predicted unfolding: some players will try contributing early (pro-social or reputation-building), others will free-ride to exploit the public pot. Cooperators will occasionally reward fellow cooperators to sustain cooperation; rewards remain sparse and targeted. Over rounds we expect some fluctuation: cooperation may fall after repeated exploitation but can rebound when players coordinate via brief chat and targeted rewards.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players against a common social dilemma: each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, so the group benefits from contributions but any individual gets a smaller private return than the cost of contributing — creating an incentive to free-ride. The punishment/reward stage afterward gives players a way to enforce cooperation (punishment is costly for the sender but very costly to the target; rewards are also costly but less so and give a modest benefit to the target). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early behavior and signaling matter: a small amount of punishment or targeted rewards can sustain cooperation, but heavy or repeated punishment is costly and tends to be used sparingly in real games. Messaging is possible and can coordinate norms, but chat tends to be brief.

Given the provided profiles, I predict:
- DUCK and DOG will be the most steady cooperators/enforcers (high contributions; occasional punishment or reward).
- CHICKEN and SLOTH will be moderate cooperators and occasionally use messages or small rewards.
- GORILLA and PARROT start more inclined to under-contribute; GORILLA may respond to occasional punishment by increasing contributions, PARROT will be an intermittent low contributor and a frequent target of mild sanctions.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly: a few single-unit punishments to deter repeated free-riding, and occasional single-unit rewards to reinforce cooperation.

Below is a simulated round-by-round transcript (round 1 through 26) consistent with these priors and the game rules.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players, binary contribution choices each round (either contribute the full 20 coins to the public pot or withdraw all 20 for private use). The public pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so an individual contributor loses 20 while only receiving ~7.43 of the return to themselves per contribution — a privately costly act unless group enforcement (punishment/reward) or reciprocity supports cooperation. Because punishment costs the punisher 2 to impose a -3 on a target, and rewards cost the giver 2 to give +2 to a target, social enforcement is possible but costly.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players try cooperating; some free-ride. Conditional cooperators will respond to others' behavior, and a few players will use punishment sparingly to deter persistent free-riders.
- Punishment tends to be used cautiously (because it costs the punisher), often targeted at repeat defectors. Rewards are used even more sparingly to reinforce cooperation.
- Over rounds, punishment usually stabilizes cooperation (defectors switch to contributing) unless retaliation or chilling occurs. Because players do not know how many rounds there are, cooperation incentives can persist.
- Chats are sparse and purposeful (requests to contribute, warnings, thanks).

- Chats are sparse and purposeful (requests to contribute, warnings, thanks).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with ten players, six rounds, and a binary contribution choice each round: contribute your entire 20-coin endowment or contribute 0. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 then split equally across all players. The social dilemma: contributing increases the group's total payoff but is costly to the contributor in the short run — for any fixed number of contributors, a non-contributor earns 20 coins more than a contributor. Because players see round-by-round payoffs for everyone, they can infer who contributed and update expectations.

Likely dynamics:
- Round 1: uncertainty; some socially inclined players will try to signal cooperation (pledges), others will free-ride to test the group. Expect a mixed outcome with several contributors.
- Middle rounds (2–4): players who contributed and were exploited may reduce contributions to punish free-riders; conditional cooperators may reciprocate, so contributions often decline after seeing free-riding. Sparse, targeted chat (calls for cooperation, mild reproaches) can partially sustain cooperation among a core subset.
- Late rounds (5–6): as the end approaches, strategic incentives to defect strengthen (backward induction), so contributions typically fall further, possibly to zero in the final round. Some altruistic or rule-driven players may still contribute in later rounds, but overall cooperation tends to decay.

I will simulate a plausible run consistent with the player profiles: CHICKEN (postgrad, older) and DOG (college degree) act as steady cooperators/organizers; PARROT and MOOSE start cooperative but are conditional; RABBIT and CHICK are somewhat cooperative but reactive; SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, OWL lean toward free-riding or testing. Chat will be sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This public goods game is a repeated, anonymous interaction with binary contributions each round (either keep all 20 coins privately (0 contributed) or contribute all 20 to the group (20 contributed)). The public pot is multiplied by 2.6 and divided equally among the 7 players. Because the multiplier is shared among everyone, a lone contributor gives up 20 coins to produce a relatively small per-person return; free-riding (withdrawing) typically yields a higher immediate payoff. However, the group can use costly punishment (each unit costs 2 for the punisher and subtracts 3 from the target) or costly rewards (each unit costs 2 and gives 2 to the target) after seeing contributions. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so conditional strategies (cooperate if others cooperated, punish persistent free-riders) and experimentation (testing defections) are common. Punishment tends to be used sparingly because it is costly to the sender, and rewards are also rare but can sustain cooperation if used selectively.

Given the player profiles and typical PGG dynamics, I predict initial mixed behavior (a minority of cooperators testing the waters), occasional targeted punishment of repeat defectors, gradual movement toward more cooperation as punishments and rewards signal norms, and a few defections later as some players test whether cooperation still holds. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to signal intentions, protest punishments, or encourage cooperation.

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, unknown horizon, and a costly reward stage after the public-good payout. Early on we expect moderate cooperation: some players (Dog, Chicken) are predisposed to contribute more, some (Sloth) lower. Because players don't know when the game ends, cooperation is less likely to crash sharply at a known end; instead we expect modest downward drift as free-riding and small shocks accumulate. Rewards are costly to the giver and only partially benefit recipients, so they will be used sparingly — mainly to reinforce high contributors or to reciprocate helpful behaviour. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to thank others or signal intentions. Over 26 rounds I predict contributions start in the middle-to-high range for some players, with occasional rewards targeted at visible cooperators; contributions gradually decline but with intermittent recoveries where small reward gestures or appreciative chat encourage cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary-contribution public goods game with 7 players. Each round players choose either to keep the 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the common pot (contribute 20). The pot is amplified (×2.6) and split evenly, so every contributed 20 gives 2.6×20 = 52 coins divided among 7 players (≈7.43 per player). That makes contributing socially efficient (everyone benefits) but individually costly compared with free-riding (keeping the 20 yields an immediate private 20 plus your share of the public pot).

Given the players' profiles, I expect a mixed pattern: several higher-education, older or more settled players (CHICKEN, PARROT, DOG, SLOTH, MOOSE) tend toward cooperation early on; younger or lower-commitment players (GORILLA, DUCK) are likelier to test free-riding initially. Because rewards are available (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target), cooperators can positively reinforce contributors; there is no direct costly punishment though (only omission of rewards serves as soft sanction). Players don't know how many rounds remain, so initial rounds will be exploratory: low levels of targeted rewards, then more reciprocity if cooperation stabilizes. Chat is sparse and typically used to thank or request cooperation.

I simulate 8 rounds where cooperation emerges among most players, with GORILLA and DUCK testing defection occasionally. Rewards are used sparingly to acknowledge cooperators; defectors are mainly ignored (not rewarded) rather than actively punished.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players who each get 20 coins each round. Each round they privately choose how much (0–20) to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After participants see contributions and the redistributed shares, they can spend costly reward units (3 coins each) to give 2 coins to another player. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early rounds usually see tentative cooperation, norms emerge, and reward is used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior or reciprocate helpful players. Because rewards are costly, players will rarely send many units; they send small, targeted rewards to encourage higher contributors. Given the provided profiles, I predict CHICKEN and GORILLA will tend to contribute more, PARROT and DUCK will contribute less, and SLOTH and DOG will be moderate. Rewards will be sparse and typically directed toward higher contributors (especially CHICKEN and GORILLA) to sustain cooperation. Chat will be used rarely for short coordination, thanks, or brief comments.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private incentive (keep 20 coins) against a group benefit: every 20-coin contribution is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally across the 10 players. The private incentive to defect (keep your 20) dominates in a single-shot decision, because a defector always ends the round 20 coins better off than a contributor given the same number of contributors. However, contributions raise total group payoffs. With 6 rounds and private messaging, common dynamics are: a modest level of cooperation early (some players pledge to contribute), especially if a few take the lead; then erosion as players observe they could have earned 20 more by free-riding; and eventual decline to widespread defection. Messaging can sustain cooperation if a clear small group coordinates, but with 10 players and only binary choices, sustaining full cooperation is difficult. Expect a few consistent cooperators early, gradual drop-out as rounds progress, sparse but targeted messages (pledges, complaints, short explanations).

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses how many to put in a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and then split equally. Because each coin put in yields less than 1 coin back to its contributor (3.8/6 ≈ 0.633), the individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) while the group would be better off if everyone contributed lots. The ability to punish (cost 3 to sender, -12 to target per unit) and reward (cost 3 to sender, +2 to target per unit) creates a mechanism for social enforcement: a small number of punishment units can strongly deter low contributions, while rewards can reinforce cooperation but are relatively costly to give.

Given the player profiles and usual lab behavior, I predict:
- Early rounds show mixed contributions as players explore others' tendencies.
- Low contributors (notably SLOTH in this simulation) will be called out and occasionally punished; punishment will be used sparingly but decisively (1 unit from a few players) because punishment is expensive but very effective to deter defection.
- Many players act as conditional cooperators: they increase contributions after others show cooperation and sometimes reward high contributors. Rewards appear but less often than punishments because rewards are relatively costly and less effective per unit.
- Over middle rounds the group tends to stabilize at relatively high contributions (often 15–18), with occasional drops by one player that trigger isolated punishment and re-adjustment.
- Chat is sparse and purposeful (short calls for cooperation, brief acknowledgements or admonishments).

game_explanation: This is a repeated (6-round) public goods game with a high multiplier (6.2) and binary contributions (0 or 20). Early rounds often see some cooperation as players test one another and possibly signal intentions; however, because contributors earn less in a round than free-riders (they forgo 20 private coins but share the multiplied pot equally), free-riding is individually attractive. Expect conditional cooperation: some players who see many contributions will keep contributing for a few rounds, but when contributors notice they earn less than non-contributors they tend to reduce giving, producing a gradual decline in contributions. Messages can sustain cooperation briefly if trusted, but in the final round many players will defect (the typical end-game unraveling).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone chooses either to contribute all 20 coins to a shared pot or keep them. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players. Individually, free-riding (keeping 20) strictly dominates contributing because a single 20-coin contribution increases each player's share by only 12.4, so a contributor loses 7.6 relative to keeping the 20. Collectively, however, everyone is better off if all contribute (the group payoff rises with more contributors).

Given those incentives, typical dynamics: some initial cooperation mixed with free-riding, followed by gradual decline in contributions over rounds as players respond to observed behavior and the known finite horizon (end-game effects). Communication can sustain cooperation early on but is unlikely to fully offset the incentive to free-ride; conditional cooperators will reduce contributions if they observe many free-riders. Uncertainties include each player's social preferences (some may be strongly pro-social), how credible pledges are, and whether defectors are punished by reciprocal withholding later. Expect sparse, focused chat (promises, short complaints or reminders) and a general downward trend in contributions across rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with optional punishment and reward after contributions. Players start with 20 coins each round and decide privately how many coins to contribute (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and split equally; then players can pay to punish (cost 3 to sender, -12 to target per unit) or reward (cost 3 to sender, +2 to target per unit). Because the public good return per coin (3.8/6 ≈ 0.633) is less than the private value of keeping a coin, there is an individual incentive to free ride, but group-level payoff is higher when people contribute. Punishment is a strong deterrent (large subtraction to the target) and often stabilizes cooperation if used sparingly; rewards are weaker (costly to the sender, small gain to the target) but can encourage cooperators.

Given the player profiles, I expect an initial moderate level of cooperation with a few lower contributors (SLOTH tends toward lower effort). If someone defects noticeably, a small number of players (more cooperative types like GORILLA and DUCK) will punish sparingly to signal that free riding is costly; punished players usually increase contributions afterward. Rewards will appear occasionally to encourage or thank cooperators, but less frequently than punishment. Because players don’t know the number of rounds, actions are shaped by recent history; cooperation should stabilize at a fairly high level after a few enforcement events, with occasional deviations and small corrective punishments or rewards.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual short-term incentives (keep your 20 coins) against group benefits (contribute 20 so the pot is multiplied and shared). Because the multiplier per coin (2.6 overall) divided among seven players yields less than the private return for any single coin, the dominant one-shot incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). However, because players observe round summaries (who punished/rewarded whom and the net outcomes) and can use costly punishments (2 coins cost to sender, -3 coins to target) or costly rewards (2 coins cost to sender, +2 coins to target), social enforcement can sustain higher contribution rates over time.

Key dynamics and uncertainties likely here:
- Early rounds: some players will experiment. Those with more pro-social priors (e.g., DUCK, PARROT, MOOSE) may contribute; others (SLOTH, CHICKEN, DOG) may try free-riding. Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), decisions are stark and easy to observe in summary payoffs.
- Punishment/reward will be used sparingly. Punishment is costly to apply, so only a few players will retaliate against obvious defectors; rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce cooperation.
- Over rounds we expect some movement toward more cooperation if punishers are persistent, but also occasional defections (especially by players who see higher net payoffs from free-riding). With no guarantee of number of rounds, players will not fully switch to “endgame” defecting and will remain partly responsive to recent sanctions.
- Chats will be sparse and mostly used to call out free-riders, thank cooperators, or signal intentions.

game_explanation:
This public goods game has simple binary contribution choices: each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws the full 20 for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied (by 2.6) and shared equally among the 7 players, so cooperators reduce their private take but raise the group share; defectors keep 20 and still receive an equal share of the multiplied pot. Because players can reward others at a cost (2 coins from sender buys +2 for a target), the only mechanism for social enforcement is positive transfers: cooperators can reward fellow cooperators to encourage cooperation, and they can withhold rewards from defectors. There is no direct punishment, only selective rewarding.

Likely dynamics given the player profiles and sparse chat/rewarding seen in many lab games:
- Early rounds are exploratory. Some players with pro-social priors (e.g., GORILLA, PARROT, CHICKEN, MOOSE) are likelier to cooperate; others (SLOTH, DUCK, DOG) may try defecting early to test returns.
- Cooperators will start to use small, targeted rewards (1 unit = 2 coins cost, +2 to recipient) to recognize cooperators. Defectors earn higher immediate payoffs, so rewards must be used strategically to encourage switching.
- Over repeated rounds (no finite-horizon known to participants), cooperation tends to rise if cooperators reliably reward each other and selectively reward newcomers who start cooperating. But occasional opportunistic defections (especially in late rounds if players suspect an end) are plausible.
- Chat will be sparse and typically used to signal intentions ("I'll reward cooperators") or react to behavior ("Thanks for cooperating"). Rewards will be few and directed.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with binary choices (contribute all 20 tokens or keep them). If many players contribute, the group pot grows (total contributions × 2.6) and everyone gets a larger equal share; when few contribute, defectors individually earn more in the short run. Because the number of rounds is unknown, players will likely use conditional strategies: cooperate if others do, punish or withhold cooperation when they see persistent free-riding, and occasionally reward cooperators to reinforce norms. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and relatively harsh to the target (3 coins deducted per unit), so we expect punishment to be used sparingly and mainly to deter repeat defectors; rewards are also used sparingly. Messaging will be sparse and goal-directed (requests, thanks, mild threats or promises). Over rounds you typically see: some early free-riding, a few punishments that push defectors toward cooperation, rising cooperation, occasional opportunistic deviations, and targeted punishments or rewards to maintain cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, private endowments of 20 coins each round, a multiplier of 3.8 on the group pot, and a separate costly reward stage (3 coins cost to the sender per reward unit, giving 2 coins to the recipient). Players do not know the total number of rounds. That combination typically leads to moderate initial cooperation (many contribute above half their endowment) because players hope to earn the shared return. Because reward is costly, it will be used sparingly — mainly to encourage steady contributors or reciprocate cooperation. Free riders (low contributors) tend to get high round payoffs from the group redistribution, which frustrates cooperators; however, since rewards are the only sanctioning tool and are costly, players usually withhold rewards rather than pay to punish. Expect some short-term cycles: cooperation rises when contributors get rewarded, dips after clear free-riding, and settles to a mid-range equilibrium with occasional targeted rewarding of consistent cooperators (especially DUCK and PARROT in this group, who have profiles suggesting higher cooperation). Chat will be infrequent and focused (requests to cooperate, brief praise or frustration). Rewards will be small (1 unit or so) and directed mostly at consistent contributors; sometimes no rewards will be given if players are frustrated by free-riding.
