game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 rounds. Each round players choose either to contribute their full 20 coins to the public pot or keep all 20 privately. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players, so each contributor increases everyone’s share but contributors earn 20 coins less than non-contributors in the same round unless punishments change payoffs. After contributions are revealed, players can punish others at personal cost (2 coins per punishment unit) which also reduces the target by 2 coins per unit.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players will try cooperating (contribute) because it increases group returns; a few may free-ride. When free-riders are visible after contributions, cooperators sometimes punish to deter future free-riding.
- Punishment is usually sparse because it costs the punisher; expect a few targeted punishments rather than broad, heavy sanctions.
- If punishments are credible, contributions tend to rise in subsequent rounds. If punished players retaliate, we can see some limited cycles of retaliation (mutual single-unit punishments).
- Toward the final rounds, the incentive to free-ride rises (endgame effect). Some players who have been punished or see little benefit may defect; others who value reputation or fairness (or want to deter last-round defectors) may still punish in the final round.
- There is uncertainty about individual disposition: some profiles suggest higher propensity to cooperate (postgraduates, employed), others might be more willing to free-ride or retaliate. Expect a mix: overall cooperation above pure defection but occasional defections and targeted punishments.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual short-term incentives (keep your 20 each round) against group returns from contributing (20 per contributor gets multiplied by 6.2 and shared). Because contributions are revealed only after choices and punishment happens after redistribution, players can use the returned pot to punish free-riders. Typical dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation, and players who are punished for free-riding tend to shift toward contributing; consistent cooperators will punish repeat defectors but do so sparsely because punishment is costly. Toward the final rounds, some players often defect more (end-game effect), reducing contributions and making punishment less frequent or cheaper because players expect little future reciprocity. There is uncertainty about how strongly each player values future reputation vs. immediate payoff; profile differences (age, employment, etc.) can bias toward either conditional cooperation or opportunistic defection, and those priors guide who is likely to punish or to change strategy after being punished.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either contributes the whole 20 coins to the shared pot (contribution = 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribution = 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and then split equally among all players, so contributing helps the group but leaves the contributor with no private coins that round (they only get their share of the multiplied pot). With 7 players the per-player return from one cooperator's 20 is (20 * 2.6) / 7 ≈ 7.43, which is less than the private gain of keeping the 20. That creates the classic incentive to free-ride (withdraw 20) for immediate private benefit. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, some may try to build cooperative reputations early on; others may free-ride hoping to benefit from others' contributions.

The reward stage lets players pay 2 coins per reward unit to give 2 coins per unit to a target. Rewards are costly to the giver but beneficial to the recipient; they can be used sparingly to reward cooperators or encourage new cooperators. Because rewards are costly, expect them to be used sparingly and targeted at stable cooperators or to encourage one-off changes. Communication is allowed and typically sparse: brief promises, thanks, or short signals. Based on the provided profiles, I expect a core of players (DUCK, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE and later SLOTH) to lean toward contributing, while GORILLA is more likely to free-ride. CHICKEN may switch back and forth. Rewarding will be occasional and mostly directed to recognized cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with a small group (7 players). Each player effectively decides to contribute all of their 20-coin endowment to the group (contribute 20) or keep it all privately (contribute 0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and then split evenly across all players. That creates a clear short-run incentive to withdraw (free-ride): keeping your 20 gives you the private 20 plus your share of the multiplied public pot, while contributing 20 gives you only your share of the multiplied pot. Over repeated rounds, however, conditional cooperation and signaling (messages, rewards) can sustain contributions: cooperators may reward each other to encourage future cooperation, and messages can coordinate mass cooperation. Because rewards cost the sender 2 coins and give the recipient 2 coins (a direct transfer), they are zero-sum across the pair but serve as a clear social signal that can motivate reciprocation.

Uncertainties for players include not knowing how many rounds remain, which typically supports more sustained cooperation early on. Over time, standard results predict declining contributions as the temptation to free-ride and the desire to exploit cooperators outweigh the signaling effects, especially without strong or costly incentives to punish. Rewards are likely to be used sparingly—small positive transfers to recognize cooperators or to thank/reward those who tried to sustain cooperation. Chats will be sparse and usually aimed at coordination or brief comments about behavior.

The total contribution is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, so contributing helps the group but is individually costly. Because players can reward others at personal cost (3 coins per reward unit) to give a smaller benefit (2 coins per unit) to the recipient, rewards are typically used sparingly to encourage cooperators or to reciprocate generosity. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early cooperation can be sustained by reciprocity and selective rewarding; toward later rounds cooperation sometimes drifts down as incentives to build future reputation weaken. Chats are rare but can be used to thank or signal intentions. Given the player profiles, I expect a few reliably cooperative players (GORILLA and DOG) who contribute fairly high early on and receive most rewards; some conditional cooperators (SLOTH, CHICKEN) who follow social cues; and a few younger/unemployed participants (DUCK, PARROT) who fluctuate and occasionally under-contribute. Rewards will be used to reinforce high contributors early and will become sparser if contributions decline.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either leaves all 20 coins in the public pot (contributes 20) or withdraws them to keep privately (contributes 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so the immediate material incentive is strong to withdraw (you keep 20 plus your share of others' contributions), while the group-level payoff is maximized if everyone contributes. Because players don't see others' current-round choices when deciding but do observe past behavior and who rewarded whom, we expect the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: heterogeneous behavior — some players start contributing (pro-social types) while others free-ride, producing unequal payoffs. Contributors may try to support each other with small rewards and messages.
- Rewarding is costly (2 coins to sender per unit) and gives an identical benefit to the target, so rewards are used sparingly — mainly to reinforce cooperators rather than to “punish” defectors (there is no explicit costly punishment available).
- Communication is possible, so players can attempt to coordinate on cooperation. Because the horizon (number of rounds) is unknown, promises may increase cooperation for a while, but immediate incentives to free-ride can still cause oscillations.
- Over rounds we expect some build-up of reciprocity among the contributors (mutual small rewards), occasional defections by opportunists seeking the private gain, and sparse chat messages attempting to coordinate or criticize observed defection. Rewarding remains limited and targeted: cooperators reward cooperators; defectors are typically ignored rather than directly punished.

Below is a simulated round-by-round transcript of how this group might plausibly play across eight rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players who each get 20 coins per round and decide how much to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can punish or reward others at private cost (punish: source pays 3 per unit to reduce target by 12 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit to add 2 per unit to target). Players see per-round summaries (who punished/rewarded whom and the monetary effects) but do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics given the profiles:
- Several players (GORILLA, PARROT at times, DOG) are more inclined to maintain cooperation; GORILLA (postgraduate, full-time) is likely to enforce cooperation with occasional punishment.
- Some players are more variable (SLOTH tends to free-ride sometimes), causing others to respond with targeted punishment occasionally.
- Punishment and reward will be used sparingly: punctuated enforcement when someone under-contributes repeatedly, and occasional rewards to acknowledge cooperation.
- Contributions will hover around moderate cooperation (10–15) early on, dip when free-riding appears, then partially recover when punish/reward signals are sent. In later rounds there may be some fatigue but occasional boosts occur.

The transcript below shows 26 rounds. At each round start I note why I expect that round to unfold as shown. Contributions are listed in the exact player order: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT. Punishment/reward tuples are (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit) with positive unit = reward, negative unit = punishment. Only non-zero edges are shown. Round summaries show net payoff and what each player spent/received that round.

game_explanation:
This is a finite, repeated public goods game with 10 players, each deciding every round whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally across all players. Because a contributor forgoes their private 20 but receives only a share of the multiplied pot, contributing is personally costly when many others free-ride; conversely, free-riders benefit from others' contributions. Typical dynamics: some initial cooperation (norms, appeals, or optimistic beliefs) followed by erosion as players see that non-contributors often earn more within a round. Communication can sustain cooperation if credible and sustained, but the finite 6-round horizon creates an end‑game tendency toward defection in later rounds. Expect a mix: a core of norm-driven contributors who try to sustain cooperation early, some opportunistic free-riders who exploit that, small attempts at coordination via chat, and a decline in contributions toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 10 identifiable players, 6 rounds, and binary contributions (0 or 20). The group's total contribution is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally; each coin contributed returns 0.62 to every player, so an individual gets less than one coin back per coin contributed. That creates a private incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) while the group is better off if many contribute. Because contributions are visible only after choices, and punishment is costly but targeted and identifiable, we expect the following broad dynamics:

- Many players will be tempted to free-ride (especially early and in the final round), because immediate private payoff is higher when others contribute.
- Some players (especially those with education or older age signaling stronger norms) will contribute to sustain group returns and may occasionally punish free-riders to enforce cooperation. Punishment is used sparingly because it costs punisher coins.
- Punishment should be targeted at repeat or obvious free-riders. Punishment in early rounds tends to reduce free-riding in subsequent rounds; however, contributions typically drift down toward the last round because of backward induction (no future benefit from cooperation).
- Chat messages will be sparse and purposeful (promises, reproaches, or short coordination attempts), and punishment will be used modestly (a few units) rather than broadly.

Given the provided profiles, I predict a mix: a core of contributors (e.g., DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT, RABBIT, OWL, MOOSE) will mostly contribute; a few players (SLOTH, CHICK, maybe DOG or GORILLA early or late) will sometimes free-ride. Punishments will target conspicuous free-riders, especially SLOTH and CHICK when they persist.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game in which each player chooses to contribute either 0 or 20 out of a 20-coin endowment. Each contributed 20 goes into a pot that is multiplied by 6.2 and split evenly among all 10 players. For any one player, contributing is privately dominated: a contributor gives up 20 but receives only 12.4 per contributing player (including themselves) in return, so a lone contributor loses relative to free-riding. The group payoff is maximized when more people contribute, but individual incentives push toward free-riding.

Given these incentives and the fact players do not see others' choices before deciding, I predict modest initial cooperation from some players with social preferences or pro-social prompts, then gradual erosion as contributors see they are being exploited (free-riders get higher private payoffs). Occasional messages may try to coordinate cooperation, but without enforcement cooperation tends to decline across rounds. In the final round many players will defect because there is no future punishment or reward. Chat will be sparse and purposeful (requests to contribute, brief acknowledgements or complaints).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players, each deciding every round whether to contribute their whole endowment (20 coins) to the public pot or to withdraw it for private use. Contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all players; defecting (withdrawing) gives a higher private immediate payoff unless enough others contribute or social rewards compensate cooperators. After the public payoffs are revealed each round players can spend costly positive rewards (each unit costs 2 coins and gives 2 coins to the target). Rewards can only reward (not punish) and are visible in the summary, so rewards are used to encourage cooperators or thank switchers.

Given the profiles, I expect a mixed start: some players with stronger pro-social priors (e.g., GORILLA, DOG, DOG/ MOOSE/ PARROT with postgraduate degrees) will contribute in early rounds while a few will defect to gain the short-term private benefit. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation and reciprocate. Over rounds we should see a modest convergence toward higher cooperation as contributors reward each other and occasional messengers ask for universal contribution; but one or two players may free-ride longer because nothing directly punishes them. Chat will be sparse — short commitments, thanks, or mild appeals. Rewards will be modest (1–2 units) and targeted at cooperators or at players who newly switch.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly rewards. Each round, players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. Because each contributed coin returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.6333 coins to the contributor, the immediate monetary incentive is to contribute nothing (free-ride). However, the reward stage allows players to spend 3 coins to give 2 coins to another player, creating a social incentive to reward cooperation (or withhold rewards from low contributors). Players do not know how many rounds remain, so conditional strategies (rewarding cooperators, withholding rewards from free-riders) can sustain cooperation.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: modest-to-moderate cooperation as players test one another (contributions around mid-range). Sparse, targeted rewarding emerges to reinforce cooperators.
- Middle rounds: players who consistently contribute get occasional positive reinforcement; free-riders get little reward. If rewards are used, contributions can stay stable at moderate levels. Because rewards are costly to the rewarder, they will be used sparingly—usually 0–2 units per round and directed to clear cooperators.
- Late rounds: without clear end knowledge, some players may reduce contributions toward the end if they expect less reciprocal rewarding; others may maintain contributions if rewards have been effective. Chat messages are uncommon but used occasionally for coordination or mild praise/criticism.

Now the game transcript from round 1 through round 26.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 6 players, private endowments (20 coins each) per round, and costly punishment/reward after contributions. Early rounds typically show moderate contributions as players learn about others; some low contributors (tempted to free-ride) will prompt mild social sanctions (punishments) or occasional praise (rewards). Punishments are costly to send (3 coins per unit) but inflict large costs on targets (12 coins per unit), so they are used sparingly and mainly to discipline repeated low contributors. Rewards are also costly (3 coin cost, +2 to target) and used rarely to reinforce consistent cooperation. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, cooperation tends to be maintained by reputation and occasional sanctions; occasional slackening and targeted punishment re-align contributions. Chats are sparse and used to call out or thank others; punishments are limited and directed.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly positive rewards. Each round, players decide how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a public pot that is multiplied by 3.8 and shared equally among the six players. Because each contributed coin returns less than 1 to the contributor (about 0.633 each), the short-term incentive is to free-ride, but the group payoff is maximized by full cooperation. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly reward-units (3 coins each) to give others a small benefit (2 coins per unit). Rewards are the only means of direct social sanction/reinforcement here: players will mostly use them sparingly to encourage repeated cooperators or reward especially high contributors. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so strategies that foster reciprocity (rewarding cooperators) or stable clustering of cooperation are likely.

Given the player profiles:
- CHICKEN and PARROT (college-educated, employed) are most likely to contribute relatively high and reward cooperators.
- GORILLA (full-time employed) is inclined to moderate-to-high cooperation and occasional rewarding.
- DUCK and DOG (young/unemployed) may be more variable — sometimes increasing contribution when others cooperate, sometimes freeriding.
- SLOTH (retired) is most likely to contribute little and rarely reward.

I expect early rounds to show heterogenous contributions (some high, some low), with sparse positive rewards directed to consistent high contributors. Over time, if rewards reinforce cooperative players, others may increase contributions, producing a cluster of higher contributions midgame. Because rewards are costly, they remain relatively infrequent and targeted. Chat will be sparse — a short proposal or a thank-you occasionally.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, each choosing either to contribute 20 coins or keep them each round. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and evenly split, so total cooperation is socially best (everyone contributes), but each individual gains more by free-riding in that same round. Because the horizon is 6 rounds, we expect some tension between short-term incentive to free-ride and longer-run norm enforcement via punishment.

Typical dynamics likely to appear here:
- Early rounds: a mix of contributors and free-riders. Contributors earn less than free-riders unless they use punishment to deter defectors.
- Punishment is costly to the punisher but can deter repeat free-riding. Expect sparse, targeted punishment (a few 1-unit sanctions) by contributors toward clear free-riders in early rounds.
- If punishment successfully signals that free-riding will be penalized, cooperation should increase in the middle rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with costly, unilateral rewards after seeing contributions. The social optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20, but private incentives push toward free-riding (contribute 0). Because players don't know how many rounds remain, there is no definite endgame collapse, but short-term reciprocity and the visible reward links will shape behavior.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test the group with moderate contributions and a few messages. Pro-social, employed and younger players (GORILLA, DOG, DUCK, PARROT) tend to contribute more; older/retired players (SLOTH, CHICKEN) are more cautious and contribute less.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperative contributors (1–2 units typically). Because each reward unit costs 3 to give and gives 2 to the target, senders will be selective — they reward high contributors or reciprocators.
- When a clear free rider appears, others will mostly withhold rewards (not punish directly), causing that player's contributions to be less attractive as a strategy. Periodic cooperation recovery can happen after mutual rewarding and short positive messages.
- Chat is infrequent and used to signal intentions, praise, or request reciprocity.
Overall, expect moderate cooperation with fluctuations depending on perceived fairness and occasional targeted rewarding to sustain higher contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, 20 coins each round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, so each coin returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.6333 to the contributor — less than 1 — which makes the individually rational move to contribute 0 (free-ride), while group-optimal behavior is to contribute all 20. Because players do not know how many rounds there will be, and because punishments (strong deterrent: each punishment unit costs the sender 3 but removes 12 from the target) and rewards (each reward unit costs the sender 3 and gives the target +2) are available after seeing contributions, we expect a mix of behaviors:

- Early rounds: moderate contributions as players explore others' behavior and try to signal cooperation. Some players may test free-riding.
- Enforcement: players who value cooperation (likely the more educated or older profiles) will punish low contributors sparingly (usually 1 unit) to deter persistent free-riding. Punishment is costly so it is applied selectively.
- Rewards: used occasionally to encourage high contributors; less frequent than punishment because the sender pays 3 to give only +2 to the recipient.
- Dynamics: contributions may bounce early, but with periodic punishment/reward the group can maintain relatively high contributions. Slackers (e.g., SLOTH or DUCK) may be punished when they repeatedly contribute little. Chat is sparse and typically used to request cooperation or call out low contributors.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits two incentives against one another. Each round players can either keep the whole 20 coins for themselves (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all players, so a contributing player's private payoff is initially lower than a free-rider's — but a larger number of contributors increases the group return and thus each player's redistributed share. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, early rounds often show experimentation: some players try contributing to seed cooperation, others free-ride to test whether contributors will be rewarded. The reward stage allows pro-social reinforcement: a small, targeted reward (each unit costs 2 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver) can encourage contributors, but players tend to reward sparingly because rewards are costly. If contributors are repeatedly exploited (they earn less than free-riders), cooperation tends to collapse; if contributors are rewarded, cooperation can stabilize or grow. Communication (chat) is sparse and often used to declare intentions or react to past behavior. Expect mixed behavior early, some praise of contributors, a brief rise in cooperation if rewards appear, followed potentially by a decline as free-riding becomes tempting.

The reward stage allows pro-social reinforcement: a small, targeted reward (each unit costs 2 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver) can encourage contributors, but players tend to reward sparingly because rewards are costly. If contributors are repeatedly exploited (they earn less than free-riders), cooperation tends to collapse; if contributors are rewarded, cooperation can stabilize or grow. Communication (chat) is sparse and often used to declare intentions or react to past behavior. Expect mixed behavior early, some praise of contributors, a brief rise in cooperation if rewards appear, followed potentially by a decline as free-riding becomes tempting.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either withdraws the full 20 coins (contribute 0) or leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so an individual always gets only a fraction of the social return from contributing while keeping 20 is immediately attractive. Left unchecked, the single-round incentive is to free-ride (withdraw 20). However, the punishment and reward stage can change effective incentives: cooperators can reward each other (costly but positive transfers) and can punish defectors (costly for the punisher and even costlier for the punished). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, reputation-building and conditional cooperation can emerge: early punishment of defectors can encourage switching to cooperation; occasional rewards sustain contributors. Expect relatively few messages, sparse punishment, and small, targeted reward/punishment actions across rounds. Cooperation may start low, rise as a subset of players reciprocate and try to enforce good behavior, and then fluctuate as some test defections and are (sometimes) sanctioned.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game played for 16 rounds. Each round every player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot. Contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across the 13 players. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 — greater than 0.5 — so every coin contributed raises total group payoffs substantially but reduces the contributor's private holding (each contributed coin returns ≈0.623 coins to its giver, a personal loss of ≈0.377 per coin). That creates the standard tension: contributing benefits the group more than keeping the coins, yet individually it is typically optimal to free-ride.

Given the 16-round finite horizon and the mixed profiles of players, I predict:
- Early rounds: a mix of moderate contributions from some (those with more cooperative priors or older/educated profiles) and lower contributions from others. Initial trust yields above-zero contributions on average.
- Middle rounds: a gradual decline in contributions as free-riding and uncertainty about others' generosity accumulate. Occasional rebounds may occur if some players briefly increase contributions.
- Late rounds: contributions tend to fall, often substantially in the final round (end-game effect) because there's no future to sustain reciprocity.
- Heterogeneity: some players (college-educated, older, or more risk-averse) are more likely to sustain above-average contributions intermittently; others (younger or unemployed profiles) will often contribute less.

Uncertainties include: players' intrinsic preferences for cooperation, risk attitudes, and whether any players try to condition contributions on observed payoffs (players only see their own round payoff, not others’ contributions), which makes reputation formation harder and accelerates decline.

The transcript below simulates these plausible dynamics across all 16 rounds, showing each round's explanation, the contribution vector (in the exact avatar order), and what each player would see at the round summary (their own net payoff for that round).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game where each player privately has 20 coins each round and chooses how much (0–20) to contribute to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among the 10 players; each coin contributed returns 6.6/10 = 0.66 coins to each player. Because an individual loses 1 coin by contributing but only gains 0.66 back, the dominant monetary incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, many players have social preferences (fairness, reciprocity) or are influenced by messages, so some initial cooperation is likely.

Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no predictable final-round “collapse,” which tends to support more persistent cooperation than in known-finite games. Players do, however, see their own net payoff at the end of each round; from their private payoff and their own contribution they can infer the group total contribution (so there is indirect feedback about how the group behaved). Chat is allowed but typically sparse; occasional positive commitments or complaints will influence expectations. Given the player profiles (mix of higher-education, various ages), I predict an initially moderate level of cooperation driven by a few committed contributors, followed by a gradual decline toward free-riding as some players reduce contributions in response to inferred low group totals. Messages will be infrequent and mostly short commitments, mild encouragement, or complaints about low cooperation.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round players privately decide how much of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot that is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can pay to reduce others' earnings (punishment): each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and deducts 5 coins from the target. Players do not know how many rounds remain. That uncertainty encourages cooperation early on (to secure steady returns), while occasional defections are likely to be met with targeted punishment by cooperative players to enforce norms. Punishment will be used sparingly because it is costly to the punisher, but it will appear when low contributions are obvious and repeated. Some players (profile hints: "SLOTH", "RABBIT", "PARROT", "MOOSE") might test by contributing low early on; high contributors (e.g., CHICKEN, CHICK, DUCK, DOG) will often try to sustain cooperation and occasionally punish free riders. Expect contributions to trend upward as punishments deter free riding, with intermittent slips and light retaliation-focused punishment rather than sweeping sanctions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each per round. Each coin contributed yields a return of 6.6/10 = 0.66 to each contributor (less than the private 1 coin you keep), so the dominant private incentive is to keep coins (free-ride), while the group payoff is maximized when many contribute. Players do not see others' contributions before choosing, and they only learn their own net payoff after each round — so they must infer others' behavior from their private payoff and any chat. Chat is cheap talk and may be used to signal intentions or to shame/praise.

Given these incentives and limited feedback, I expect:
- Early rounds: some optimistic/cooperative messages and relatively high contributions from a minority (conditional cooperators); a few will experiment by free-riding.
- Middle rounds: contributions decline overall as conditional cooperators respond to perceived free-riding; a small core of consistent contributors (who value the group outcome or want to signal cooperation) keep contributing.
- Late rounds: further gradual decline or low plateau, possibly a small rebound if players try to reinitiate cooperation via chat. Because players don't know the total number of rounds, we won't see a clear end-game spike; instead, behavior stabilizes at a lower cooperation level.

Now the play-by-play transcript.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round every player privately has 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and shared equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins on punishment: each punishment unit costs the sender 3 coins and removes 7 coins from the target’s payoff. Because punishment is costly to the sender, players tend to use it sparingly, and punishment is typically directed at obvious low contributors (free-riders). Repetition and the possibility of future punishment create an incentive to contribute early, but as the horizon shortens and if some players persist in free-riding, contributions commonly drift down. Uncertainties include heterogeneous preferences (some players are more enforcement-oriented), mistakes, and how strongly individuals update after being punished.

Given the provided profiles, I predict a mix of steady cooperators (e.g., GORILLA, PARROT, MOOSE, OWL, RABBIT) who contribute relatively high amounts and occasionally punish low contributions, and persistent low contributors (e.g., SLOTH, CROCODILE, SNAKE, sometimes COW or CHICKEN). Punishments will be infrequent (1–2 units typically) and targeted; they will sometimes nudge low contributors up briefly, but fatigue and end-game effects will push average contributions down toward the final rounds. Punishment intensity will be limited because it is costly, and in the very last round it will be minimal.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits ten players who each get 20 coins per round and decide privately how much to contribute to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, so group benefit is maximized when everyone contributes, but individuals gain more by free-riding (keeping their private coins while still getting a share of others' contributions). Because players do not know how many rounds remain, conditional cooperation and reciprocity typically shape behavior: many players start by contributing moderately (testing others), conditional cooperators increase contributions if others do, and free-riders get targeted by costly punishment from those who want to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) and highly costly to targets (5 coins per unit), so punishment is used sparingly and strategically — usually to deter repeated low contributions rather than as widespread retaliation. Chat messages are typically few and purposeful (requests, explanations, or short moral appeals). Over the course of many rounds we expect an initial phase of mixed contributions, then a period of raised contributions as punishment and social norms stabilize cooperation, and finally some gradual unraveling toward lower contributions as uncertainty about the endgame and occasional retaliatory punishments creep in.

game_explanation:
This 16-round public goods game pits 13 players against the usual social dilemma: each round everyone can keep private coins or contribute them to a communal pot that is multiplied and split equally. The individual incentive is to keep coins (free-ride), while the group benefit is maximized when everyone contributes. Because the pot multiplier (8.1) is large relative to group size, full contributions benefit the group, but the private marginal return per coin is less than 1, so self-interested play favors low contributions.

Two mechanisms shape likely dynamics here:
- Social enforcement (punishment): Players can pay to punish others; punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) and very costly to the target (7 coins per unit). In practice this deters extreme free-riding and sustains moderate cooperation early-to-mid game, but punishment itself is used sparingly because it is expensive.
- Time horizon / endgame effects: Knowing there are 16 rounds encourages higher cooperation early, sustained by occasional punishment. As the end approaches, backward-induction and reduced expectation of future reciprocity typically reduce contributions; punishment also declines because it is less useful late in the game.

Given the player profiles and typical empirical behavior:
- Many educated/employed players (DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, SNAKE, COW, etc.) are likely to contribute at least moderately early on and punish a few low contributors.
- A few players with lower engagement or "slower" profiles (SLOTH, CROCODILE, CHICK) are more likely to start low or drift down, inviting occasional targeted punishment.
- Punishment is relatively targeted and sparse (1–3 units) and mainly applied to clear low contributors or repeat defectors. Retaliatory cycles are possible but limited because punishment is costly.
- Cooperation will be moderate-high for the first third to two-thirds of the game, then gradually decline toward the last few rounds; punishment frequency will fall late in the game.

Below is a round-by-round transcript consistent with these priors: contributions in avatar order, brief rationale each round, and the (sparse) punishment choices. Only non-zero punishment actions are listed.

game_explanation:
This is a finite (16-round) public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player decides privately how many of their 20 coins to place into a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally. Individually, each coin you contribute reduces your private holding by 1 but raises each player's share by 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623. Because 0.623 < 1, the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (keep your coin), while the social optimum is full contribution (everyone contributing 20). Typical empirical dynamics: many groups begin with moderate-to-high contributions driven by social preferences, fairness or uncertainty about others; as rounds progress and players infer others’ behavior from their own round payoffs, contributions often drift downward. The finite horizon predicts an end-game decline, with more defection in the final rounds. Heterogeneity among these 13 players (education, age, employment) suggests some will be more persistent cooperators (e.g., postgraduate-educated players or those who value norms) while others (part-time younger players, more "self-oriented") will cut contributions faster. Because players only observe their own net payoff each round (not others' individual contributions), updates are noisy: players infer aggregate cooperation from their payoff and adjust cautiously at first, then more sharply if payoffs are consistently low or inconsistent with expectations.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players decide with no prior-round feedback except their priors. Many will start from social heuristics or fairness norms. Postgraduate or more civic-minded profiles (CHICKEN, SNAKE, DUCK, OWL, COW) lean toward higher contributions. Younger, less attached players (SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT, CROCODILE) tend to start modestly. Expect a fairly cooperative but heterogeneous first round.

Each player now sees their net payoff and infers something about total contributions, but cannot see individual choices. Those who enjoyed a higher-than-expected payoff (suggesting others contributed) will stay or slightly increase; those with lower payoffs will reduce. Small downward drift begins.

Patterns are emerging. Some steady cooperators (DUCK, CHICKEN, SNAKE) keep contributing high amounts out of pro-social preference or habit. Others moderate further after inferring that private returns are better by keeping more coins. Expect gradual decrease in the group average.

Players begin to treat the game as a repeated interaction with imperfect feedback. Those who receive middling payoffs suspect partial free-riding and reduce contributions; persistent cooperators keep contributing but may start trimming. Small coordination failures appear.

Midway through the early phase, several players economize more. The expected private benefit of retaining coins is felt, so contributions drop further for reactive players. A core group of cooperators still contributes relatively more.

As players accumulate a few rounds of their own payoff history, beliefs solidify. Those who infer low aggregate cooperation cut faster. Some older or norm-driven players (CHICKEN, SNAKE, DUCK, OWL, COW) remain comparatively committed.

By this point, the average contribution is noticeably lower. Conditional cooperators who saw payoffs consistent with some others contributing may hold their level; purely self-interested or opportunistic players cut further toward zero.

Halfway through the game, end-game thinking begins to influence behavior for some (they anticipate future defection, so they defect earlier). Nevertheless a stable minority of cooperators persists, and some who wanted to signal fairness still contribute moderately.

Players who repeatedly observed modest payoffs now favor private holdings. Remaining cooperators trim contributions somewhat to avoid being exploited. The average continues to fall; a few keep contributing out of intrinsic motivation.
The approach of later rounds makes free-riding more attractive. Some players who maintained moderate contributions begin to reduce to a modest level; others nearly stop contributing entirely.

With only five rounds left, many players switch into "wait-and-take" mode, reducing contributions noticeably. A minority of consistent cooperators (e.g., SNAKE, CHICKEN, DUCK, OWL) still give nontrivial amounts, though reduced from the start.

End-game effects strengthen; the strategic benefit of contributing is clear (it hurts your own payoff), so many move toward zero. Some keep token contributions for fairness or to avoid feeling they gave up entirely.
Only a few rounds remain. Most players give very little or nothing. A small core continues to contribute at modest levels out of habit or intrinsic cooperation; strategic defections dominate.
With two rounds left, the incentive to free-ride is near-maximal; many contribute zero. Stubborn cooperators trim further but may still give 1–4 coins for normative reasons.
The penultimate round is close to the single-shot equilibrium: nearly all defect. A handful (those with strong cooperative norms or who misestimate others) contribute tiny positive amounts.

Final round: backward induction logic predicts everyone contributes zero; in practice a few people still give a token amount due to intrinsic preference or habit. Expect a near-universal collapse to 0 with a couple of small outliers.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment but unknown horizon. Each round players choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. That creates a strong group incentive to contribute (everyone benefits from others' contributions), but a private incentive to free-ride (keep your 20 and still receive a share of others' contributions). Costly punishment allows contributors to sanction low contributors: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and subtracts 5 coins from the punished player's round payoff. Because punishers are identified, punishment can enforce cooperation but can also provoke retaliation.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: many players try moderate-to-high contributions to signal cooperation. A few lower contributors test the waters (either strategic free-riders or constrained players), and are likely to be lightly punished by pro-social players (those with stronger norms or experience).
- Punishment will be used sparingly: enforcement tends to target clear low contributions (0–5), with 1–2 punishment units from one or two players rather than mass sanctioning.
- If punished, low contributors often increase contributions in subsequent rounds to avoid further cost; occasional retaliation can occur but usually remains small because punishment is costly to both sides.
- Over many rounds with punishment available and horizon unknown, cooperation tends to stabilize at moderately high contribution levels (well above zero), with occasional lapses that invite limited punishment.
- Chat messages will be sparse and typically normative (requests to contribute, short explanations, or brief threats/promises to punish).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated one-shot public goods game played for 16 known rounds. Each round players decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split evenly among the 13 players. The marginal private return on a coin contributed is about 0.62 — so contributing is socially efficient but privately costly. After payoff redistribution, players can spend costly punishment units (each unit costs the punisher 3 coins and removes 7 coins from the target).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show moderate cooperation as some players (especially those with more education or older profiles) lead by contributing relatively high amounts; others test the waters and may contribute less.
- Because punishment is available and identifiable, a few “prosocial enforcers” (those who care about fairness or long-term cooperation) will punish clear low contributors, but punishments are typically sparse because punishment itself is costly.
- Those punished tend to raise contributions in subsequent rounds; this stabilizes cooperative behavior in the middle rounds.
- Toward the known end of the game (final few rounds), the incentive to maintain cooperation weakens and some players reduce contributions; punishers may punish less because there is less future benefit from enforcement.
- Uncertainties: individual heterogeneity in motives (some may spitefully punish late, some may free-ride throughout, some may be conditional cooperators) means we’ll see occasional defections and correspondingly occasional punishments rather than a perfectly smooth pattern.

I will simulate a plausible sequence consistent with these tendencies: a modest start, some early targeted punishments that raise cooperation, stable midgame cooperation, and some end-game erosion of contributions. Punishments will be limited in number and typically directed at the lowest contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated, indefinite-length public goods game with 10 players. Each round each player privately chooses how much of 20 coins to contribute; the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. The private incentive is to free-ride (each coin you keep yields 1 to you, while each coin contributed returns 0.66 to you), while the group optimum is full contribution. Because players do not know the number of rounds in advance and can send messages, we expect the following likely dynamics:

- Early rounds: uncertainty about others produces mixed behavior. Some players (conditional cooperators) will propose moderate contributions (around 8–15) to test reciprocity; a few will signal trust by committing higher amounts or by messaging. A small subset (consistent free-riders) will contribute very little or zero.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators respond to observed pattern of payoffs and messages. If cooperation is rewarded by high group payoffs, contributions may stabilize at moderate levels; if a few free-ride persistently, cooperation tends to decay as conditional cooperators reduce contributions.
- Occasional recovery spurts: short bursts of coordinated cooperation can happen after persuasive messages from reliable cooperators, but these are fragile.
- Endgame uncertainty: because players do not know the horizon, there is no guaranteed final-round collapse; however fatigue and persistent free-riding usually cause gradual decline in contributions toward later rounds. A few players may attempt one-off generous moves to "reset" cooperation.

Chats are sparse and purposeful: proposals to "do 10 each," short complaints about free-riders, or simple announcements of one’s own contribution. Some players (e.g., RABBIT, OWL, GORILLA) have profiles suggesting they are more likely to contribute reliably; others (PARROT, SLOTH) are more likely to contribute little. The transcript below simulates plausible choices and sparse chat across 19 rounds using these priors.

- Early rounds: uncertainty about others produces mixed behavior. Some players (conditional cooperators) will propose moderate contributions (around 8–15) to test reciprocity; a few will signal trust by committing higher amounts or by messaging. A small subset (consistent free-riders) will contribute very little or zero.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators respond to observed pattern of payoffs and messages. If cooperation is rewarded by high group payoffs, contributions may stabilize at moderate levels; if a few free-ride persistently, cooperation tends to decay as conditional cooperators reduce contributions.
- Occasional recovery spurts: short bursts of coordinated cooperation can happen after persuasive messages from reliable cooperators, but these are fragile.
- Endgame uncertainty: because players do not know the horizon, there is no guaranteed final-round collapse; however fatigue and persistent free-riding usually cause gradual decline in contributions toward later rounds. A few players may attempt one-off generous moves to "reset" cooperation.

Chats are sparse and purposeful: proposals to "do 10 each," short complaints about free-riders, or simple announcements of one’s own contribution. Some players (e.g., RABBIT, OWL, GORILLA) have profiles suggesting they are more likely to contribute reliably; others (PARROT, SLOTH) are more likely to contribute little. The transcript below simulates plausible choices and sparse chat across 19 rounds using these priors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 anonymous players, 20 coins each round, and a group multiplier (6.6) producing a per-coin group return of 0.66 split equally. Each individual loses 1 coin from private holding for each coin contributed but gains 0.66 coins back from the group's pot for each coin contributed (i.e., a private net of -0.34 per coin contributed). That creates a tension: the socially optimal outcome is everyone contributing everything, but individually each coin contributed reduces one's private payoff, so free-riding is always privately tempting.

Typical dynamics here:
- Early rounds often start optimistic/moderate: some players test cooperation with mid-to-high contributions while a few try to free-ride.
- When free-riding is observed (via lower personal payoffs or verbal reports), conditional cooperators reduce their contributions and overall contributions fall.
- Occasional attempts to re-coordinate (public messages like "let's all contribute 20" or pledges) can produce short-lived rebounds if enough players follow, but sustained high cooperation is hard without enforceable commitments.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there isn't a predictable final-round collapse, so endgame defection is muted; however, gradual erosion of cooperation across rounds is still likely.
- Messaging will be sparse: some players (MOOSE, PARROT, DUCK) are likelier to encourage cooperation; others will stay silent or state their low ability to contribute (DOG, GORILLA).

I will simulate these tendencies across 19 rounds: an initial moderate-cooperation phase, a decline as some free-ride, occasional coordinated pushes to raise contributions, and overall fluctuating but generally below-full-cooperation contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and divided equally across all players. Individual incentives: each coin you contribute costs you 1 privately but returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to you through the public share, so the private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Group incentive: the group is best off if everyone contributes more, ideally all 20s. Uncertainty and information: players do not see others' decisions before choosing and after each round they only see their own net payoff (not others’ payoffs), so learning about others' behavior is imperfect and indirect. Given these incentives and limited feedback, I predict some initial moderate cooperation from socially inclined or conditional-cooperator profiles (e.g., MOOSE, RABBIT, COW, PARROT), while some players start low or quickly reduce contributions (e.g., SLOTH, CROCODILE). Over repeated rounds, contributions tend to decline as conditional cooperators respond to perceived free-riding; however some players maintain medium contributions through the middle rounds.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

Early learning continues. Those who lowered contributions and still received reasonable payoffs may further defect; consistent cooperators begin to test whether their contributions are exploited and may reduce somewhat.

Patterns of decline emerge as some players observe their private payoffs and adjust downward. A core of cooperators (postgrads, retirees, or more other-regarding profiles) still contribute moderately.

As average contributions fall, marginal return to contributing becomes clearer to players. Some who started high decrease their contributions gradually; persistent cooperators still give above-average amounts.
Trailing rounds show continued downward drift. Players balancing fairness and self-interest often cut back slowly rather than abruptly; clear free-riders keep contributions at or near zero.
With several rounds of feedback, many players converge toward low but not always zero contributions; a small set still gives moderately to the public good.
Further erosion occurs. Those who place value on reciprocity continue modest contributions; more selfish players stick closer to zero.
Approaching later rounds, some players conserve coins for private payoff as cooperation seems unrewarded; a handful still contribute a moderate amount out of consistent preference.
Endgame effects start to appear: many reduce further since future punishment or reciprocity is limited. Some “principled” players still give small positive amounts.
As the final third of rounds progresses, contributions mostly stabilize at low levels; remaining contributors decrease slowly but do not drop to zero all at once.
Few rounds left increases incentive to maximize private payoff; several participants give only token amounts or stop entirely, while a small group still contributes minimally.
With only a couple rounds remaining, most players prioritize private returns; some consistent cooperators keep low but nonzero contributions out of preference or habit.
Endgame logic dominates: many drop to zero; a few contributors still make small contributions either by habit or mild other-regarding preference.
Final round: no future consequences, so the individual incentive to free-ride is strongest. Expect the lowest average contributions of the game, with only a small minority contributing.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment. With 10 players and a multiplier of 6.6 (so each coin contributed yields 0.66 to each player), the social return on a contribution (0.66) is less than the private cost (1), so individual incentives favor free-riding. However, because players can punish after seeing contributions, punishment lets the group impose costs on low contributors to sustain higher cooperation. Early rounds are uncertain—some players will "test" by contributing less. When low contributions are visible, others may send small, targeted punishments (1–2 units) to enforce norms. Punishment is costly to send (2 coins per unit) and quite punitive to receive (5 coins per unit), so punishments are used sparingly. Messages will be sparse and mostly serve to coordinate expectations or call out low contributions. Over 19 rounds I expect cycles: initial moderate cooperation, occasional defections, targeted punishments that raise cooperation again, and a bit of retaliation but not a full-blown punishment war because punishment costs deter overuse.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round everyone privately decides how many of 20 coins to contribute to a public pot; each contributed coin is multiplied by 8.1 then split equally among all players, so the social return per coin is positive (total multiplier 8.1) but the private return is less than 1 (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 per coin). That creates a classic tension: contributing benefits the group but is individually costly. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish specific other players (each punishment unit costs 3 to the punisher and reduces the punished player's round payoff by 7). Punishment is costly but can enforce cooperation; it also allows retaliation and can spur cycles of revenge. Because players know the game length (16 rounds) and see who punished them, we should expect:

- Early rounds: uncertainty about norms. Many players inclined toward cooperation (college/postgrad profiles), so initial contributions will be moderately high (around 12–18), but some cautious or opportunistic players may contribute less. Some first-round punishments are possible in response to clear low contributions.
- Middle rounds (3–12): norms and enforcement settle. Players who deviate repeatedly are likely to be punished sporadically. Punishment is used sparingly in most empirical settings; expect a few targeted punishments each round rather than mass sanctioning. Retaliation can occur but is typically limited because punishment is costly.
- Late rounds (13–16): endgame effects appear. Some players start to free-ride more as future retaliation loses leverage; others try to sustain cooperation, and there may be a few last-round punishments directed at obvious defectors. Overall cooperation may drift down slightly toward the end unless punishment successfully maintained higher contributions.

Across 16 rounds I predict an overall pattern of relatively high but imperfect cooperation, occasional low contributions that trigger light punishment, some limited retaliation, and a modest decline in contributions in the final round.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and private endowments of 20 coins per round. Each round players choose how much (0–20) to contribute to a pot that is multiplied by 8.1 and divided equally among the 13 players. Individually, contributing reduces your private holding by 1 coin per unit contributed, while your contribution returns to you only as a fraction (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623) of each coin contributed — beneficial for the group but costly privately. Because players do not see others' current contributions and only observe their own round payoff at the summary stage, uncertainty about others' behavior is persistent. Typical dynamics: early rounds often see some cooperation or "testing" (moderate contributions) by more pro-social or optimistic players; others may free-ride or contribute less. Over rounds, if players infer from their own payoffs that others are contributing less, contributions tend to decline (conditional cooperation and the temptation to free-ride), producing a gradual breakdown of cooperation. Endgame effects (near round 16) often push many players to reduce contributions to zero because future reciprocity is impossible. Individual differences in patience, reciprocity, and social preferences (informed by the provided profiles) shape who leads or follows in raising or lowering contributions.

The trend toward minimal contributions strengthens. Some players contribute zero in anticipation of little reciprocal benefit; a few still contribute small positive amounts.

Final round: nearly every player contributes 0 since there's no future to sustain reciprocity. Everyone keeps their private endowment.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20-coin endowments each round. Every coin a player contributes is multiplied by 6.6 and the resulting pot is split equally among all 10 players. That means each coin a player contributes returns 0.66 to each player (including the contributor). The private cost of contributing a coin is 1, so the individual marginal return (0.66) is less than the private cost (1). The dominant selfish incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride), while the group-optimal outcome is for everyone to contribute 20.

Because players do not know how many rounds remain, classic experimental outcomes are likely: some early cooperation or conditional commitments (people testing or signaling cooperation), followed by gradual decline in contributions as free-riding appears. Communication is allowed, so occasional short messages (promises, encouragement, mild complaints) will shape behavior, but messages are cheap and unverifiable, so compliance will vary. Expect a mixture of steady cooperators (who keep contributing a lot), conditional cooperators (who reduce contributions if others free-ride), and persistent free-riders. Payoffs per round will reflect both the group's total contribution and individual choices; players only see their own round payoff at the summary stage, so public signals are limited to chat.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment and unknown horizon. Individually, each player gains only 0.66 coins per coin they contribute (6.6 total multiplied and split across 10), so the dominant single-round incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, groups can sustain cooperation if players punish low contributors—even though punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) because it imposes a bigger hit on the target (5 coins per unit). Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as players "test" each other; a few low contributions are likely. Norm-enforcers (players who appear more strategic or mature) will sometimes punish defectors to signal that free-riding won't be tolerated. That tends to raise average contributions over time, though occasional lapses and targeted retaliation/punishment can appear. Chats are typically sparse and short, often requests to cooperate or apologies after being punished. Punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher, concentrated on clear under-contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a 13-player, 16-round public goods game. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their 20-coin endowment. All contributions are pooled, multiplied by 8.1, and divided equally among the 13 players. The marginal per capita return (MPCR) is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 — each coin contributed returns about 0.623 coins to the contributing player through the public pot, less than the private cost of 1 coin. That creates a fundamental tension: socially optimal behavior is high (everyone contributing more increases total group payoff), but individual monetary incentives push toward free-riding (contribute 0).

Because players act anonymously, cannot communicate, and know the game has a fixed 16-round horizon, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: some players try contributing (conditional cooperators, social preferences, or curiosity). Contributions are often moderate.
- Middle rounds: reciprocation and conditional cooperation lead to short swings—cooperators reduce contributions after seeing low returns, and some people try to re-establish cooperation.
- Late rounds: backward-induction pressure and fading hope for future reciprocity generally push contributions down toward zero. In the very final rounds many players contribute little or nothing.

We use the provided player profiles as priors about likely tendencies (e.g., some with higher education or stable employment may be more inclined to contribute early; others may be more cautious), but anonymity and simple monetary incentives make free-riding increasingly attractive. At each round's summary stage players see their own net payoff for that round only (not peers'), but here the transcript records every player's round payoff for the reader.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with ten players, 20 coins each round. Each coin a player contributes increases the group pot by 6.6 coins total, but that total is shared equally among all ten players, so each coin contributed yields 0.66 to every player (including the contributor). Private return from contributing is -1 (you give up 1 coin) plus +0.66 from the shared benefit, so the individual net return is -0.34 per coin contributed. That means each player has a strong private incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) even though the group is better off when players contribute.

Given the heterogeneity in the profiles (age, occupation, education) and the fact that players do not know how many rounds will be played, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: players often "test" the waters with moderate contributions and occasional promises in chat. Conditional cooperators may contribute moderately (8–15), while more selfish players contribute little (0–3).
- Mid game: cooperation often drifts downward if free-riding appears. Sparse, strategic chat can sometimes partially restore contributions (appeals or small coordination).
- Late rounds: because players do not know when the game ends, there is not always a clear "last round" effect, but contributions often remain lower on average than early rounds and can fluctuate as some players try to re-start cooperation.
- Chat tends to be sparse and mostly consists of short appeals, promises, or complaints — it rarely enforces full cooperation.

I will simulate 19 rounds. For each round I show a short explanation of expected motivations, any short chat that occurs, the contribution vector in the exact avatar order, and the private round payoff each player sees (their own net payoff only).

game_explanation:
This is a finite 16-round public goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute; contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and shared equally, creating a tension between private incentive to free-ride and social incentive to contribute. Because punishment is costly to send (3 coins per punishment unit) but strongly hurts targets (7 coins per unit), we expect punishment to be used sparingly and mainly to discipline clear low contributors early on. Early rounds typically see moderate cooperation from conditional cooperators; a few low contributors attract isolated punishment. That can provoke limited retaliation, producing brief pockets of tit-for-tat in the middle rounds. As the finite horizon becomes salient, contributions normally decline toward the end (especially the last few rounds), and punishment fades because sending it is costly and there is little future to enforce. Overall: moderate contributions early, scattered punishments targeting persistent low contributors, some mild retaliation midgame, and progressive decline in contributions and punishment toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so group-minded players can increase everyone's payoffs by contributing. But private incentives favor free-riding: keeping coins privately gives the contributor the full marginal value, while contributions are shared. Punishment lets cooperators deter free-riding by imposing a costly sanction (punisher pays 3 per unit, target loses 7 per unit). Because punishment itself is costly, it is used sparingly in real groups — usually targeted at clear under-contributors early on to establish norms, then used less as those norms stabilize or as endgame incentives reduce enforcement.

Uncertainties: players do not see others' choices before acting, so early rounds feature belief formation and testing. Individuals with stronger pro-social priors (older, higher education, or those with history of cooperative behavior in similar settings) are more likely to contribute more; younger, less-committed, unemployed or lower-education profiles may be more likely to contribute less. Punishment decisions are strategic: some players will punish low contributors to sustain cooperation, but because punishment costs the punisher, it will be modest and concentrated on clear defectors. Toward the final rounds (especially last few rounds), we should expect a drift toward lower contributions as the shadow of future retaliation wanes; punishment may become rarer because it's costly and yields little future enforcement.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment and an unknown number of rounds. The private return on each coin contributed (0.66) is less than the private cost (1), so purely self-interested players have an incentive to free-ride. However, because contributions are visible after the decision and players can punish others (at a personal cost), cooperative norms often emerge: some players contribute more to increase group payoffs and punish low contributors to discourage free-riding. Punishment is costly to both parties, so it tends to be used sparingly and target persistent low contributors. Early rounds typically show heterogeneous contributions and occasional punitive responses to low offers; if punishment effectively deters free-riding, contributions rise and punishments decline. If punished players retaliate or punishers overuse sanctions, cycles of punishment can persist and reduce net payoffs. Chat messages are typically few and focus on encouragement, norm-setting, or short complaints about punishments.

Because players do not see others' contributions directly, they must infer group behavior from their own net payoff each round. Typical dynamics: moderate cooperation at the start (many players give a positive amount), followed by a gradual decline as self-interested players free-ride and conditional cooperators reduce contributions in response to inferred lower group contributions. A few players (socially-oriented or consistently cooperative types) tend to contribute more persistently; a few low contributors/free-riders stay low throughout. Toward the final rounds, anticipating the end of future reciprocity, overall contributions usually drop further.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, private endowments (20 coins each) per round, a group return (total contributions × 6.6) split equally, and a costly punishment stage (each punishment unit costs 2 to the punisher and removes 5 from the punished). Players do not know how many rounds remain, so there is incentive to build cooperative norms early and sustain them through conditional cooperation and occasional punishment of clear free-riders. Expect a mix of behavior:

- A core of consistently high contributors (those who value the group return or have strong conditional-cooperator tendencies) will contribute near or at 20 each round.
- Some players will explore or test the group by under-contributing early; they will be punished occasionally by the norm-enforcers, which should reduce repeat free-riding.
- Punishment will be used sparingly because it is costly to the punisher; most punishments will target clear low contributors (0–5) and occur after repeated low contributions or blatant free-riding.
- Chat will be sparse, used for exhortations ("please contribute"), short reproaches after defections, or thanks after cooperative rounds.
- Over time we should see increased cooperation as punishments discipline persistent low contributions, with occasional lapses.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, so every coin in the pot returns 0.66 to each player. That means a single coin costs a player 1 but yields 0.66 back to them personally (net -0.34), while benefiting the group. The one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0); however, visible payoffs and messages can encourage some cooperation early on. Because players only see their own round payoff (not others'), they infer group behavior indirectly from whether their payoff is high or low. Typically this leads to partial cooperation early (some players contribute moderately/high), then gradual erosion as free-riding is tempting and unverifiable promises can be cheap. Chat is allowed but unverifiable, so expect a few short pledges or calls for cooperation early, and sparse reactive comments later. Over 19 rounds I predict an initial cluster of contributions from several players, some low contributions from habitual free-riders, intermittent attempts to revive cooperation, and a gradual decay toward very low contributions in later rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game among 13 players. Each round every player gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution 0–20 to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across all 13 players. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62: each coin a player contributes returns about 0.62 to that same player through the public fund. Individually, contributing costs a player 1 coin but gives them only ≈0.62 back, so a purely selfish short-run incentive is to contribute 0. However, because MPCR>0.5 and the multiplier is large, group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes more — the social optimum is high contributions.

Players do not see others' contributions directly. After each round they only see their own net payoff for that round, from which they can infer the group's total contributions (since they know their own contribution). This limited feedback produces uncertainty: conditional cooperators will try to infer others' behavior from their own payoff, while free-riders may exploit high aggregate contributions. Over repeated rounds we expect an initial burst of cooperation from more pro-social or norm-sensitive players, followed by gradual decline as some players respond to incentives or exploit observed payoffs. Some players (those with stronger pro-social priors) tend to maintain moderate contributions; others (who are more opportunistic or unconvinced) reduce contributions. In later rounds, the threat of future punishment disappears, so contributions tend to fall further; a few players may nonetheless contribute on the final round out of principle.

All rounds below follow these dynamics: at the start of the round I give a brief round-level explanation, then the predicted integer contributions in the exact avatar order, wrapped exactly as required. After contributions each round the summary stage is shown to players (they see only their own net payoff).
