predicted_rounds.explanation: First round expectations: many educated or norm-sensitive players (postgraduates, college degrees, those not currently working who might be community-minded) typically start relatively cooperative without prior signals. Some players with lower trust in strangers or more opportunistic priors start low. Everyone only has priors now, so contributions cluster around moderate-to-high for cooperators and near-zero for a few free-riders.

Players saw their own large net payoffs (because the multiplier is large). Cooperative types infer that group contributions were high and mostly sustain contributions; some low contributors slightly increase to exploit a cooperative group; a few remain cautious or opportunistic and keep contributions low.

With two rounds of self-payoff feedback, conditional cooperators trim their contributions slightly if they see no personal advantage compared to keeping coins; some opportunists test slightly lower contributions to keep more private coins while still reaping the public return.

A gradual downward trend begins for several players as they balance private incentives with social return. Trustful players keep contributing at mid-high levels; a few lower-contributing players hold steady near zero to small values.

Some players who previously gave more trim contributions to test whether others will sustain cooperation; those who kept contributions very low earlier see high net payoffs and remain incentivized to free-ride or slightly increase to exploit the public pot.

As the pattern of mixed contributions consolidates, average group contributions fall. Conditional cooperators gradually reduce, while a core of pro-social players still contribute moderately. Free-riders continue to contribute little.

Mid-game, the downward trend continues. Some players with low initial contributions go to zero, confident they can keep more private coins while still receiving a share of the pot. More cooperative players keep contributing but at reduced levels.

With several rounds of payoff-only feedback, players form beliefs about average group behavior. Many reduce contributions further; a few maintain modest positive contributions out of principle or habit.

Decline in contributions persists. Those who still give are often the most norm-sensitive or altruistic; opportunists hold at zero or very low amounts. The expectation of future rounds briefly slows declines for some, but the trend is downward overall.
Approaching the final third of the game, strategic players foresee dwindling cooperation and cut contributions further. A small core continues modest giving; several players remain at zero.
Diminishing cooperation and the lack of direct information about others (beyond own payoff) produce continued reductions. Some players who previously contributed moderately drop to low single digits.
Near the endgame, many players shift toward keeping more private coins. A few persistent cooperators still give small positive amounts (morality or habit).

As the final rounds loom, forward-looking players recognize limited scope for future enforcement — contributions decline further. Some players reduce to near-zero; a modest group of steady cooperators remains.
With two rounds remaining after this one, many patients switch definitively to low contributions. A socially-minded minority keeps contributing small amounts; opportunists stick to zero.

Second-to-last round: expectations of future reciprocity are minimal for many players, so contributions stay low. A slight number of players keep small, steady contributions either out of habit or intrinsic preference.

Final round: classical game-theoretic prediction is everyone contributes zero since no future interactions remain. In practice some players still contribute small amounts out of personal norms or to "finish on a good note." Expect a mixed pattern with several zeros and a few small-to-moderate contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a fairly large group multiplier (total contributions × 8.1) that is shared equally. Because the multiplier is split among 13 people, each coin contributed returns about 0.623 coins to the contributor (8.1 / 13 ≈ 0.623). That means contributing is socially beneficial (raises group payoff) but individually costly each round, so the one-shot incentive is to contribute 0.

Typical dynamics in such experiments: many groups start with moderate-to-high contributions (people try to cooperate), then contributions decline as free‑riding is discovered. Costly punishment can sustain cooperation by deterring free‑riding, but punishment itself is costly (3 coins per unit to the punisher, 7 coins removed from the target), so players punish sparingly. Punishment is more likely to appear early or mid-game to enforce norms; as the end of the known horizon approaches, punishment frequency usually falls and contributions tend toward defection.

Uncertainties and behavioral priors used here:
- Some players (by profile or name) are modeled as more inclined to cooperate; others are more likely to free-ride. These are only soft priors.
- Punishment will appear selectively against repeated or very low contributors, but it will be limited because it is costly for punishers.
- Near the final rounds, incentives to punish are weaker and contributions generally fall further.

Below I simulate a plausible 16-round transcript following these expectations.

Each coin contributed benefits the group (multiplied by 6.6, so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to every player), but contributing is individually costly (-1 versus +0.66). game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a high group multiplier (8.1) split equally. Individually, each coin you keep gives you +1, while each coin you contribute returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to you — so contributing is privately costly but socially beneficial. Two broad tendencies will shape play:

- Self-interest and backward induction: the one-shot dominant action is to contribute 0. Over repeated rounds some players will defect early or drift to low contributions, especially toward the final rounds (end-game effect).
- Conditional cooperation and learning: many players start with positive contributions expecting reciprocity. Because players only observe their own net payoff (not others' individual contributions), they infer group behavior from that payoff. If the inferred group contribution is low, conditional cooperators reduce their contributions; if the inferred group contribution is reasonably high, they may sustain or modestly increase contributions. Private-payoff feedback makes beliefs noisy and heterogeneous: different players will interpret the same private payoff slightly differently based on their own contribution and prior expectations.

Uncertainties: players differ in social preferences and responsiveness. We use the provided profiles as priors: some (DUCK, MOOSE, CHICK, OWL) are more likely to be steady contributors; others (SNAKE, RABBIT, some younger experimenters) are more likely to free-ride or experiment. Over rounds 1–5 we expect an initial mix of moderate contributions, then a drifting down as people detect free-riding, occasional rebound attempts from cooperative types, and a clear decline in the last few rounds as end-game free-riding dominates.

Transcript below simulates these dynamics round-by-round, showing a short explanation at each round start, the chosen contributions in the required order, and each player's private net payoff at the summary stage.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment and a known finite horizon (16 rounds). Each round players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and shared equally, so contributions create strong group returns but individual incentives favor free-riding. Because punishment is costly (punisher pays 3 per unit, target loses 7 per unit), punishment can sustain cooperation by deterring low contributions, but it is expensive and may be used sparingly. Players observe only contributions when punishments are assigned and learn who punished them, enabling targeted retaliation. Typical dynamics: some initial uncertainty and mixed contributions, a few norm-enforcers punish low contributors early, cooperation rises as enforcement takes hold, and toward the final rounds contributions tend to decay (end-game effect) while punishment activity often drops because future enforcement benefits vanish and punishment is costly. Given the player priors, expect a handful of consistent punishers (to enforce norms) and generally rising cooperation mid-game, followed by decline in the last several rounds. Punishment is used sparingly — occasional single- or two-unit sanctions against clear low contributors or repeat offenders.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual short-term incentives (keeping your 20 coins) against a group benefit: each coin contributed is multiplied by 6.6 and the total is shared equally among the 10 players, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each player. Rational self-interest suggests contributing 0, but conditional cooperators and costly punishment (2 coins per punishment unit for the punisher, which removes 5 coins per unit from the punished player) can sustain higher contributions. Because players don't know the number of rounds, cooperation is more likely to be stable (people don't "save" defections for a known final round). Expect an early mix of moderate contributions with a few low contributors; visible punishment of clear free-riders will be used sparingly to enforce norms. Over rounds we should see contributions drift upward toward high cooperation, punctuated by occasional defections that attract selective punishment. Chat will be infrequent and usually short calls to cooperate or short explanations/apologies after being punished.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits ten players against a standard collective-action problem: each round everyone gets 20 coins and decides privately how many to contribute to a shared pot. The pot gets multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, so an individual’s private return from contributing 1 coin is 0.66 to each player (including themselves), i.e. a private return of 0.66 per contributed coin. Because 0.66 < 1, the dominant short-run incentive is to keep coins (free-ride) while hoping others contribute. However, players who care about group outcome or reciprocity can sustain higher contributions early on.

Key likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds tend to show moderate cooperation (people testing norms, some conditional cooperators contributing modest amounts). Expect mixed contributions at first (some high, some moderate).
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, the “end-game” unraveling will be gradual rather than an immediate collapse. Over time, conditional cooperators respond to perceived free-riding and contributions generally drift downward.
- Sparse chat can help sustain cooperation if credible commitments appear (e.g., a few players saying they will cooperate). But without binding agreements or punishment, messages only partially slow decline.
- Individual differences: some players (older, degree-holders) may be more cooperative on average; others (or those labeled with names that suggest low engagement) may contribute less. Expect a mixture that produces a gradual decline with occasional short-lived rallies if players encourage each other.
- Uncertainty: each player only sees their own payoff at summary, not others’ payoffs, so beliefs about others’ incentives are imperfect and based mainly on observed contributions (which are public each round in this transcript). This reinforces conditional cooperation or reciprocal decline depending on the trend.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with ten players. Each round every player has 20 private coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and divided equally among all ten. Because each coin contributed yields 0.66 back to its giver (6.6/10) but costs 1 coin, the individual incentive is to contribute 0 (dominant strategy) while the group payoff is maximized if everyone contributes 20. In practice, behavior usually starts mixed — some try to cooperate, some test others by free-riding — and then evolves as players learn about aggregate behavior via their own round payoffs (they can infer total contributions from their own payoff and contribution). Messages can help coordinate or punish, but claims may be unverified. Expect early experimentation and some cooperation from motivated players (pledges or higher contributions), followed by cycles of reciprocation and withdrawal as free-riding is detected. Because players do not know the horizon, end-game effects are uncertain; some will attempt late pushes to restore cooperation, others will defect.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> COW, SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and an efficient multiplier (8.1) that nevertheless leaves a private incentive to free-ride (each coin kept yields 1 to the player, each coin in the pot yields about 0.62 to each player). Typical dynamics: some players initially contribute moderately (conditional cooperators), some contribute little (free-riders). Punishment is possible but costly to the punisher, so it tends to be used sparingly and targeted at persistent low contributors to sustain cooperation early and mid-game. Over time, and especially near the final rounds, contributions commonly decline as the future enforcement value falls. Expect early-to-mid rounds of moderate cooperation sustained by a few targeted punishments against repeat low contributors (especially the smallest contributors like SNAKE, DUCK, CROCODILE), then gradual unraveling toward the end as players anticipate no future consequences.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, each receiving 20 coins each round and choosing an integer contribution 0–20 into a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and then split equally among all players. The private incentive is to contribute 0 (each coin you keep gives you 1 coin back, while each coin you contribute yields only 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 back to you personally), so purely selfish, risk-neutral payoff-maximizers should free-ride and contribute 0 each round. The group-optimal outcome is for everyone to contribute all 20.

Because players do not see others' contributions before deciding and only see their own round net payoff afterward, uncertainty is substantial. Typical experimental dynamics: many people start with positive contributions (conditional cooperators test cooperation), then contributions decline over rounds as free-riding appears and there are no communication or punishment channels to enforce cooperation. A few players often remain steadily cooperative. With a known finite horizon of 16 rounds, there is sometimes an end-game drop in contributions toward the final rounds.

Given the player profiles as priors, expect a mix: a few consistently pro-social contributors (e.g., PARROT, MOOSE, OWL, CHICK) who tend to sustain higher contributions; several conditional cooperators who mirror recent success but taper off; and some low contributors/free-riders (e.g., SLOTH, DOG, COW) who contribute little. Overall, contributions decline over the 16 rounds, with public payouts high early (when total contributions are higher), then falling as contributions drop.

game_explanation:
This is a classic voluntary contribution public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins endowment each round, and a group multiplier (total contributions × 6.6) that is split equally. Because each coin contributed returns 0.66 coins to each player (6.6/10), a single coin benefits the group more than the individual (social return) but the private marginal return is 0.66 < 1. So the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Nevertheless, early rounds often show some initial cooperation driven by trust, social preferences, or leadership. Over time, without binding agreements and with only private feedback (players only see their own net payoff each round), cooperation typically decays: people who reduce contributions get a slightly higher private return when others still give, so free-riding spreads. Expect a few players (natural cooperators or leaders) to try to sustain contributions by messaging or small persistent contributions; most others will drift toward 0. Chat will be sparse and used to encourage or to express disappointment; because players cannot see peers' contributions directly, beliefs about others will be noisy and reactions will be gradual.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a multiplier of 8.1 on the group pot. Individually, each coin you contribute returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to you via the shared pot, which is less than 1. That means the dominant private incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride) each round, even though the group as a whole is better off when many contribute. Typical dynamics: some players start fairly cooperative (fair-split or altruistic), others start low or at zero; because players do not observe others' contributions and only see their own net payoff after each round, social learning is noisy and coordination is hard. Expect early moderate contributions from conditional cooperators and a subset of consistent contributors, followed by a gradual decline toward low contributions as free-riding pays off or as uncertain feedback discourages cooperation. A few players are likely to remain relatively generous (age/education profiles suggest committed cooperators), while others free-ride or contribute very little throughout.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment and anonymous contributions but identifiable punishers. Typical dynamics: some players (likely those with higher education and full-time employment) will tend to contribute more, while a few players may try to free-ride. Because punishment is available and identifies who punished whom, early rounds often see a norm-setting phase where cooperators punish low contributions sparsely to discourage free-riding. That tends to raise average contributions over a few rounds. Occasional retaliatory punishments can create short-lived cycles of targeted punishment, but because punishment is costly to the punisher, these cycles usually die out and cooperation stabilizes at a moderate-to-high level. Chat messages are typically few and used to encourage, warn, or apologize; punishments are used sparingly and mainly aimed at persistent low contributors or perceived unfair punishers.

Given the player profiles, I expect GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT, MOOSE, and OWL to be relatively cooperative (higher contributions and occasional enforcement). SLOTH, DOG, RABBIT, and CHICK may start lower and adjust in response to punishment. CHICKEN may be moderately cooperative but not extreme. Over 19 rounds we’ll likely see an initial enforcement phase, some retaliation, and then a relatively stable pattern of mostly high contributions with occasional low outliers being punished.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally. Because 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 < 1, each coin a player contributes returns less than one coin to them personally (so purely selfish incentives favor contributing 0). However the multiplier is large enough that the group payoff is much higher if many people contribute. Without chat and with only private round payoff feedback (players see only their own net payoff), players cannot directly observe how others behaved and therefore cannot condition their own decisions on precise peer contributions. Expect the following likely dynamics:
- Initial rounds: many players start at moderate contributions out of fairness, social preference, or heuristic (e.g., “give half” or “give a lot”). Some players with lower motivation or tighter budgets (or who are more selfish) start low.
- Middle rounds: because players cannot see others’ contributions, conditional cooperators cannot easily reciprocate; some players respond to their private net-payoff signal by reducing contributions slowly. Overall contributions slowly drift downward.
- Endgame (last few rounds): the well-known finite-horizon incentive to free-ride intensifies and contributions decline markedly; many players cut contributions to near zero.
- Heterogeneity: a core of consistently pro-social players (e.g., profiles suggesting higher education, retirees, or non-work roles who may prioritize norms) will keep contributing relatively higher amounts throughout, while others (younger/unemployed or “sloth” types) will remain low.

Given the player profiles, I predict a moderate-to-high start with gradual decline and a sharper drop toward the final rounds. Below I simulate each round, give a short round-specific explanation, show the contributions in the exact avatar order, and then show what each player would see as their own net payoff for that round.

game_explanation:
This is a standard linear public goods game with 13 players each given 20 coins per round. Any coin a player contributes to the public pot is multiplied by 8.1 and then split equally among all 13 players. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623. That means every coin you contribute benefits the group (it produces more total payoff than kept), but the private return to the contributor is less than the coin cost (you get ≈0.623 back for each coin you contribute). Individually, the dominant strategy in a one-shot decision is to contribute 0; collectively, the group would be best off if everyone contributed their entire endowment.

Because the game repeats for 16 rounds and players can punish others at a cost (3 coins per punishment unit, with 7 coins deducted from the punished player per unit), we can expect conditional cooperation and some targeted punishment early and mid-game to sustain contributions. Punishment is costly to the punisher and thus typically used sparingly and targeted at obvious low contributors/free-riders. Uncertainty about others' preferences, noise in choices, and heterogeneous priors (different ages, employment, education) make initial behavior varied: some players will start moderately cooperative, others will free-ride. Over the middle rounds, cooperation tends to rise if punishment is credible; in the final rounds, forward-looking players anticipate the end and contributions typically decay (endgame effect), reducing the incentive to punish because enforcement yields no future benefit.

Given the profiles, expect a mix of moderate-to-high early contributions from more cooperative or professionally/socially invested players (those with postgraduate education or older players) and lower contributions from some younger/unemployed or less-engaged players. Punishments will be occasional, small (1–2 units), targeted primarily at repeated low contributors, and taper off toward the final rounds.

game_explanation: This is a 16-round public goods game with costly punishment. Each round, players choose how much of their private 20-coin endowment to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across the 13 players. The private marginal return per coin (8.1/13 ≈ 0.62) is less than 1, so the individual incentive is to free-ride, while the group interest is to contribute. Because punishment is possible (costly to the punisher and harmful to the punished), players who care about group outcomes or fairness may punish low contributors to enforce cooperation, but punishment itself is costly and can provoke retaliation. Typical dynamics: initial contributions are moderately high (people test cooperation), a few low contributors may be targeted by sparse punishment early on, which can raise contributions from offenders; over time, contributions often drift downward as enforcement fatigues and players anticipate end-game behavior. Punishment is used sparingly in most real experiments; here I predict occasional single-unit punishments aimed at persistent low contributors (or retaliations), not continuous heavy sanctioning.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-player repeated public goods game with costly punishment. Each round, players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute; contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to its contributor (a private loss of 0.34) but benefits the group. Because players don't know the total number of rounds, they face persistent uncertainty about whether cooperation or defection will be rewarded in future rounds. Costly punishment (2 coins per unit to punish, 5 coins deducted per unit from the target) provides a tool to discipline low contributors, but it itself is costly and may be used sparingly.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: heterogeneous contributions as players test others — many moderate contributors (10–15) and a few low contributors (0–5).
- Punishment will be used selectively and sparsely to target consistent low contributors; a few players who begin low (or sporadically defect) will be lightly sanctioned by several peers.
- After initial punishment, punished players tend to raise contributions to avoid further penalties; cooperation gradually increases toward a high but not perfect level.
- Occasional lapses (fatigue, experimentation) produce intermittent punishments later on; retaliation cycles are possible but limited because punishment is expensive.
- Chat is infrequent and purposeful: brief greetings, requests to contribute, and a few apologies or expressions of sanctioning intent.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game where each player gets 20 coins each round and decides privately how many to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among all ten players, so each coin contributed yields a social return of 6.6/10 = 0.66 coins to the contributor. Because 0.66 < 1, the individual incentive is to keep coins (contribute 0), while the group optimum is that everyone contributes the full endowment. Typical dynamics in such settings: some players start by contributing a moderate amount (conditional cooperators), while others (free-riders or self-interested players) contribute very little. Early rounds often show partial cooperation, sometimes sustained by short messages or promises. Over many rounds, without enforcement, cooperation tends to decay as free-riding is rewarded; however, occasional communication or strong conditional cooperators can slow the decline. Uncertainty about the number of rounds typically encourages somewhat more cooperation early on compared to a known finite horizon, but the overall tendency across 19 rounds will likely be a gradual decline in average contributions, with occasional brief rebounds if players appeal to reciprocity or shame free-riders in chat.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players who each choose either to contribute their entire endowment (20) or keep it (0) each round. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so a single contributor benefits the group but individually loses out relative to a lone free-rider in the short run. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, reputation and conditional strategies can sustain cooperation: some players will contribute to signal cooperation or because they value fairness; others may free-ride early to test tolerance. The punishment stage provides a costly enforcement mechanism: sending a punishment unit costs the punisher 2 and reduces the target by 5, so punishments are used sparingly to deter repeated free-riding or to retaliate. Expect a mix: a core of players (those with stable employment and higher education in these profiles) will tend toward cooperation and occasional punishment to enforce norms; a few players (or those with “sloth” or testing tendencies) will defect intermittently and be lightly punished until they conform. Chat will appear occasionally to coordinate norms, threaten punishment, or complain after being punished.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with a multiplier < 6, so individually it's always profitable to free-ride (each coin contributed returns ≈0.367 to the contributor). However, because there is an explicit reward stage and one interpersonal communication channel, there's scope for conditional cooperation: some players may contribute in round 1 to signal goodwill and use rewards to reinforce cooperation. With only two rounds, we expect some initial cooperation from people who are prosocial or who want to signal (older or more educated profiles often contribute more), but a modest decline by round 2 because the endgame reduces future benefits from building reputation. Rewards will be used sparingly — mostly to reward higher contributors or to reciprocate a reward — because each reward unit is relatively costly (4 coins). There's some uncertainty: a few players may free-ride from the start (especially a profile labeled SLOTH), and because reward receipts are anonymous, it's harder to target praise/punishment precisely; that tends to dampen heavy use of rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with six players. Each round every player privately chooses how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among all six players. Individually, each coin you contribute returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to you, so contributing is costly for the individual even though it raises total group payoff. After redistribution, players may spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 3 coins. Rewards are costly to send and only partially transfers value, but they can be used sparingly to recognize contributors and encourage cooperation in the second and final round.

Given the small group and only two rounds, I expect:
- Some players with cooperative priors (education, steady employment) will contribute moderately in round 1 to signal cooperation (and hope for others to reciprocate in round 2).
- A few players may contribute little or nothing (free-riders), exploiting the fact that individual payoff from contributing is negative.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to acknowledge relatively high contributors after round 1, mainly by other contributors who can afford the cost and hope to sustain cooperation into round 2.
- In round 2, players update: those who were rewarded and saw reciprocation tend to keep or increase contributions; those who were unrewarded or saw low returns may reduce contributions. Overall, cooperation may increase slightly among reciprocating pairs and remain low among persistent free-riders.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players over 19 rounds. Each round every player decides how much of the 20 coins to keep privately and how much to leave in the shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and divided equally. After the public good payoff is distributed, players can spend coins to reward others (2 coins cost to sender = 2 coins added to recipient per reward unit). Players see only their own round summary (their net payoff, how much they spent rewarding, and how much they received), so reputational effects arise from messages and visible rewards when they happen.

Likely dynamics: early rounds typically show some cooperation from players inclined toward pro-social behavior (higher contributions from DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT), while a few players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG) may contribute less. Small positive rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperators; as free-riding persists, conditional cooperators reduce their contributions and the group average drifts downward. Chat messages will be relatively rare: occasional requests to cooperate, short complaints about free-riding, and occasional praise when someone gives a reward. Because the horizon is finite, contributions tend to decline over rounds; a small uptick is possible in the final round as some players contribute as a one-shot fairness or final-signal.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard binary public goods game: each round every player chooses either to contribute their entire endowment (20) to the group pot or keep it. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly among the six players. Individually, defecting (keeping 20) always yields a higher immediate private payoff than contributing, because you still receive the shared return while keeping your private 20. That creates a strong incentive to free-ride. However, some players are conditional cooperators or motivated by social preferences — they may contribute early to try to establish cooperation. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding, messages and observed round payoffs become the only signals. Observing net payoffs after a round tells players how well they did, and they can often infer whether they were exploited (they contributed but did worse than defectors). Typical dynamics: a few players try to lead by contributing in early rounds, free-riders exploit them and gain higher round payoffs, contributors respond by reducing contributions in subsequent rounds, and overall cooperation tends to decline unless sustained coordination through repeated promises/messages emerges. Because players do not know how many rounds they will play, there is no deterministic final-round unraveling; instead, cooperation may persist sporadically or collapse into repeated free-riding. Expect sparse, purposeful chat (offers to coordinate, short complaints or commitments), occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation, and an overall downward drift in contributions with intermittent recoveries.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game where each player starts with 20 coins in the "shared pot" and chooses how many coins to withdraw for private use; the remainder is their contribution. The group contribution is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally across the 6 players (each player's share = 0.3 × total contribution). After payoffs from the public pot are revealed, players can punish (cost 2 to sender, -3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target per unit). Messages can be posted at any time. Players know the horizon (19 rounds), which tends to produce relatively high cooperation early on and a gradual decline as the end approaches; punishments and rewards are typically used sparingly to signal norms or retaliate against obvious free-riders. Given the profiles, I expect: moderate-to-high early contributions (some conditional cooperators like DOG, CHICKEN, PARROT keep contributing), occasional targeted punishments of low contributors (mostly single-unit punishments rather than heavy retaliation), occasional small rewards for consistent contributors (often from PARROT or DOG). Over the middle and late rounds contributions fall steadily (finite horizon effect), with only sparse sanctioning. Chats are likely short — encouragements, mild reprimands, or explanations.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with optional costly positive rewards. Individually, each coin you keep gives you 1, while each coin you contribute returns 2.2 to the group and is split equally (2.2/6 ≈ 0.3667 back to each player). That creates the classic tension: contributing benefits the group more than a single contributor (group benefit) but is costly to the individual (private incentive to free-ride). Because there are only two rounds, a full backward-induction logic predicts declining contributions (especially in round 2), but many real players are conditional cooperators: they contribute a moderate amount in round 1 and adjust in round 2 based on others' behavior and rewards.

Rewards are costly: each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives 3 coins to the target, so rewards are net-inefficient (they reduce total wealth slightly) but can be used to signal appreciation or encourage cooperation. Because rewards are anonymous to recipients (players cannot identify who rewarded them), rewards won't create direct tit-for-tat back-and-forth tied to identities, but they still serve as group-level signals and can influence behavior in round 2.

Given the player profiles (mix of ages, education and employment), I expect modest-to-moderate initial contributions in round 1, some targeted small positive rewards from those who value cooperation, sparse chat (a few promises or thank-yous), and adjustments in round 2: cooperators who were rewarded will tend to maintain or increase contributions; unrewarded cooperators may reduce theirs; low contributors may either increase a bit if convinced by group, or stay low.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This repeated public goods game gives each player a binary choice each round: keep 20 privately or contribute 20 to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players. Because contributors forgo the private 20 but still receive the same split of the pot as non-contributors, there is a strong individual incentive to free-ride (keep the 20) while hoping others contribute. Repeated interaction without a known end can allow cooperation to emerge if some players value reciprocity or reputation; conversely, a few early free-rides often trigger a downward spiral of defections. Messages can be used to signal intentions or shame/explain behavior, but with sparse, costly signaling and binary choices, the most likely pattern is: some initial cooperation (especially from older/retired players who may be more prosocial), a mix of exploitation and retaliation that reduces contributions over several rounds, occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation through messages and a contribution or two, and an endgame move toward defection once players suspect the game is ending (since rounds are unknown, endgame unraveling may be delayed). I'll simulate a plausible path: modest initial cooperation, some free-riding, partial collapse, a couple of recovery attempts, a temporary return to high cooperation, then partial unraveling and final defection.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with costly punishment and costly reward. Because the pot multiplier (2.2) yields a group return greater than individual retention (2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 per coin contributed), the group is better off if players contribute, but each individual has an immediate incentive to free-ride (keep their 20 rather than contribute). With only two rounds, strategic considerations matter: players who expect future retaliation or reciprocity may contribute more in round 1 to avoid punishment or to build cooperation; in round 2 (the final round) there is less incentive to invest in reputational signaling — but past punishment/reward can still change behavior if players expect direct retaliation or feel social norms.

Given the provided profiles, I expect a mix of moderate-to-high contributors from the more risk-averse / prosocial profiles (PARROT, DUCK, SLOTH) and lower initial contributions from those less invested or with weaker incentives (GORILLA, DOG). Punishment and reward will be sparse: players punish clear low contributors (to enforce norms) and occasionally reward high contributors. Because punishments are costly to the punisher and especially costly to the punished (4 coins cost to punisher per unit, -10 to target), expect only a few single-unit punishments; rewards (costly as well) will also be small and used occasionally to encourage cooperators. After round 1 punishments, some low contributors should increase their contributions in round 2 to avoid further sanctions, while punishers may slightly reduce contributions because of sanction costs.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with a costly punishment/reward stage after contributions. Players get 20 coins each round and decide privately how much to contribute; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Because the game is short (only 2 rounds), incentives are mixed: some players may try to free-ride (especially early), knowing cooperation can't be enforced by future retaliation beyond one more round, while others may still contribute to boost group payoffs or to signal fairness. The punishment option is harsh (target loses 10 per punishment unit, while punisher pays 4), so punishment is typically used sparingly and mainly to discipline clear free-riders. Rewards are costly to the giver (4) but give a smaller benefit to the recipient (+3), so rewards are also used infrequently, usually to acknowledge good contributors.

Given the player profiles, I expect a mix of cooperation and some low contributions. "SLOTH" is likely to contribute little; "DUCK" (younger, part-time) may initially free-ride but respond to punishment; middle-aged, employed players (CHICKEN, DOG, GORILLA, PARROT) will tend toward moderate-to-high contributions. Punishments will be targeted at obvious zero or very low contributors and will be limited to one or two single-unit punishments. Rewards will be sparse and used to acknowledge higher contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players and 19 rounds. In each round every player can withdraw any number of coins (0–20); the remainder is contributed to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed increases the group pot by 1.8 but gives any individual only 0.3 back (1.8/6). That means the private incentive is to withdraw (keep the coin) rather than contribute, while the group optimum would be for players to contribute more. Because players see only their own round payoffs and reward flows (not others' contributions directly) there is uncertainty about others' motives; players must infer behaviour from how their own payoff changes and from occasional chat and rewards.

Rewards are costly but selective: sending 1 reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. That allows targeted reciprocity or encouragement but is itself costly. In practice, I expect:
- One player (SLOTH) will act more selfishly — low contributions from early on — because their profile suggests lower effort/engagement.
- A few players (PARROT, GORILLA, CHICKEN) will try to support cooperation, occasionally giving small rewards to signal or thank cooperators.
- Rewards will be sparse and small (usually 1 unit) because they cost the sender real money and are less common online.
- Contributions will start moderate, then drift down as players observe limited enforcement and anticipate endgame defection; occasional small rewards will temporarily stabilize cooperation, but overall contributions tend to decline across the 19 rounds and collapse toward zero near the end.

Below I simulate the full 19-round transcript consistent with those incentives and uncertainties.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated (19-round) public goods game with six players. Each round players decide how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) versus withdraw privately. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After payoffs are revealed, players can punish (costly to both sides) or reward (costly to sender, beneficial to recipient). Key dynamics to expect:

- Conditional cooperation: many players will contribute fairly high amounts early on to build the public good; others may experiment with low contributions to free-ride.
- Social control emerges: occasional punishments will be used sparingly to discipline clear free-riders; occasional rewards will be used to reinforce cooperators. Because punishments are costly to the punisher, they are infrequent and typically small (single-unit punishments).
- Communication is sparse but meaningful: short messages (requests for cooperation, brief apologies, thanks) will appear and influence behavior.
- Over 19 rounds we expect some fluctuations (a few defections and corrective punishments) followed by relative stabilization: players learn who tends to contribute and use small punishments/rewards to maintain cooperation. Final rounds will show more cooperative contributions and a few parting messages.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players against a standard tension: contributing 20 coins to the public pot increases everyone's payoff (the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally), but contributing is individually costly compared with free-riding. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, cooperation may sustain longer than in a fixed-horizon game, but persistent free-riders can earn higher immediate payoffs unless others punish them. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and costly to the punished (5 coins per unit), so punishment will be used sparingly and targeted at repeated non-contributors. Players with stronger norms or greater resources (postgraduates, employed) are more likely to push for cooperation and to use punishment occasionally; unemployed or opportunistic players may defect early but can be deterred if punishments are consistent. Chat is scarce but can be used to request cooperation, signal intent to punish, or offer apologies. Over 12 rounds I predict many rounds of majority cooperation, a small set of repeated free-riding by one or two players early on (with targeted punishment from 1–2 players), and gradual convergence toward full cooperation by the middle or late rounds as punishment and social pressure take effect.

Individually, players maximize immediate payoff by contributing 0 (because each coin returned to them is worth less than 1), but the group is better off when many contribute. Because players get to punish or reward after seeing contributions, we expect some social enforcement: cooperators may reward each other and punish clear free-riders, especially after the first round. In a two-round game with punishment, players often contribute moderately in round 1 (testing who is cooperative), and contributions typically fall in round 2 (final-round defection), though targeted punishment in round 1 can push a punished low contributor to increase their contribution in round 2. Punishment tends to be sparse because it is costly to the punisher; rewards are also used sparingly.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players who each choose either to contribute their entire endowment (20) or nothing (0) each round. The group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns about 0.6167 to each player. Individually, contributing is costly (you lose 20 privately but gain ~12.33 back per 20 contributed), so a one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Over repeated rounds players who value group outcomes, reciprocity, or reputation may contribute early; others will exploit those contributions. Because players do not know how many rounds will be played, we can expect some initial cooperation from pro-social or conditional cooperators (especially those with postgraduate education or demonstrated pro-social priors), followed by erosion of cooperation if free-riding pays off. Sparse messaging may include pledges to contribute, requests to coordinate, and occasional attempts to re-start cooperation; most rounds will have few messages. Expect an early cluster of contributions, then mostly defection, with occasional short-lived attempts to revive cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each round each player chooses an integer contribution (0–20); the common pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, then players may spend resources to reward others (each reward unit costs 2 to the giver and gives 2 to the recipient). Players cannot see peers' choices before acting, and reward givers are anonymous in the summaries. Because players know the horizon (19 rounds), we expect typical dynamics:

- Early rounds: testing/cooperation phase. Players signal willingness to cooperate with messages and small positive rewards; moderate-to-high contributions are common as players try to build cooperation.
- Mid game: cooperation stabilizes if rewarders consistently favor contributors; occasional low contributors will be sanctioned implicitly by withholding rewards while cooperators are selectively rewarded.
- Late rounds: some endgame decline in contributions is likely as forward-looking incentives shrink, but if reward patterns have supported cooperation, the decline may be gradual rather than collapse. Rewards will be used sparingly and mostly to encourage high contributors; explicit punishment is not possible, so withholding reward is the primary deterrent against free-riding.
- Chat will be sparse and used to coordinate or call out behavior in general terms (e.g., “let’s keep contributing,” “thanks everyone”), not to identify who rewarded whom.

Predictions below simulate plausible choices, chat, and sparse reward flows consistent with these incentives and the six player priors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players and 19 rounds. Each player has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to the group pot; the remainder they keep privately. The total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After redistribution, players may spend costly punishment (each unit costs the sender 2 and deducts 3 from the target) or costly reward (each unit costs the sender 2 and adds 2 to the target). Messages can be sent any time.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds are exploratory. Some players will test generosity; others may test free-riding. Initial contributions often start moderately high from prosocial, educated or experienced players (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) and lower from players with less stake or less patience for cooperation (SLOTH, PARROT). DOG is likely pragmatic/middle.
- When free-riding is observed, a small number of players (often the more cooperative ones) will occasionally punish to enforce norms. Punishment is typically sparse because it is costly. Rewards are also used, but less frequently than verbal encouragement.
- Over middle rounds, conditional cooperators will adjust: increase contributions if others also contribute, reduce if they see persistent free-riding. Punishments can stabilize cooperation somewhat.
- As the known final round approaches, strategic incentives push toward more defection (end-game effect). Punishment becomes less effective because future rounds are limited; costly sanctioning tends to decline late in the game.
- Uncertainties: who will punish or reward is anonymous in the summary, so social signaling matters via chat. Players may misinterpret one-off low contributions. Individual profiles matter: GORILLA and DUCK likely anchor cooperation, CHICKEN mild cooperator, DOG pragmatic, PARROT variable, SLOTH tends toward lower contributions but responds to sanctions.

Overall I predict moderate cooperation in early–middle rounds with occasional targeted punishments of repeat low contributors (mostly SLOTH and the occasional PARROT lapses), a few rewards to acknowledge high contributors, and an erosion of contributions toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players. Each round players choose either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to the group pot or keep it. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so when many people contribute everyone’s payoff from the pot rises. Free-riders (those who keep their 20) get the private benefit plus the shared pot share, so they often earn more in the short run. Because punishment is costly (each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 5 coins from the target), punishment will be used sparingly but purposefully to discourage persistent free-riding. Players can see who punished them in the round summaries, which enables targeted punishment and limited retaliation.

I expect an initial phase of mixed choices as players test each other, followed by cooperators using occasional punishment to stabilize contributions. A small set of players (here DUCK and DOG in many rounds) tends to free-ride while CHICKEN and PARROT act as enforcers, with SLOTH and GORILLA adjusting their behavior in response to punishment. Chat messages will be sparse — short requests to contribute or brief calls-out of free-riders. Punishment will mostly target obvious defectors at 1 punishment unit each rather than large, costly escalations.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a one-shot repeated public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20 per round), a multiplier of 3.7 on the group pot, and equal division among six players. Each coin a player contributes returns only 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167 coins to them personally, which is less than 1, so the private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) every round. game_explanation:
This is a standard binary-choice public goods game with 6 players. Each round every player decides privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (contribute = 20) or keep it (contribute = 0). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 3.7, and split equally among all six players. Because the return to the group per 20-coin contribution is 74 total (20 * 3.7) or 74/6 ≈ 12.333 coins per person, a contributor always reduces their own private holding by 20 while increasing everyone's share by 12.333 — so, for any fixed number of contributors, a non-contributor earns 20 coins more than a contributor. That makes free-riding the individually dominant action in one-shot terms, but the group total payoff is higher when more people contribute.

Likely unfolding and uncertainties:
- Early rounds often see some cooperation due to social preferences, pledges, or inexperienced players testing the waters. Because players can send messages, we expect a few short pre-commitments or appeals to cooperate in early rounds.
- Once players observe round payoffs (they see their own net payoff and peers' payoffs each round), they can infer who likely contributed. If contributors are consistently exploited (they earn less than non-contributors), cooperation tends to drop over rounds.
- The unknown horizon (players do not know how many rounds remain) tempers the classic endgame unraveling; that can sustain more cooperation than a known final round would. Still, the standard pattern is decay in contributions over time, with occasional isolated attempts to restart cooperation via messages or experimental contributions.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: short pledges, brief complaints when exploited, and occasional proposals to coordinate.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot style public goods game with costly punishment and messages. Each round players decide privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so cooperating helps the group but is individually costly unless enough others also contribute. Because players do not know the number of rounds, forward-looking incentives to maintain cooperation (and to punish non-contributors) are present: punishment can sustain contributions by imposing a cost on free-riders, but punishment itself is costly to the punisher. Expect initially mixed behavior (some testing the waters), targeted punishment of clear free-riders, a few rounds of high cooperation once norms form, occasional lapses (temptation or retaliation), and relatively sparse, targeted punishment rather than broad retribution. Chat will be brief and typically used to call out defectors or to reassure others. Because players can identify who punished them, punishment will be used mainly to correct behavior rather than to hide. Near the middle rounds cooperation should be high; occasional defections will be punished to maintain norms. In the (unknown) final rounds some players may tempt a defection, but because the players do not know the true end, punishment still has some deterrent value.

game_explanation:
This public goods game will likely show the standard tension between private incentive to withdraw coins and the collective benefit from contributing. Early rounds often begin with relatively high contributions as players test cooperation; once a few players free-ride, conditional cooperators respond by lowering contributions. Because punishment and reward are costly, players will use them sparingly — punishments to deter clear free-riding and occasional rewards to signal appreciation — but not enough to eliminate all defection. Messages will be short and strategic (encouragement, mild scolding, or signaling intent). Towards the middle players may re-establish higher cooperation when punishment/reward deter freeloaders; in the final round(s), forward-looking enforcement weakens and freeriding becomes more common.

Order of play each round: players decide withdrawal/contribution in secret, contributions are pooled (1.8×) and split equally, then players may punish or reward others (each unit sent costs 2 coins; punish deducts 3 coins per unit from the target; reward adds 2 coins per unit to the target). Players see only their own round summary. Chat is allowed at any time but will be sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public goods game with 6 players, unknown horizon, and a high multiplier (3.7). Because players only choose between contributing their whole endowment (20) or nothing (0), early behavior will be informative and may shape beliefs. Typical dynamics here: many groups start cooperative (trust/experimentation), a single free-rider can earn noticeably more that round (20 + share) and prompt conditional cooperators to punish by withholding contributions in later rounds, and some players (consistent cooperators or "leaders") may try to restore cooperation via messages. Uncertainty about how many rounds remain tends to slow a harsh final-round wave of defection, but as cooperation falls and players learn about others' strategies, the group often drifts to a mixed or defecting equilibrium. Chat tends to be sparse but can shift behavior when players signal intentions or threaten reciprocation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players who choose between full contribution (20) or full withholding (0) each round. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly, so an individual who contributes benefits the group but bears the private cost. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, cooperation can be sustained with occasional punishment: when contributors see a defector, some will pay to punish, reducing the defector's payoff and signaling that free-riding is costly. Punishment is costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) and much harsher for the target (5 coins per unit), so players use it sparingly — typically a few 1–2 unit penalties focused on clear defectors. Expect mostly cooperation with intermittent defections (experimentation, stubborn free-riders, or "sloth") and targeted punishments from the more enforcement-inclined players (profiles suggesting Gorilla, Dog, Duck are attentive/pro-social). Chat is sparse and used mainly to call out defections or apologize.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each coin contributed benefits the group (multiplied by 2.2) but returns less than 1 to the individual, so the dominant (selfish) move is to contribute 0. However, many people are conditional cooperators or care about fairness and will contribute some positive amount; others will free-ride. Because punishments are costly but effective (cost to punisher 4, removes 10 from the punished) and rewards are cheaper for the target (giver pays 4, receiver gets +3), we expect only a few targeted punishments in response to obvious free-riding, and a small number of reciprocal rewards for higher contributors.

Round 1 is informational: players may try to signal or pledge contributions. If someone contributes very little (a clear free-rider), some of the higher contributors may punish that person sparsely (1 unit each). Rewards in round 1 are likely few but may be used to encourage cooperators. After observing punishments, marginal players (those punished) often increase contributions in round 2 to avoid further sanctions; others may reciprocate or fine-tune their contributions. Because this is the final round, strategic incentives to maintain reputation across rounds are limited, but immediate reciprocity and second-round responses still matter.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 rounds among six players. Each round everyone starts with 20 coins and privately decides how much to withdraw (so contribution = 20 - withdrawal). The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (equivalently, each player receives 0.3×(sum of contributions)). After everyone sees contributions and redistribution, players may spend coins to punish (cost 2 to giver, -3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 2 to giver, +2 to target per unit). Messages can be sent anytime.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation with a few low contributors (free-riders). Peers sometimes punish low contributors and occasionally reward cooperative behavior. This tends to raise contributions slightly after punishments/rewards.
- Middle rounds: with occasional sanctioning and targeted rewards, cooperation can increase to a peak as players learn others' tendencies.
- Late rounds (endgame): incentives to defect increase (future consequences vanish). Contributions tend to decline; punishments become less common or sporadic because punishing is costly and less effective late in the game. Rewards become rarer as earnings are prioritized.
- Chat will be sparse and pragmatic: reminders, appeals, short acknowledgements. Punishments and rewards are used sparingly — usually 0–2 units per actor and only when players find it worthwhile.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 19. At each round start there's a short explanation for the expected behavior, then contribution choices, optional chat, punishment/reward actions (only non-zero edges shown), and the per-player summary each player would see for that round (their own net payoff and their personal punishment/reward spending/receiving).

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with costly positive rewards. Individually, contributing to the public pot is costly (each coin returns only ~0.37 to each player), so the short-run incentive is to free-ride. However, some players may be conditionally cooperative or motivated by fairness and may contribute non-zero amounts, especially in round 1 when social norms are being tested. Because rewards cost the giver (4 coins) and benefit the receiver (+3), players will use them sparingly and mainly to encourage higher contributors or to reciprocate seen generosity.

Key dynamics and uncertainties:
- Round 1 typically sees a mix of contributions: some moderate cooperators, some low contributors. Players have heterogeneous priors (age, employment, education) that make a spread of behaviors likely.
- Rewards are used selectively to signal approval of high contributors; because reward information is not attributed to specific senders, its signaling power is limited but can still indicate that cooperation is noticed by others.
- In round 2 players update based on their personal round-1 outcome (their net payoff and whether they used/received rewards). Those who received positive reward are likely to keep or raise contributions; those who paid a lot for rewards without receiving may reduce contributions or reward less.
- Overall, expect modest cooperation, sparse rewarding targeted at the more generous contributors, and some adjustment between rounds: cooperators tend to stay cooperative; self-interested free-riders may continue to contribute little.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, 19 rounds, and an opportunity to reward others after seeing contributions. Each player starts each round with 20 coins in the common pot but may withdraw some privately; the remainder is their contribution. A contribution of 1 benefits the group by 1.8 total, but each individual only gets 0.3 back — so the private incentive is to withdraw (contribute 0) while the group optimum is full contribution. Rewarding lets players transfer value to others (cost 2 to giver, +2 to receiver per reward unit) and can be used to sustain cooperation through reciprocity, but rewards are anonymous (players don’t see who rewarded them), and costly to the giver.

Given the profiles, I expect the higher-education players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG) to lean toward cooperation early; some others (CHICKEN, PARROT, DUCK) may free-ride more or test the waters. Early rounds will see moderate-to-high contributions with a few small rewards targeted at visibly cooperative choices. Over time, free-riding and the anonymity of rewards will erode cooperation: cooperators will reduce rewards when exploitation appears, and contributions will gradually decline. Toward the end (final few rounds), classic endgame behavior (defection) becomes likely and cooperation collapses; rewards largely disappear.

Each player starts each round with 20 coins in the common pot but may withdraw some privately; the remainder is their contribution. A contribution of 1 benefits the group by 1.8 total, but each individual only gets 0.3 back — so the private incentive is to withdraw (contribute 0) while the group optimum is full contribution. Rewarding lets players transfer value to others (cost 2 to giver, +2 to receiver per reward unit) and can be used to sustain cooperation through reciprocity, but rewards are anonymous (players don’t see who rewarded them), and costly to the giver.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 19 rounds. Each round players decide how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; individually, the marginal per-capita return is 0.3 (1.8/6). That creates a tension: contributing helps the group but yields less than keeping coins privately. Because players see only their own round summaries (their net payoff, how much they spent on rewards, and how much they received) and cannot identify who rewarded them, reputational incentives are noisy. The reward stage (cost 2 per unit to giver, +2 per unit to receiver) can sustain cooperation by selectively rewarding contributors, but it is costly to the giver and will therefore be used sparingly.

Given the player priors, I expect:
- DOG (postgraduate, pro-social) to be a consistent high contributor and an early focal reward target.
- PARROT and GORILLA, older/less employed profiles, may be cooperative and occasionally reward.
- DUCK and CHICKEN may be more conditional or strategic; CHICKEN may drift to freeriding near the end.
- SLOTH is moderate and responsive to group cues.

We should see moderate contributions early, some positive reinforcement through rewards that lift cooperation mid-game, then a gradual decline as the end approaches (classic endgame unwinding). Chat will be sparse and mostly short appeals or acknowledgements. Rewards will be used selectively and not every round.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with a modest multiplier (2.2) and a separate costly reward stage. Each round players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute; total contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so private returns from contributing are less than the cost (marginal private return < 1) but the social return per coin contributed (2.2) exceeds the private cost — creating a social dilemma. The reward stage lets players transfer help at a cost (4 coins) to give a smaller benefit (3 coins) to another player; this supports reciprocity and rewarding of cooperators but is expensive to the giver.

Expectations and likely dynamics:
- In round 1, without past behavior to condition on, many players use moderate contributions: some will try to encourage cooperation (higher contributions), others will hedge or free-ride. Contributions will be publicly revealed each round, so players can condition rewards on observed contribution levels.
- Rewards are typically sparse and targeted: players are likelier to reward clearly high contributors (to encourage them) or reciprocate those who rewarded them. Because rewards are costly to send and players cannot identify the source of rewards they receive, reward patterns will be relatively cautious and not too numerous.
- With only two rounds, there is an endgame effect: some players may maintain cooperative contributions in round 2 if they expect reciprocity, while others may reduce contributions if they see free-riding rewarded or if they wish to free-ride in the final round. Reward behavior in round 1 will influence round 2 choices: cooperators who were rewarded are more likely to keep cooperating; those who were ignored may reduce contributions.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a short (2-round) public goods game with 6 players. Each round every player privately decides how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so each 1 coin a player contributes returns only about 0.3667 to them individually — a strictly selfish incentive is to contribute 0. Because the game lasts only two rounds and identities are not persistent in punishment/reward feedback, incentives to free-ride are strong. However, many players here have postgraduate backgrounds and full-time employment, which in lab settings often correlates with some cooperation in early rounds. Expect a mix: several moderately cooperative contributions (~half the endowment) and at least one low contributor or free-rider. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (cost 4 to source, -10 to target) or reward (cost 4 to source, +3 to target). Punishment is costly for the group (it destroys value) and is usually used sparingly — mostly targeted at clear free-riders. Rewards are used even more sparingly because they are costly to the giver and only partially benefit the receiver. Because players cannot identify individual sources of punishment/reward, retaliation is less targeted, but players will still update beliefs: those heavily punished are likely to increase contributions in round 2 to avoid further sanctions, and those who punished may reduce contribution or switch to rewarding in round 2. Chat is possible but usually sparse.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits a simple cooperation incentive (contribute 20 to a shared pot) against the private benefit of free-riding (keep 20). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so if many contribute everyone benefits; an individual who defects can gain more that round unless others punish. Because punishment is costly to assign (2 coins per punishment unit) but effective at reducing a target's payoff (5 coins lost per unit), norm enforcers will use punishments sparingly to deter repeated free-riding. Players do not know the number of rounds, so we should not see a clear "final-round collapse." Given the player profiles, I expect a few reliably cooperative players (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT, CHICKEN, DOG) and one who lapses often (SLOTH). PARROT (older, postgraduate) is the most likely norm enforcer and will punish defectors occasionally; GORILLA may also punish sometimes. Punishment is sparse and targeted: single-unit punishments to signal displeasure early, occasionally repeated against persistent defectors. Chats will be infrequent, with PARROT issuing short normative reminders and occasional short replies from defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each round every player chooses how much to withdraw from a common 20-coin pot; the remainder is their contribution. The group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and divided equally, so each player’s share of the public good equals 0.3 times the total contributions. After payoffs from the pot are distributed, players can spend reward units (cost 2 coins per unit) to give 2 coins per unit to others. Players only see their own round payoff and their own reward sending/receiving totals; identities of rewarders are hidden.

Given the profiles and the limited feedback (players don’t see others’ contributions directly), a realistic dynamic here is moderate initial cooperation followed by gradual decline: early rounds tend to feature some rewarding of higher contributors to encourage cooperation, but without detailed information about others’ contributions conditional cooperation is harder to sustain. Over time, contributions drift downward, and rewards become sparser — used occasionally to acknowledge comparatively cooperative behavior or to encourage a laggard. Chat is likely to be sparse and purposeful (promises, thanks, brief complaints), and rewards will be used sparingly (one or two units at most in many rounds).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses how much to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. The MPCR is 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367, so privately it's best to contribute 0, but socially the group does best if everyone contributes all. After contributions are revealed, players can spend costly reward units (4 coins cost to giver, 3 coins benefit the recipient) to reward others. Rewards are typically used sparingly: players often reward clear cooperators and sometimes reciprocate rewards received earlier. Because the game lasts only two rounds, players may increase cooperation if they expect reciprocal rewards in round 2, or they may exploit (free-ride) if they expect few punishments and limited future interaction. Communication is allowed but usually limited; short strategic or social messages (thanks, requests to cooperate) are the most likely.

Given the profiles, I predict moderate heterogeneity: a few players will contribute above the group average, a few will free-ride or contribute less, and rewards will be a small number of single units directed at those who contributed relatively more in round 1, with some reciprocity in round 2.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round repeated public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone decides privately how many of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution) and how many to withdraw privately. The total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so every player's payoff from the public good equals 0.3 times the total group contribution. After returns are distributed, players can spend costly punishment (2 coins per punishment unit, which deducts 3 coins per unit from the target) or costly reward (2 coins per reward unit, which gives 2 coins per unit to the target). Messages can be posted at any time.

Likely dynamics: early rounds often show moderate-to-high cooperation as people test each other. Some players (given profiles) may try to free-ride early (DUCK and sometimes SLOTH), while CHICKEN, PARROT, and DOG are more likely to contribute strongly and to try to influence others. Punishment and reward are used sparingly; a few low contributions will trigger targeted punishment from cooperative players to keep free-riding in check. Rewards are used occasionally to praise consistent contributors. Over many rounds people experiment and respond: contributions may dip when punishments are rare or when players believe future enforcement is weak, and rise when targeted sanctions succeed. Near the final rounds there's usually some drift toward lower contributions, though prior punishments can sustain intermediate cooperation. Chat is sparse and used mostly to remind others about cooperation or to apologize/justify choices.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishment and costly rewards. Each player has a private incentive to keep coins (marginal private return < 1 per coin contributed), but group payoffs are higher when people contribute. In round 1 players are uncertain about others and often "test" the group — we should see a mix of contributions (some cooperative, some low). Punishment and reward are typically used sparingly: punishment is a strong deterrent (costly to target) but costs the punisher as well, so people punish only when they perceive clear free-riding; rewards are less efficient (costlier to the sender than the benefit to the receiver) and are used occasionally to acknowledge cooperators.

Because the game lasts only 2 rounds, players may be less interested in long-run enforcement, but a single round of punishment can still push a low contributor to raise contributions in round 2. Expect modest increases in contributions in round 2 as players respond to round 1 signals. Chat messages will be sparse and purposeful (calls for cooperation, brief apologies or acknowledgements). Punishment and reward actions will be few and targeted — likely punishment directed at very low contributors in round 1 and a few rewards for notable cooperators or for improvement in round 2.
