game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a multi-round public goods game where each player gets 20 coins and can either contribute all (20) or nothing (0) to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the six players. Individually, contributing is costly: each coin you contribute yields less than one coin back to you personally (your marginal return per coin is 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167), so the self-interested incentive is to keep the 20 and free-ride. Collectively, however, everyone would be better off if multiple players contribute.

Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect some initial willingness to test cooperation, especially from players predisposed to be cooperative or to make signals (PARROT, DOG, GORILLA). Others may try free-riding early (SLOTH looks like a likely free-rider). Communication is sparse but can help form tacit agreements; however, as free-riding benefits the individual in each round, cooperation typically decays over time. We should see some early cooperation (partial), then decline as free-riding is rewarded, with occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation via messages and isolated contributions. Players will see only round-level payoffs (their own and peers'), so responses will be based on those observed payoffs and the occasional chat.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players, each given 20 coins per round and forced to choose either full contribution (20) or full free-ride (0). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so contributing helps the group but is individually costly if others free-ride: a lone contributor returns less to themselves than keeping the 20 privately. Because players do not know the number of rounds, early cooperation can be sustained by conditional responses (punishment) to free-riding. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) but imposes a larger cost on the target (5 coins per unit), so a small, credible threat of punishment can deter defection.

I expect many rounds to show high cooperation (most players contribute) interspersed with occasional defections (experimentation or lapses). Cooperators who want to enforce norms (GORILLA, PARROT, CHICKEN in these profiles) will punish sparingly but visibly after clear free-riding, while some players (SLOTH, DUCK, or PARROT occasionally) may test the tolerance of the group. Punishment amounts will typically be 1 unit from one or two players; occasionally a harsher 2-unit response may be used against repeated defectors. Chats will be short — appeals to contribute, calls-out after free-riding, or brief justifications of punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with punishment and reward. The social optimum is that everyone contributes their endowment (20), because the group benefit multiplier (2.2) makes the public pot large; but individually each coin contributed returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to the contributor — less than 1 — so the private incentive is to free-ride. Punishment and reward allow players to change payoffs post-hoc: punishing is costly to the punisher (4 per unit) but imposes a heavier penalty on the target (-10 per unit), while rewarding costs 4 and gives only +3 to the target. These instruments can sustain cooperation if players care about norms or reciprocity, but they are themselves costly and, in a known-two-round game, backward induction makes contributions especially fragile in round 2 (players have no future rounds in which to be rewarded for cooperating).

Given the player profiles and the short horizon, I expect moderate cooperation in round 1 (many players contribute around halfway or more) with a few sparse rewards to reinforce high contributors and at most a light punishment directed at noticeable low contributors. In round 2 contributions will tend to fall (final-round unraveling), and punishment will be used sparingly (some players may still punish zero contributors out of fairness or spite), while a small number of rewards may be used to acknowledge decent contributions. Chat will be sparse and largely normative or apologetic.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 19-round public goods game with optional costly rewards. Each round players choose how much to withdraw (keep privately) and the remainder is contributed to a shared pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. The basic tension: withdrawing more increases your private take that round but reduces the group return; contributing more benefits everyone (including free riders). Rewarding is the only social enforcement mechanism: it is costly (2 coins per reward unit) and gives an equal benefit (2 coins) to the recipient. Rewards therefore are a way to incentivize contributors (or signal approval) but cost the sender.

Typical unfolding: early rounds are a test — some players signal cooperation and others probe for free riding. If many contribute, the multiplier returns visible payoffs that encourage continued cooperation; if free riding appears, contributors may reduce contributions or direct sparse positive rewards to cooperators. Rewards are usually infrequent because they cost real money, so expect a few targeted rewards to cooperative players rather than broad-scale rewarding. Uncertainty about who rewarded whom (players cannot identify reward sources) makes rewards a cleaner prosocial signal but less effective at direct reciprocity. Over 19 rounds, expect an initial cooperative phase with a few small rewards to reinforce contributors, a mid-game dip as some players reduce contributions, and a recovery toward the end if players try to encourage cooperation before the game ends. Chat will be sparse, mainly short suggestions, calls for cooperation, or short reactions to perceived opportunism.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated binary public goods game with private contributions of either 0 or 20 each round. Each round players face the classic tension: contributing 20 increases the group pot (which is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly), raising everyone’s payoff, but any individual who withholds (keeps 20) can earn more that round if enough others contribute. Communication is allowed but non-binding, and players do not know how many rounds will be played — that uncertainty tends to sustain cooperation somewhat relative to a known finite horizon, but the temptation to free-ride often causes cooperation to fall over time.

Given the six-player makeup and the profiles:
- Some players (e.g., DOG, GORILLA) read as plausibly conditional cooperators and may try to sustain cooperation.
- Others (e.g., CHICKEN, PARROT) appear more likely to free-ride or be opportunistic.
- Younger or part-time players (SLOTH, PARROT, DUCK) may be mixed — they might signal cooperation early, then adjust based on observed payoffs.

I predict moderate initial cooperation (several players contribute to signal good intent), then oscillations as players react to payoff feedback: when non-contributors earn noticeably more in a round, more players defect next round. Sparse, persuasive chat (promises, short reminders) will appear occasionally; it will sometimes help re-establish cooperation but will not fully prevent decay. Because contributions are all-or-nothing, payoff differences are stark and easy for players to infer from the per-player summaries, accelerating contingent responses.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentive (keep 20) against collective benefit (contribute 20 so the pot grows and everyone receives a share). Because contributions are binary (0 or 20) and the group payout is multiplied by 3.7 and divided equally, a lone cooperator helps the group but is personally worse off than a free-rider in that round unless others also contribute. Players can punish at a cost to themselves (2 coins per punishment unit) and inflict larger losses on targets (5 coins per unit). Punishment is the only sanction; it is costly and therefore used sparsely in real games, typically by cooperators to deter free-riding.

Given the provided profiles, I expect an initial mix of cooperation and some free-riding (particularly from more "laid-back" or opportunistic profiles). Cooperators (those who value fairness or have stronger pro-social priors) will occasionally punish free-riders, keeping group cooperation relatively high after the first rounds. Because players do not know when the game ends, there's no guaranteed end-game collapse; this tends to support stable cooperation enforced by intermittent, targeted punishment. Chat will be minimal and usually functional (calls for cooperation; short apologies or threats after punishments). Punishment events will be infrequent, targeted, and moderate in size—just enough to shift incentives.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 2-round public goods game with optional costly positive rewards. Each round, players choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Rewards are costly to give (4 coins per unit) and give less benefit to the receiver (3 coins), so rewards are mainly a signaling/reciprocity device rather than an efficient transfer.

Likely dynamics:
- Round 1: Because the game is short but not single-shot, many players will contribute a moderate amount out of social preference or to test others. Some players (especially those described as more "slothful" or with lower engagement) may contribute little. Players who see others contribute reasonably may use a single reward unit to signal appreciation for high contributors; rewards will be sparse because of the cost.
- Round 2 (final round): Incentive to free-ride is stronger (no future rounds beyond round 2), so average contributions typically decline. However, reciprocity or the desire to reward/reciprocate may sustain cooperation among a subset. Rewards in round 2 are likely to be similarly sparse and often reciprocal between those who cooperated in round 1.

Uncertainties:
- Players only see their own summary at the end of the round (their net, how much they gave in rewards, how much they received), so beliefs about who rewarded whom remain fuzzy. That weakens targeted reciprocal punishment/reward across rounds but does not eliminate the urge to reward visibly cooperative behavior immediately after contributions.

Given the player profiles: DUCK and PARROT (postgrad, older/postgrad) are more likely to contribute relatively high; GORILLA, DOG, CHICKEN moderate; SLOTH likely contributes little. Rewards will be few and directed toward the higher contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players for 19 rounds. Each round players choose an integer contribution (0–20) out of a common 20-coin pot; what remains they keep privately. Total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each player gets 0.3 times the group total in addition to their private withdrawal. After seeing the round outcome (their own payoff and reward activity involving themselves), players can send costly rewards: each reward unit costs 2 coins to the sender and gives 2 coins to the recipient.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some uncertainty; players often start moderately cooperative (near the group average) while testing others' willingness to contribute.
- Rewarding is costly but used sparingly to reinforce cooperators or reciprocate kindness. Because rewards are costly, they are typically small (1 or 2 units).
- Deviations (low contributions) tend to provoke social signals: withholding rewards, direct chat criticism, or selective rewarding of cooperators rather than active punishment.
- Over repeated rounds, stable cooperation around a moderate level (near the group average) is plausible if a few players consistently reward cooperators. Occasional rounds of reduced contribution may appear but small positive rewards can help restore cooperation.
- Chat is sparse and strategic: promises, short calls for cooperation, or targeted rebukes when someone clearly free-rides.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 rounds, moderate multiplication (1.8) and six players. Each round players choose a contribution (0–20) and then may punish or reward peers. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- The social optimum is high contribution by everyone; the private incentive in a single round is to withdraw (free-ride). Because the game is finite and players know it ends, cooperation tends to be higher early-on and erode toward the final rounds unless costly enforcement (punishment/reward) sustains it.
- Punishment is costly to administer (2 coins per unit) and inflicts a larger hit on the target (-3 per unit), so it can deter persistent free-riding but is used sparingly in experiments.
- Rewards cost the same to the giver (2 per unit) but transfer smaller benefits (+2 per unit), so they are also used conservatively to reinforce cooperators.
- Expect early rounds to open with modest-to-high cooperation from those with pro-social priors (SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT). Unemployed players (GORILLA, DOG) may be more inclined to withdraw early but can be nudged by others' punishments and rewards.
- Chat is typically sparse; players will send short normative appeals ("please contribute") or short complaints after seeing low contributions.
- Punishments will be limited and targeted at obvious low contributors; rewards will be occasional to encourage cooperation. As the end approaches, players will reduce costly enforcement and contributions decline.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript from round 1 to round 19. Each round begins with an explanation of expected behavior, then shows any chat, contributions, punish/reward actions (only non-zero edges), and the private summaries players get for that round.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with a multiplier 2.2 split among six players. Each coin contributed benefits the group but returns less than one coin to the contributor individually (2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 per coin), so the purely selfish incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Social preferences, reputation-building, and the availability of costly punishment/reward can sustain positive contributions. Because the game is only two rounds, backward induction tends to reduce cooperation in the final round, but the ability to punish/reward after round 1 gives players a mechanism to influence behavior in round 2. Expect modest contributions in round 1 as players test each other, a few targeted punishment actions against very low contributors (punishment is costly but punitive: sender pays 4 per unit, target loses 10 per unit), and sparse rewards for clear cooperators (sender pays 4 per unit, target gains 3 per unit). After round 1 punishments, low contributors who are sanctioned are likely to raise contributions in round 2 to avoid further sanctions; some players will still defect in round 2 knowing retaliation opportunities are nearly gone, so expect mixed behavior and very limited punishment in round 2.

game_explanation:
This is a classic social-dilemma public goods game. Each coin you keep yields 1 to you, while each coin contributed yields 2.2 in total, split equally across the six players — so each coin you contribute returns only about 0.37 to yourself but 2.2 to the group. That creates an incentive to free-ride (keep your coins) while socially optimal play is high contributions. Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking incentives are weak: players may cooperate in round 1 but tend to reduce contributions in the last round. The punishment and reward stage lets players impose social enforcement: punishments are costly to send (4 coins) but harsh to targets (-10 per punishment unit), rewards cost 4 and give +3 to the target. In practice, players reward or punish sparingly. We can expect a mix of cooperation and free-riding in round 1, some light punishment aimed at obvious free-riders, a few rewards to high contributors, and adjustments in round 2 — likely some drop in contributions overall, with punished players possibly increasing contributions slightly but not fully recovering because the game ends after round 2.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, fixed roles, and 19 known rounds. Each round each player chooses an integer contribution (0–20); contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.8, then split equally, so each player receives 0.3 × (sum of contributions) in public-payoff units. A higher personal contribution lowers immediate private take but raises the group payoffs; low contributors free-ride. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly punishment or reward units: punish costs 2 to the source and removes 3 from the target; reward costs 2 to the source and gives 2 to the target. Messages are allowed and can coordinate or shame, but identities for punish/reward are not revealed in summaries.

Likely unfolding and incentives:
- Early rounds tend to show relatively high cooperation as players test each other’s behavior.
- Some players (given the profiles) are more likely to contribute consistently (e.g., DUCK, CHICKEN), while others may be more opportunistic or inconsistent (e.g., SLOTH, PARROT). GORILLA and DOG are plausibly steady contributors but not extreme.
- Punishment and reward typically appear sparingly. Punishment is used to deter clear free-riding; rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperative behavior. Because punishments are costly to send and costly to receive, expect mostly single-unit actions when they happen.
- Because the horizon is known (19 rounds), contributions and enforcement motives can drift: early cooperation can erode if free-riding isn't checked; conversely, occasional punishment/reward can sustain moderate cooperation.
- Messages are used occasionally to propose norms, call out free-riders, or thank contributors; they are short and purposeful.

I simulate a plausible path across 19 rounds. Contributions are shown in avatar order <PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>. Punishment/reward edges list only non-zero actions. Positive unit = reward, negative unit = punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with binary choices (contribute 0 or 20). The dominant individual incentive is to free-ride: contributing costs you 20 but raises the group pot for everyone; because the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among six, a single contribution increases each player's return by (20*3.7)/6 ≈ 12.33 — less than the private cost (20). So pure self-interest pushes toward 0 contribution. However, conditional cooperation (people contributing if others do), social preferences, short-term reciprocity, and messages can sustain contributions early on. Because players do not know how many rounds there are, there is no guaranteed endgame unraveling — cooperation may persist or drift depending on early experiences.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: some players (especially those with stronger pro-social priors like DOG and SLOTH) will try contributing, while others test the waters by free-riding.
- When free-riding is visible through payoffs, contributors may reduce their contributions in later rounds unless messages or reciprocal behavior restores trust.
- Sparse, simple messages (promises, short appeals, or mild shaming) will occasionally appear and can temporarily increase contributions from a few players.
- Over 12 rounds we expect fluctuations: moderate cooperation early, occasional recoveries after appeals, but also drops when contributors feel exploited. DOG (older, postgraduate) is likely the most consistent contributor; younger players may be more variable.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with a clear social dilemma: each coin you contribute yields 2.2 coins to the group but only about 0.367 back to you personally (2.2/6), so the individually rational move is to contribute 0. Cooperation can raise everyone’s payoffs, but because players act simultaneously and anonymously, uncertainty about others' choices is high. With only two rounds, strategic incentives matter: players may try to establish cooperation in round 1 to earn rewards in round 2, but they may also free-ride early to get a guaranteed high round payoff. Rewarding is costly (4 coins spent to give 3 to someone), so rewards tend to be small and targeted: given anonymity of who rewarded whom, rewards function more as general social encouragement than precise reciprocity.

Given the supplied profiles:
- Some players (postgraduate or pro-social priors) are likely to contribute moderately to strongly in round 1 to signal cooperation.
- Others (risk-averse, older, or pragmatic) may contribute little or nothing, exploiting the public good.
- Because rewards are costly and the game is short, expect sparse rewarding in each round, targeted at those who clearly contributed more.
- After round 1, contributors who were exploited may reduce contributions, while those who saw rewards for contributing (or expect to be rewarded) may maintain or increase contributions in round 2.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated one-shot public goods game where each player must either contribute all (20) or nothing (0) each round. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among six players, so the marginal per-capita return on a contributed 20 is (20 * 3.7) / 6 ≈ 12.33 to each player — less than the 20 the contributor sacrifices but beneficial to the group. The dominant private incentive in any single round is to free-ride (contribute 0) because a free-rider keeps their 20 and still gets the group share. However, because players can punish at a cost (2 per punishment unit, removing 5 per unit from the target), punishments can deter free-riding, though they are costly for punishers.

Given players' profiles and typical PGG behavior, I expect:
- Early rounds: partial cooperation with a couple of free-riders. Pro-social players (e.g., DUCK and PARROT) are likely to punish observed defection, but punishment will be moderate because it costs the punisher.
- Middle rounds: punishment will be used selectively to bring defectors back into cooperating; repeated or hard-to-ignore defectors may be targeted with multiple units.
- Late rounds: without an end-point known, patterns stabilize: most players cooperate most rounds; occasional defection gets brief, targeted punishment. Chat messages will be sparse and typically admonitions or short coordination messages.

Uncertainties: players don't know the total number of rounds, so end-game effects are muted. Predictions assume bounded rationality and social preferences: some players punish to enforce fairness even at personal cost; others occasionally exploit the pot.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with six players for 19 rounds. Each round players decide how much of 20 coins to keep privately versus contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly (so each player's share = 0.3 × total contributions). After seeing contributions and redistribution, players may spend costly rewards (each reward unit costs 2 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver). Players know the horizon is 19 rounds.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty — mixed contributions. Some players with higher priors for cooperation (CHICKEN, DUCK, PARROT) will contribute relatively more; more self-interested or inexperienced players (GORILLA) may contribute less. SLOTH and DOG moderate and conditional.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors and reciprocate generosity; rewards are costly, so they are used selectively.
- Midgame: if rewards successfully encourage cooperation, contributions rise. Expect some reciprocity chains (small rewards to cooperators).
- Endgame (final rounds): with finite horizon, contributions typically fall (free-riding). Rewards drop off late because they are costly and strategic incentives to incentivize future behavior disappear.
- Communication is allowed; expect sparse, short chats (thanks, requests, short norms). Players will mainly act through contributions and occasional rewards.

Now the play-by-play simulation (transcript) from round 1 through round 19.

Typical dynamics: some players will try to cooperate (contribute a sizable part of their 20 coins) because the group multiplier (2.2) makes mutual cooperation profitable for the group, while self-interested short-run incentives favor free-riding (contribute 0). Rewarding is costly (4 coins per reward unit) but can selectively reinforce cooperation by giving recipients +3 per unit. Because rewards are costly, players reward sparingly and usually only reward clearly cooperative peers; those who contribute little are unlikely to be rewarded. Players who gave but were not rewarded may reduce contribution next round; players who received positive feedback often maintain or raise contributions. There is also uncertainty: some profiles (e.g., SLOTH, PARROT) suggest lower engagement or lower contributions, while others (GORILLA, DOG, CHICKEN, DUCK) are more likely to contribute and to use rewards strategically. Chat tends to be sparse — short pledges, thanks, or appeals for reciprocity.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This public goods game runs 19 rounds with 6 players. Each round everyone chooses privately how many of their 20 coins to withdraw; the remainder is contributed to a shared pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After observing the redistribution, players may spend coins to reward others (2 coins spent per 1 reward unit, and each unit gives the recipient +2 coins). Players see only their own round payoff and their own reward activity (how much they spent and how much they received), not who rewarded them.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others leads to moderate cooperation from many players, while one or two (typically younger/part-time profiles) may test freeriding. Small, targeted rewards will be used to reinforce cooperative behavior.
- Midgame: conditional cooperators increase contributions if they see cooperative signals (rewards/messages). Rewarding remains sparse and focused on consistently high contributors.
- Late game: as the horizon approaches, contributions drift downward (classic end-game effect). Players tend to reward less, though a few continue to reward consistent cooperators out of reciprocity or fairness.
- Throughout: chat is sparse and functional (encouragement, brief claims about strategy), and rewards are used sparingly to stabilize cooperation rather than as a constant tax/punishment.

Transcript (round-by-round). I include brief explanations of expected incentives at each round, sparse chat, the contributions in the specified avatar order, the reward actions (only non-zero), and each player's personal summary showing their net payoff for that round, amount they used for rewarding, and amount they received.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with costly, targeted punishment. Each round players decide simultaneously whether to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment or keep it. With a group multiplier of 3.7 split among 6 players, each coin contributed returns 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167 coins to each contributor — less than 1 coin to the contributor personally, so contributing is privately costly but benefits the group. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, forward-looking incentives can support cooperation: players who contribute may punish persistent free-riders to deter them, and that threat tends to sustain relatively high contributions. Chats appear occasionally as warnings or signals. Punishments are costly for both sides (punisher pays 2 per unit, target loses 5 per unit), so punishment tends to be used sparingly and targeted at obvious defectors.

Given the profiles and typical PGG dynamics, I expect most players to contribute regularly (20), one player (SLOTH) to test free-riding intermittently, and others to respond with small, targeted punishments to keep cooperation credible. A second player (PARROT) may test defection occasionally as well. Chats will be sparse — mostly short calls for cooperation, brief reactions to being punished, and occasional short admonitions.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with punishment and reward. Each round players choose how much of their private 20-coin endowment to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Contributing helps the group but costs the individual; free-riding (contributing little) can increase an individual's private return while reducing the group's payoff. The punishment and reward stage lets players impose costs or give benefits to others: punishment is costly to the sender (4 coins per unit) and severe to the target (10 coins per unit); reward also costs the sender (4 per unit) and gives a modest gain to the recipient (+3 per unit). Because the game lasts only two rounds, players weigh immediate payoff and the limited possibility of future retaliation or reciprocity. Typical dynamics: some players will contribute moderately to create shared benefit; obvious low contributors risk being punished (though punishment is expensive to send), and cooperators may sparingly reward each other. After a punitive round, low contributors often increase contributions in the final round to avoid further sanctions. Chats tend to be sparse and used to request cooperation or apologize.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with an intermediate costly reward stage. Each round players privately decide how much of their 20 coins to contribute; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and evenly split. Because players do not see others' choices before deciding, messages and reputational considerations (via rewards) are the only signals. Rewards are costly (4 coins per reward unit) but give a smaller benefit (3 coins) to the recipient, so sending rewards is a private cost used mainly to promote cooperation or reciprocate perceived cooperators.

Likely dynamics:
- Round 1: Many players will moderately contribute because there is some expectation of mutual cooperation and because rewards allow pro-socials to signal and reciprocate. Contributions will vary by individual priors (some “slothier” players contribute less). Rewards will be sparse: a few 1-unit rewards to recognize cooperators.
- Round 2 (last round): Standard backward induction reduces incentives to invest in others; contributions tend to fall. If players used rewards in round 1 and signaled reciprocation in chat, a small amount of reciprocal rewarding may occur again, but overall contribution and reward activity usually decline. Uncertainty remains because messages can persuade some to cooperate despite the endgame.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with simultaneous binary contributions (0 or 20) and the option to punish after outcomes. Key incentives:
- Contributing 20 increases the public pot (multiplied by 3.7) and benefits all players, but costs the contributor 20 immediately.
- Free-riding (contributing 0) gives an immediate private advantage if others contribute, because the free-rider still receives an equal share of the multiplied pot.
- Punishment allows the group to deter free-riding: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 5 coins from the punished player. Because punishment is costly to send, it tends to be used sparingly and targeted at clear free-riders.
Uncertainties and likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show mixed behavior: some players test if others will contribute. If free-riding appears, cooperators may punish to signal that free-riding will be costly.
- If punishment is credible and not too costly for punishers, cooperation tends to stabilize at high contribution rates. If punishments are too few or too costly, free-riding can persist.
- Players do not know how many rounds remain, so reciprocal and enforcement motives can sustain cooperation longer.
Given the player profiles and typical behavior, I expect a pattern of mostly cooperative rounds, with occasional single-player defections that are promptly punished by one or two others. Punishment will be relatively sparse and targeted, sustaining cooperation for most of the 12 rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 12-round online public goods game where each player has a binary choice each round: keep their 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or put the whole 20 into the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players, so a single contributor increases everyone's payoff but benefits free riders more in the short run. Players do not see others' choices before deciding, but after each round they see each player's net payoff for that round (so choices can be inferred imperfectly). Players can send sparse messages to attempt to coordinate.

Likely dynamics: initial rounds often show some cooperation from players with stronger pro-social priors (PARROT, GORILLA, DOG), with others free-riding to exploit that generosity. Seeing higher immediate payoffs for non-contributors typically erodes cooperation over rounds. Occasional short-lived recoveries are possible if someone publicly commits to contribute and others trust them, but without enforceable punishment and with fractional returns that favor free-riding, cooperation tends to decline toward zero contributions by the later rounds. Messages will be used sparingly — brief promises, complaints, or attempts to re-coordinate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players who each have 20 coins each round and can either keep all (0 contributed) or put the whole 20 into the group pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally. A single contributor increases everyone’s payout but loses on net individually because each contributor's personal return from contributing (≈12.33 per contribution divided by 6 shares) is less than the 20 they give up. The short-term incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0), while group-optimal outcomes require many players to contribute.

Because players do not know how many rounds will be played, we expect relatively higher cooperation in early rounds (a "shadow of the future" effect) and gradual decline as players observe that free riders earn more in early summaries. Some players appear likely to be conditional cooperators (they will contribute if they expect others to), while a subset will freeride consistently. Chat is cheap talk but can encourage cooperation early; messages will be sparse. Overall dynamic expected: modest cooperation in the first few rounds, then decay toward free-riding, with occasional isolated attempts to revive cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with six players. Each round players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Because each coin contributed returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to each contributor personally, the individually rational (money-maximizing) choice is to contribute 0. However, many people have social preferences (conditional cooperation, fairness) and may contribute positive amounts, especially early on. Contributions are observed after decisions, so players can reward (costly) those they like after seeing actual behavior. Rewards are expensive to give (cost 4 for the giver, give 3 to the recipient), so they tend to be used sparingly as a targeted incentive or thank-you.

Uncertainties: players don't know others' types exactly; they only see behavior as it unfolds. Because the game has only two rounds, backward-induction pressure favors low contributions in round 2 (and therefore reduces the incentive to spend on rewards in round 1 to influence future behavior). Still, conditional cooperators may contribute in round 1 to establish norms; some players may reward visible cooperators to encourage them to keep cooperating in round 2. Reward decisions are private in the sense players see how much they personally spent/received but cannot identify who gave them rewards.

Given the player priors:
- Some players are likely to be moderately cooperative (e.g., DUCK, PARROT, CHICKEN).
- Some may lean toward lower contributions (SLOTH, DOG).
- GORILLA may be a cautious cooperator, increasing slightly if others show cooperation.

I therefore expect a mix of positive contributions in round 1 (but not full cooperation), a few targeted reward transfers to thank/encourage high contributors, and modest adjustments in round 2: cooperators mostly persist or slightly increase; clear free-riders remain low. Rewards will be sparse and targeted at the higher contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round every player decides how many of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot (contributions 0–20); the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, and then players can punish (cost 2 per unit, -3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 2 per unit, +2 to target per unit) others. Players see contributions before choosing punish/reward, but cannot identify who punished or rewarded them later. The horizon is finite and known (19 rounds), so there's some incentive to defect toward the end. In early rounds there's uncertainty about others' norms; many groups begin with moderate contributions and test cooperation. Punishment and rewards are used sparingly in most real experiments: punishments tend to be used to discipline clear free-riders, and rewards are used occasionally to reinforce consistent contributors. Over time, absent very strong enforcement, contributions tend to drift down; however intermittent punishment/reward can stabilize cooperation mid-game. Near the final rounds, expect a decline in contributions (endgame effect). Chat tends to be sparse and strategic (calls for cooperation, occasional accusations).

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with a group of six. Each round each player privately decides how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute. Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so there is a social incentive to contribute (the group gets more), but a private incentive to free-ride (your private return on your own contribution is less than 1). After contributions are revealed, players can punish (costly to the punisher, strongly harmful to the punished) or reward (costly to the rewarder, modest benefit to the rewarded). Because punishment is expensive for the punisher, players tend to punish sparingly; rewards are also used sparingly. Communication (chat) is possible and often used to signal intentions or justify sanctions.

Uncertainties: players do not know others' moves before contributing, and while contributions are revealed before the punishment/reward stage, players cannot later be identified as the source of any punishment/reward in the summary information. Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking incentives differ across rounds: in round 1 some conditional cooperators may contribute more to elicit cooperation or to avoid punishment in round 2; in round 2, with no future rounds beyond it, there is often a tendency to reduce contributions and to punish less (retaliation is less profitable).

Using the supplied profiles as priors, I expect a mixed pattern: some players (e.g., DUCK, CHICKEN) lean toward cooperation and may contribute relatively high in round 1; others (SLOTH, PARROT) may free-ride or contribute little. Moderate contributors (GORILLA, DOG) may choose middle amounts. Punishments and rewards will be sparse: a small number of targeted punishments of clear free-riders in round 1 and a few rewards to high contributors. In round 2 overall contributions will tend to drift downward, but previous sanctions may induce some adjustment (punished free-riders may raise contributions slightly to avoid further sanctions; rewarded cooperators often stay cooperative).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each round players decide how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot (by withdrawing privately and leaving the remainder as a contribution). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.3 coins to every player. That makes a single coin contributed worth 1.8 total but only 0.3 to the individual — a classic social dilemma favoring free-riding in the short run but cooperation can raise everyone's payoffs.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high contributions as players test each other and build norms. A few positive messages and occasional small rewards can sustain cooperation.
- Mid game: occasional defections (low contributions) will appear. Because punishment and reward are available, other players may respond with small, targeted punishments to deter persistent free-riding, and occasional rewards to reinforce high contributors. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit sent; -3 to target) so it will be used sparingly.
- Late game: as the end approaches, contributions typically drift downward (endgame effect) because future retaliation has less deterrent power, so cooperation decays. Players send fewer messages and punish less in the last rounds.
- Chat will be sparse and functional (requests to cooperate, apology after being punished, occasional praise). Punishments and rewards will be rare and small (1 unit or so) and primarily targeted at clear low contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 each round and a binary contribution choice (0 or 20). The social optimum is everyone contributing because the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally; if all six contribute, each gets a large return. However, contributing is costly to the individual: regardless of what others do, any single player is better off keeping their 20 (free-riding) because the private benefit of keeping 20 exceeds the marginal return they get from their own contribution. Typical dynamics: some players (more prosocial or trusting) will try to start cooperation early, but free-riding pays off in the short run and tends to erode contributions over rounds. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no guaranteed endgame collapse, but uncertainty usually reduces cooperative persistence. Chat can temporarily sustain cooperation through promises or appeals, but repeated reward for defection will likely push many players to stop contributing. Expect an initial burst of cooperation from more prosocial/older/educated players (e.g., DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT), then a decline as free-riders (e.g., younger or strategic players) exploit cooperative offers. Occasional re-starts of cooperation via chat are possible but fragile.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players for 19 rounds. Each round players choose how much of 20 coins to withdraw privately (so contributions = 20 - withdrawal). The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each player's return from the public pot equals 1.8*(sum of contributions)/6. After seeing the redistribution each round, players can pay to reward others (each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Players cannot identify who rewarded them, and at summary each player only sees their own round payoff and the amounts they gave/received.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds feature relatively high contributions from a subset of players (those predisposed to cooperate: CHICKEN, DUCK, PARROT), mixed with a persistent lower-contributor (DOG) and moderate contributors (SLOTH, GORILLA).
- Because rewards are visible only as amounts received (not who sent them), players will use small, sparse rewards to signal appreciation for visible pro-social behavior or to encourage cooperation. Rewards are costly, so they're used sparingly and mainly to reward consistent high contributors (CHICKEN, DUCK, PARROT).
- Without explicit attribution, conditional cooperators will adjust slowly. If free-riding persists (DOG), others will attempt to sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors rather than punishing free-riders.
- Toward the middle and end, a few small reward spikes may appear as players try to stabilize cooperation and encourage high contributors; overall contributions drift modestly but remain above pure defection because of the reward channel and some players' intrinsic cooperativeness.

Players choose how much to keep (withdraw) so that contributions to the public pot are integers 0–20; contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally among the 6 players. A few players (PARROT, GORILLA) are likeliest to keep contributing more and to exchange small reciprocal rewards; CHICKEN and DUCK may alternate between cooperative acts and restraint. SLOTH is expected to keep contributing little.
- Chats will be sparse and pragmatic (short encouragements, thanks after receiving rewards), not sustained negotiation.

game_explanation:
This two-round public goods game pits individual short-run incentives (keep your 20 coins) against group incentives (contribute to a joint pot that is multiplied and shared). Contributions increase everyone’s earnings but are risky: others may free-ride. The reward stage lets players reward cooperators at a cost (4 coins to giver, +3 to recipient), which can sustain cooperation if players expect reciprocity. Uncertainties include how much others value future reciprocity (especially in the last round), how much they punish low contributors (by withholding rewards), and heterogeneity in preferences across profiles. Because the game lasts only two rounds, we often see moderate cooperation in round 1 and some decline in round 2 (last-round effect), with sparse, targeted rewards: modest positive reinforcement for high contributors and likely zero reward to clear free-riders.

Given the provided profiles, I expect a mix: a few high contributors (younger, highly educated players often contribute more), some moderate contributors, and one or two low contributors. Rewards will be used sparingly to acknowledge high contributors rather than to punish. In round 2, contributions may fall slightly overall, but players who contributed well in round 1 will tend to get rewarded again.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and a binary contribution choice (0 or 20). Each round players decide privately whether to contribute their entire 20 to the group pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can punish or reward others at personal cost (punish: source pays 3 per unit and target loses 11 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit and target gains 4 per unit). Because the game is finite and identities are anonymous in summaries, I expect the following broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: many players try to establish cooperation; a few test or exploit by free-riding. Punishment and reward are used sparingly to enforce norms — a couple of cooperators occasionally punish repeat defectors or reward steady cooperators.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is effective, cooperation stabilizes with most contributing. Occasional temptation defects appear (players test whether others will respond), punished lightly.
- Late rounds (endgame): forward-looking incentives weaken; some players start defecting more. Punishment tends to decline because it costs the punisher and there's little future leverage, though a few players still punish out of norm enforcement or spite.
- Rewards are used less frequently than punishments overall; both actions are relatively rare and small in magnitude.

I will simulate plausible, modest use of punishment and reward across the 14 rounds consistent with these tendencies and the provided player profiles.

Punishment pressure and social norms lead to full-group cooperation this round. With everyone contributing, players mostly refrain from punishing and a small reward acknowledges helpful behavior.

Cooperation recovers; most contribute as the norm remains relatively strong. Small pro-social gestures continue.
As the horizon shortens, a couple of players (SNAKE, CROCODILE) defect again. Cooperators respond with sparse sanctions; punishment remains low-intensity because costs to punishers are nontrivial.

Defection increases as players approach the endgame: CROCODILE and SNAKE continue to free-ride and CHICK (testing the waters) also defects. Others punish minimally to signal disapproval, while a leader occasionally rewards a cooperator.
Endgame behavior intensifies: several players defect (including CHICK and CHICKEN), and a few cooperators still incur punishment costs to sanction them. Punishments remain small and sparse.

Final-round collapse: several players defect knowing there is no future punishment opportunity. Punishment is minimal because most players avoid paying to punish in the last round, though one or two punitive acts still occur.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players for 26 rounds. Each round everyone decides simultaneously whether to contribute the full 20 coins (cooperate) or withdraw them privately (defect). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so individual monetary incentive favors withdrawing (defection) because a defector always keeps the private 20 in addition to the shared return. Cooperation can nevertheless appear if some players value group outcomes, reciprocity, or if others use the reward stage to reward cooperators (or, indirectly, to create a norm).

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty. A minority of “prosocial” players will test cooperation; many will defect to see how others behave.
- Middle rounds: if cooperators get rewarded (small positive transfers) and begin to see reciprocity, cooperation can climb to a majority or even full cooperation. Rewarding is costly, so only some players will consistently use it; others free-ride on the public good and on the social incentives.
- Late rounds (endgame): rational players anticipating the end may reduce cooperation; free-riding tends to increase unless norms are very strong.
- Chat will be sparse: a few short messages about intentions, thanks, or appeals to fairness. Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted mostly to cooperators; punishments are indirect (withholding rewards) rather than negative transfers.

Below is a round-by-round transcript simulation from round 1 through round 26. Each round starts with a short explanation of expectations, shows any chat, the contributions in the required avatar order, any chat after contributions, the reward actions (only non-zero edges), and then a note that each player sees their own round summary (net payoff, amount used to reward others, amount received).

game_explanation:
This is a multi-round public goods game with 12 players and repeated interaction over 26 rounds. Each round every player either keeps the full 20 (contributes 0) or leaves it in the shared pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally; defecting (keeping 20) gives a direct private advantage unless group cooperation is high. After redistribution players can spend coins to punish (cost 1 to sender, -2 to target per unit) or reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target per unit). Messages are allowed but identities of punishers/rewarders are not revealed in summaries.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some mix of cooperation and defection as players test norms. A few cooperative messages ("please contribute") and sparse punishments/rewards will appear.
- Midgame: if a subset enforces cooperation through occasional punishment and rewards, cooperation stabilizes at a relatively high level (most players contributing) because the group benefit is visible. Punishment will be used sparingly — only against repeat defectors — and rewards used occasionally to encourage cooperators.
- Endgame: toward the final rounds, incentives to defect grow (no future rounds to enforce reputation), so cooperation tends to unravel. Expect more defections and fewer punishments/rewards in final rounds.
- Messages will be sparse and functional: calls to cooperate, gratitude, occasional accusations or resignation in the endgame.

We will simulate these tendencies. Expect a handful of persistent defectors to be punished occasionally, a majority of honest cooperators through the middle rounds, and a clear breakdown toward the last few rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 anonymous players against the standard incentive tension: contributing 20 coins helps the group (the pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally) but costs the individual more than their immediate private return, so the one-shot rational action is to free-ride. However, repeated interaction, visible round-by-round summaries, and the ability to punish/reward make conditional cooperation and social enforcement likely. Expect an initial mix: many players will try cooperating early to see reciprocal behavior; a small number will test by free-riding. When free-riders show up, a few players—those who value reciprocity or inequality aversion—are likely to spend small amounts to punish (costly to them but strongly deterring to the target). Rewards are used sparingly to reinforce consistent cooperators. As rounds progress, enforcement will push defectors to switch to cooperation at times, but toward the end (final few rounds) a modest decline in contributions is likely (endgame effect), with fewer rewards and fewer punishments because punishment is costly and payoffs from enforcement late in the game are lower.

I will simulate 14 rounds. For each round I explain the expected motivations, then show the contribution vector (in the exact avatar order) and list only non-zero punishment/reward actions as tuples (source, target, unit), where positive unit = reward, negative = punishment. Punishment/reward is sparse and modest in magnitude throughout.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game where each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot or withdraws them all (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally among the 12 players, so individual payoffs depend on how many others contribute. After contributions are revealed, players can send costly positive rewards to specific others (1 coin from sender adds 1 coin to recipient). Messages are allowed but identification of reward givers is not revealed in the summary, so rewards act as targeted incentives rather than public reputational signals.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds usually see some experimentation and a fair number of cooperators; conditional cooperators will try contributing to see responses.
- Persistent cooperators (those who care about group payoff or reciprocity) will contribute repeatedly and sometimes reward other cooperators.
- Free-riders tend to withdraw privately most rounds; their occasional cooperative moves are often short experiments.
- As the finite horizon approaches, cooperation typically declines because backward induction reduces future incentive to foster reciprocity. So cooperation will drop noticeably in the later rounds.
- Rewards will be sparse and targeted: a few transfers from cooperators to other cooperators to encourage or thank them. Because reward givers remain anonymous, reward use will be conservative and mostly among those who consistently contribute.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round binary public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to a group pot (cooperate) or keep it (defect). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all 13 players, so contributing helps the group but reduces the contributor's private holding that round. After payoffs from the pot are distributed, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the receiver 4 coins. Rewards are anonymous (recipients don't know who sent them).

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds: players are uncertain about others' types. Some will contribute to signal cooperation; others will free-ride to exploit contributors. Because contributions are all-or-nothing and players can't condition within a round, we expect a mix of contributions in round 1.
- Reward can sustain cooperation: cooperative players will sometimes use small, targeted rewards to encourage reciprocators. Because rewards are costly to the sender, they will be used sparingly and targeted at consistent cooperators (and occasionally to encourage new cooperators).
- Mid-game: with occasional mutual rewarding, cooperation can climb or stabilize among a core of reciprocators. Persistent free-riders may remain.
- Endgame: as the horizon approaches, contributions typically decline (endgame effect). Rewards can moderate, but many players reduce cooperation in final rounds because future reciprocity loses value.
- Uncertainty remains about motives: some players appear consistently cooperative, some consistently defect, and a small set may oscillate responding to rewards and previous round behavior. Because rewards are anonymous, they function mainly as an indirect signalling/incentive device, not as direct contingent punishment.

Following these priors, the transcript below simulates a plausible trajectory: a modest cooperative core that uses a few small rewards across rounds to sustain cooperation mid-game, followed by the common endgame decline in contributions. Rewards are sparse and directed toward repeat cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game with 12 players. Each round each player either contributes the whole 20 coins (cooperates) or withdraws them all (defects). The group pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so a cooperating player's contribution helps everyone but is individually costly; defecting yields the private payoff but can lower the public return. After seeing contributions, players may spend coins to punish (cost 1, subtracts 2 from target) or reward (cost 1, adds 1 to target) any other player. Players see only their own round payoff and the amounts they spent/received; they do not see who targeted whom.

Typical dynamics in such a setting: early rounds often show moderate cooperation as social norms are tested; a few punishers may target obvious free-riders to encourage cooperation. If punishment or reward is effective, cooperation rises and stabilizes mid-game. Toward the end (endgame), players have a stronger incentive to defect because there are no future consequences, so cooperation tends to unravel unless punishment remains strong and costly. Chats will be sparse and purposeful (requests, promises, complaints). Punishments and rewards are used sparingly: frequent punishment is costly to the punishers and thus limited; rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperation.

Now the transcript from round 1 through round 26.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game played for a known finite horizon (26 rounds). Each round every player faces a simple binary choice: keep the whole 20 coins (contribute 0) or leave the 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally across the 12 players, so each additional contributor increases everyone's round payoff but at a private cost of 20. That creates the tension: individually it often pays to defect (withdraw), but collectively higher contributions raise everyone’s payoff.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often see a mix of cooperation and defection as players test each other and try to establish norms. Some players (conditional cooperators) will contribute as long as others do; others (selfish or opportunistic types) will defect early to cash in.
- Punishment (costly to the sender and doubly costly to the target) and rewards (cheap encouragement) are available. In experiments, punishment is used sparingly but strategically: cooperators may punish clear free-riders to sustain cooperation. Rewards are used less often but can signal approval.
- Because players cannot identify who punished or rewarded them, punishment is impersonal and creates uncertainty about motives — making retaliation less directed but also less predictable.
- Over a long but finite horizon, cooperation often drifts downward as the end approaches (endgame effect): the last few rounds tend to have more defection because future retaliation or reputation is less relevant.
- Given the supplied profiles (mix of ages, education, countries), expect a heterogeneous mixture: a few steady cooperators (older or more communal profiles), several conditional cooperators who follow group norms, and a handful of persistent defectors. Punishment will appear early to enforce contributions and then become rarer, while chat will be sparse and mostly used for short appeals, calls-out, or explanations.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This PGG pits individual incentives (keep 20 each round) against collective benefit (20 contributed becomes 52 and each player receives 4 per contributor). Individual rationality pushes toward defection (0 contribution) because every 20 contributed returns only 4 to oneself; social efficiency and group payoff increase when many contribute. Because the game is repeated and anonymous, we expect conditional cooperation: many players will start by contributing at moderate-to-high rates, and cooperators may use costly punishment or small rewards to discourage free-riding and to reinforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 per unit) and very damaging to the target (11 per unit), so it will be used sparingly and primarily on persistent defectors early-to-mid game. Rewards are also costly (3 per unit) but provide smaller boosts to targets (4 per unit); these will be used occasionally to encourage stable cooperators.

We also expect an endgame effect: as the known final round approaches, incentives to punish decrease and defection typically rises because there's no future to influence. Thus cooperation should be higher early and mid-game (especially if punishment/reward sustain it) and decay in later rounds, with few punitive actions late in the game.

game_explanation:
This is a finitely repeated public goods game with 14 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20) and the possibility to reward others after payoffs are redistributed. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Each round, contributing 20 benefits the group: the pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. However, the private monetary cost is immediate (20) and the per-person return of one contribution depends on how many players contribute. With 13 players, a single 20 contributes 52 to the pot and yields 52/13 = 4 to each player — well under the 20 cost. So, individually, free-riding (contributing 0) is often the higher short-run payoff unless enough others contribute.
- Because players can reward after redistribution, there is an incentive for conditional cooperators to reward contributors (encouraging cooperation) and to withhold rewards from free-riders. But rewards are costly (3 to the sender per unit, 4 to the receiver), and players typically reward sparingly.
- Early rounds often show some cooperation as players try to establish norms or experiment. Over repeated rounds, unless rewards reliably compensate contributors, cooperation tends to drift down as free-riding pays off. Toward the final rounds, many participants anticipate the endgame and reduce cooperation further (backward induction), though some may still contribute out of intrinsic pro-sociality or reciprocity.
- Uncertainties: we don’t know each player's exact preferences (besides demographic priors). Some players (e.g., those with postgraduate education or stable employment) may lean toward conditional cooperation; others (younger, part-time, or unemployed) may be more variable. Because rewards cannot identify senders in summaries, reputational signaling through rewards is limited to the knowledge that someone rewarded contributors—still, consistent reward patterns can sustain cooperation for a while.
- Overall expected path: a moderate level of cooperation in early rounds, gradual decline as free-riding becomes visible, occasional small rebounds when contributors are rewarded, sparse reward activity concentrated on a handful of cooperators, and lower cooperation toward the end.

This is a 14-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round players decide privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (contribute = 20) or keep it (contribute = 0). The sum contributed is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all players; after payoff shares are revealed, players can spend coins to reward others (3 coins cost to the sender, 4 coins to the receiver per reward unit). Because rewards are the only costly interpersonal action available and source identity of received rewards is not shown in players' summaries, rewards are typically used sparingly to signal appreciation for cooperation and try to sustain contributions. Early rounds often show a reasonably high cooperation rate (people initially cooperate), some players free-ride consistently, and conditional cooperators react to others' behavior. Mid-game there tends to be fluctuation: cooperation can drop if free-riding looks profitable, but targeted rewards can partially restore cooperation. In the last rounds, backward induction and end-game effects often reduce cooperation — some persistent cooperators continue to contribute out of principle, while many exploit the last round. Overall expect: many cooperators in early rounds, moderate decline mid-game with occasional rebounds when cooperators are rewarded, and a marked drop in contributions in the final round. Rewards will be few, usually 1-unit gestures, aimed at visibly cooperative peers.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 12 players against a recurring social dilemma for 26 rounds. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the common pot or withdraws all 20 for private use. The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so contributing helps the group but is costly to the individual unless many others also contribute. Because contributions are visible after the decision and players can reward others (at a one-to-one cost), rewards become the only instrument to encourage cooperation: players can boost cooperators' payoffs, but they cannot directly punish. Typical dynamics here are:

- Early rounds: uncertainty and some experimentation. A handful of players try contributing; they may reward each other to signal cooperation.
- Middle rounds: players respond to evidence of cooperation. If contribution rates appear low, many will free-ride; a few persistent cooperators may keep contributing and be sporadically rewarded.
- Late rounds: with a known finite horizon (26 rounds), cooperation tends to unravel — many stop contributing as the end approaches. Rewards may continue from a few altruistic players, but they usually aren't enough to sustain broad cooperation.
- Communication is sparse and focused: short calls for cooperation, a few expressions of frustration or thanks, and occasional declarations of intent. Rewarding is also sparse and typically directed at visible cooperators.

I expect a modest start (several contributors), gradual decline through the middle rounds, occasional attempts to re-start cooperation by rewarding, and finally near-complete defection in the final rounds.

- Early rounds: uncertainty and some experimentation. A handful of players try contributing; they may reward each other to signal cooperation.
- Middle rounds: players respond to evidence of cooperation. If contribution rates appear low, many will free-ride; a few persistent cooperators may keep contributing and be sporadically rewarded.
- Late rounds: with a known finite horizon (26 rounds), cooperation tends to unravel — many stop contributing as the end approaches. Rewards may continue from a few altruistic players, but they usually aren't enough to sustain broad cooperation.
- Communication is sparse and focused: short calls for cooperation, a few expressions of frustration or thanks, and occasional declarations of intent. Rewarding is also sparse and typically directed at visible cooperators.

I expect a modest start (several contributors), gradual decline through the middle rounds, occasional attempts to re-start cooperation by rewarding, and finally near-complete defection in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 14 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20) and post-decision punishment/reward. Each 20 contributed yields 20 * 2.6 = 52 total to be split among 13 players, so each contributor gets only 4 back from their own contribution — a private loss of 16 coins per contribution absent sanctions or rewards. That sets up a tension: individually the dominant move is to free-ride (give 0), but groups can sustain cooperation early through social preferences and selective use of rewards/punishments. Because players see only aggregated outcomes and cannot identify who punished them later, sanctions are anonymous in the summary but still deter defectors if used enough.

Typical dynamics I expect:
- Early rounds: some optimism and conditional cooperators lead to moderate contribution levels (many players give 20). A few consistent free-riders appear immediately.
- Middle rounds: cooperators react to persistent free-riding with occasional, targeted punishments (usually single-unit punishments), and sometimes small rewards to reinforce cooperation. Punishment and reward actions are sparse because both are costly.
- Late rounds: contributions decay (end-game effect) as the shadow of the future weakens; punishment becomes rarer because its deterrent value is low in the final rounds. Rewards might persist a little between reciprocating cooperators.
- Uncertainty: players don't know exact motives of others; heterogenous priors (age, employment) make some players more likely to be conditional cooperators (e.g., older or career-profiles) and others likely to free-ride.

Now the play-by-play transcript.

game_explanation:
This is a classic repeated public goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each round players effectively choose a binary action: keep the 20 coins privately (defect) or leave the 20 coins in the shared pot (cooperate). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split across all players, so cooperation benefits the group but is costly to the individual in isolation. Because the horizon is finite and known (26 rounds), backward-induction logic predicts defection, but human subjects typically start with substantial cooperation and respond to social incentives (messages, rewards, punishments). Punishment (costly to the sender, punitive to the target) can sustain cooperation early and mid-game; rewards can also encourage cooperators. Toward the end of a finite game, cooperation tends to unravel as the incentive to sanction declines and players prioritize immediate private payoffs.

Given the player profiles and typical behavior patterns:
- A group of older / highly educated avatars (RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, DUCK, MOOSE) will lean toward cooperation and pro-social messaging and will use small sanctions or rewards early to sustain contributions.
- Some avatars with "sloth/unemployed/younger" priors (SLOTH, CHICKEN, PARROT, DOG) are more likely to defect occasionally early on; they may switch to cooperating if punished or rewarded, then revert as the end approaches.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly (1–2 units) in typical experiments; messages are sparse and usually appeals to fairness or short phrases of thanks/requests.

I predict cooperation will start moderately high, improve after a few enforcement actions, stay high through mid-game, then gradually decline in later rounds as endgame effects appear. Punishments will be targeted and infrequent (used to deter persistent free-riders); rewards will be occasional signals of appreciation. Chat will be short: a few appeals to contribute early, thanks when cooperation happens, and occasional grumbles near the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and binary contribution choices each round (contribute all 20 coins to the public pot or withdraw all 20 to keep privately). The public pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally among all players, so each cooperator bears the private cost of contributing but helps everyone through the multiplied return. After contributions are revealed, players may punish (cost 1 to sender, -2 to recipient per unit) or reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to recipient per unit). Messages can be sent at any time. Players cannot be identified as punishers/rewarders in the summary.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others’ behavior. Many players will try cooperation to establish a norm, some will free-ride immediately. Early cooperative signals (few messages, a couple of small rewards) and limited punishments are likely.
- Midgame: norms tend to stabilize. If defectors persist, norm-enforcing players (those willing to pay the cost of punishing) will punish sparingly to deter free-riding. Rewards may be used occasionally to reinforce cooperation.
- Late game (approaching round 26): endgame effects often cause cooperation to decline because future retaliation is less effective. Punishment may decline as well (players are less willing to pay costs when the long-term benefits drop), and more players may defect.
- Uncertainty: we don’t know exact preferences. Some players will be conditional cooperators (cooperate if many others do), some will be consistent cooperators, and some will opportunistically defect. Punishment and reward usage will be sparse and targeted rather than widespread.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 14-round binary public goods game with an additional costly reward stage. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment to the public pot or keep it. A single contributed 20 becomes 52 total (20 * 2.6) in the pot and, divided among 13 players, yields 4 coins back to each player per contribution. So each contributor pays 20 and gets 4 from each contributor including themselves, meaning the private net effect of contributing one’s 20 is typically a loss (roughly -16) while socially it increases the group's total. Given that, the dominant private strategy is to free-ride (contribute 0) while the group-optimal outcome would be many contributors.

The reward stage lets players spend 3 coins per reward unit to give 4 coins per unit to a chosen peer. This creates a tool to reward cooperators and (less commonly) to punish defectors indirectly by withholding reward. Rewards are anonymous to recipients (they see amounts but not who sent them), so reciprocal reputation-building is limited but still possible via expectations and repeat interactions.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty and social norms generate a moderate level of contribution (some players start cooperative). A few players (those with stronger pro-social priors, older or more experienced) will contribute more often.
- Rewarding will be used sparingly and targeted: small 1-unit rewards from cooperators to other cooperators to signal approval and try to sustain cooperation. Because rewards are costly, they will be rare.
- Midgame: conditional cooperation and small reciprocal reward patterns may sustain a core of contributors, but overall contributions tend to drift down as free-riding is individually attractive.
- Endgame: backward induction and the known finite horizon reduce incentives to sustain cooperation; contributions and reward activity typically collapse toward the final rounds.
- Uncertainty about other players' types and the anonymity of reward receipt prevents strong enforcement; expect gradual decline in contributions, a few targeted reward acts, and near-zero reward activity in the final round.
