Rewards will be used sparingly to acknowledge cooperators.

Players update slightly from observed summaries. Some reciprocation begins between early cooperators; a few who saw net gains from others' contributions may try contributing. Rewards remain targeted and small.

Some contributors remain consistent (established cooperators). A few players who received or expected rewards continue cooperating; others experiment with free-riding. Rewarding remains modest and mostly mutual among cooperators.

As the cooperative pattern becomes clearer, a small core of players emerges. Some players stop contributing when they see many free-riders. Rewarding is used to reinforce the emerging core of cooperators.

Midgame begins. Some contributors remain steady; others start to defect more often as the private incentive to keep 20 looks attractive. Rewards continue but are selective and small, reinforcing those who still contribute.

A slight decline in contributions as free-riding spreads. Players who value cooperation still contribute, and the occasional reward is used to stabilize that behavior. Rewards are usually 1 unit because larger expenditures are costly.
Reciprocal links among cooperators keep a small cluster contributing, but overall the group is drifting downward. Rewarding continues but is frugal.
Cooperation thins; players anticipate fewer returns from the pot and may conserve funds for rewards only if they believe it will influence future giving. Expect few reward acts.
Further erosion of cooperation continues. A committed minority (often the same people: GORILLA, MOOSE, OWL, CHICKEN) still contributes occasionally. Rewards remain focused on those few.
Approaching the final third of the game, incentives to free-ride strengthen further. Some steady cooperators give up; reward use declines.
As players think about the endgame, contributions become quite sparse. A few players (who may be intrinsically pro-social) still contribute. Small mutual rewards persist among that tiny set.

Penultimate round: most players anticipate the end and cease contributing. Only a tiny remnant of cooperators remain. Rewards are very sparse, largely symbolic.

Final round: standard backward-induction logic predicts minimal cooperation — most players defect because there are no future rounds to influence. Rewarding is pointless for future influence and is typically zero.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round online public goods game with 13 players. Each round each player decides whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to a common pot (0 or 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally across all players. A single player's 20-coin contribution yields only 2.6*20/13 ≈ 4 coins back to that same player, so contributing is socially efficient but individually costly (private return < private cost). After the contribution stage, players may spend reward units: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 4 coins — a mechanism that can sustain cooperation by privately penalizing or (here) selectively rewarding players.

Key strategic forces and likely dynamics:
- Free-riding temptation is strong (contribute = net private loss) so without incentives cooperation tends to fall.
- Positive rewards allow cooperators to be compensated and encourage conditional cooperators to keep contributing, but rewards are privately costly so they are used sparingly.
- Because players do not see others' choices before deciding, contributions are simultaneous; players form priors from profiles and past behavior.
- Finite horizon (14 rounds) creates an end-game tendency: cooperation often unravels in later rounds as incentives to reward or punish decline.
- Expect modest cooperation early on, intermittent small rewards aimed at cooperators, and gradual decline in contributions toward the last rounds, with sparse rewarding throughout.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript consistent with these incentives and with player priors. Contributions are either 0 or 20. Reward tuples are (SOURCE, TARGET, UNIT), and only non-zero rewards are listed.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round binary public goods game with 12 players. Each round each person either keeps their 20 coins (contribute 0) or puts the full 20 into the group pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, and players can then pay coins to reward specific other players (1 coin spent = 1 coin received by the target). Players see everyone's contributions each round before choosing rewards, but summaries only show each player's own net, sent, and received rewards (not who sent them).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds usually show some initial trust: a core of conditional cooperators contribute while others test or free-ride.
- Rewards are used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior (small positive transfers to contributors) rather than costly, large-scale punishment. Because rewards are visible and assigned after contributions, cooperators will often give a small bonus to other cooperators; defectors rarely reward.
- Over repeated rounds, cooperation typically drifts downward as defectors persist and conditional cooperators reduce contributions to avoid being exploited. Small, targeted rewards can slow decay among a committed core but rarely sustain full cooperation without broad participation.
- With a known finite horizon (26 rounds), end-game effects mean contribution rates tend to fall, especially in the last few rounds, as forward-looking players stop rewarding and stop contributing.
- Social heterogeneity in the group (education, age, names) produces a mix: a stable pro-social core (several postgraduate/experienced players) will contribute most of the game; several others will mostly free-ride or only occasionally contribute; rewards remain sparse and targeted.

Now the play-by-play transcript from round 1 through round 26.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game with 12 players. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them privately (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so collective contributions raise everyone's payoff but the individually dominant move is to withdraw (free-ride). Players can punish (cost 1 to sender, −2 to target per unit) and reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target per unit) others after seeing contributions. Messages are allowed but sparse. Because the multiplier divided by group size gives a low marginal per-capita return (<1), we expect persistent incentives to defect. Yet social preferences and enforcement (punishment, occasional rewards) can sustain cooperation early and in the middle of the game. Over many real PGGs, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions as players test each other, some cooperative signaling and a few enforcement actions.
- Middle rounds: if enough players enforce normatively (punish defectors, reward cooperators), cooperation can stabilize at high levels.
- Late rounds (endgame): cooperation typically collapses as the incentive to free-ride dominates and punishment becomes less credible (players don't want to spend to punish in the final rounds).

Given the player profiles, I predict some players will be consistent cooperators and/or enforcers (older or more prosocial profiles), some will be occasional punishers, and a few will be stubborn defectors early and late. Chat will be sparse and mostly normative ("please contribute") or reactive ("why did you punish me?"). Punishments and rewards will be used sparingly to enforce cooperation in early/mid rounds and largely disappear by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players across 14 rounds. Each round players choose to contribute either 0 or 20 of their private 20-coin endowment to a group pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so each contributing player effectively adds 52 coins to the pot which increases the per-player share by 4 for each contributor; the per-player share is 4 × (number of contributors). Free-riding (giving 0) keeps the 20 coins privately and still collects the shared return, so in many rounds defecting gives a higher immediate payoff than contributing. After the redistribution, players may use costly punishment (cost 3 to the punisher, -11 to the target) or costly reward (cost 3 to the giver, +4 to the recipient) but cannot be identified as the source of any incoming sanction/reward in their personal summary.

Given these incentives and the 14-round finite horizon, I expect:
- Early rounds: many players will try cooperating (contribute 20) as a social/conditional norm; a few will free-ride. Cooperators will use scarce punishment and occasional rewards to discipline or encourage defectors.
- Middle rounds: contributions will gradually decline as payoffs for defecting remain attractive and the incentive to invest in sanctions weakens. Punishments will be used sparingly and targeted at repeated defectors.
- Endgame: in the last several rounds contributions fall further as players anticipate no future influence of sanctions, so free-riding becomes more common and punishment largely disappears.
- Rewards will be rare and used mostly early to reinforce cooperation; punishment will be fairly sparse and targeted rather than widespread.

The transcript below simulates one plausible path consistent with these tendencies: early majority cooperation with a few defectors who are disciplined; a brief full-cooperation rebound in round 2; then a gradual decline in cooperation and diminishing use of sanctions toward the final rounds. Numbers shown in summaries are per-round: contribution (0 or 20), how much they spent on punishing and rewarding others, how much was deducted from them by punishment, how much they received in rewards, and their net round payoff.

game_explanation:
This is a finite-horizon public goods game with 12 players and repeated interactions over 26 rounds. Each round every player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so full cooperation benefits the group but individual incentives favor withdrawal (free-riding). Because the game is anonymous and finite, we expect these forces to interact as follows:

- Early rounds: Many players will try cooperating (contributing 20) to capture mutual benefits and signal prosocial intent. Some players will "test" the group by free-riding early or remain skeptical; a small, consistent set of free-riders may appear from the start.
- Mid game: The reward stage allows cooperators to support each other. Conditional cooperators will use small positive rewards to reinforce contributors; this can sustain cooperation for a while. Rewards are costly, so they are used sparingly and targeted.
- Late game (endgame): Because the horizon is known (round 26), forward-looking incentives to maintain cooperation unravel. Expect a gradual decline in contributions and reward use, with most players defecting near the last few rounds. A few altruistic or reputation-insensitive players may continue contributing occasionally, but overall contributions will fall.
- Uncertainties: Individual differences (backgrounds, age, employment) provide priors about patience and reciprocity, but anonymity blurs identification. We therefore predict modest initial cooperation, targeted but limited rewarding, a midgame plateau if reward is effective, and a clear decline toward the end.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 anonymous players against the classic tension: each round everyone gets 20 private coins and may either contribute all (20) or nothing (0) to a public pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so contributing is socially efficient (if many contribute everyone is better off) but individually risky (a defector keeps 20 while still sharing the multiplied pot). Because players see peers' contributions and reward activity in the round summary, but cannot link received rewards to specific senders, behavior will reflect conditional cooperation, strategic experimentation, and uncertainty about others' motives.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: a subset will contribute to signal cooperation (especially older/educated or norm-oriented profiles). Those contributors will occasionally reward other contributors with small, costly rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: some free-riders will switch to cooperating if they observe they are being excluded from rewards or want to gain favor; some cooperators will continue rewarding to sustain norms. Rewarding will be sparse and focused on a few reciprocating pairs.
- Late rounds (endgame effects): as the horizon shortens, incentives to free-ride increase. Cooperation typically erodes in the final few rounds; reward use declines as players cease investing in future reciprocity. Because rewards are anonymous, punishment comes via withholding rewards rather than targeted negative sanctions.

Uncertainties:
- Some players are conditional cooperators and will adapt after a round or two; others are stubborn free-riders. The presence of rewards can prolong cooperation but not eliminate endgame collapse.
- Because reward sources are anonymous, players can’t precisely reciprocate to an individual, so reputation-building is noisy; this leads to modest, dispersed rewarding rather than heavy centralized sanctioning.

game_explanation:
This 14-round public goods game pits 13 anonymous players against a standard social dilemma: each round everyone chooses to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so group benefit is larger than private benefit, but any individual who defects (keeps their 20) gains against cooperators. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish or reward others; punishment is costly and reduces the target strongly, reward is costly but benefits the target. Punishment and reward are private (players see amounts but not who acted), creating uncertainty about motives.

Likely dynamics: early rounds usually see fairly high cooperation as players test each other, with sparse rewards to encourage cooperators and occasional punishments aimed at obvious free-riders. Because punishments are costly, they will be used sparingly and primarily against persistent defectors. Over time, some players will respond to punishment by switching to cooperation; however, as the finite horizon approaches, cooperation typically unravels (end-game effect) and defections increase, with punishment and reward largely disappearing near the end. Expect a mixture of mostly-cooperative early rounds with a gradual decline into more widespread defection by rounds 10–14.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, binary contribution choices (either keep all 20 privately = 0 to public, or leave all 20 in the pot = 20 to public), a relatively high multiplication of the public pot (2.9) and an equal split of the public return across all players. Players cannot see others' choices before deciding, can send short chat messages, and can use costly positive rewards after seeing the contributions. The game runs for 26 rounds, so expectations about future reciprocity and final-round selfishness matter.

Likely dynamics:
- A stable "core" of socially inclined players is likely to contribute most rounds and use small positive rewards to sustain cooperation among themselves.
- Some players will free-ride most of the time, withdrawing the 20, because that gives a safe private payoff and anonymity of who rewarded you weakens deterrence of free-riding.
- Rewards will be sparse and mostly positive: cooperators will occasionally reward other cooperators (1 or 2 units) to encourage continued contribution; defectors rarely reward.
- When a cooperator feels exploited they may either stop contributing or increase targeted positive rewards for other cooperators; talk messages will be rare but used to signal intentions or call out selfishness.
- Over the sequence, expect a modest decline in contributions from marginal cooperators, while a core of about 5–7 players continues contributing; occasional experiments (a defector contributing one round) may occur. Rewarding is used sparingly to sustain a cooperative core, not to outright eliminate free-riding.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 14-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and an opportunity to punish or reward after contributions are revealed. Each contributed 20 increases the shared pot so that every player receives +4 (per contributor) after multiplication and equal split — that makes the social return obvious: cooperation benefits the group but each individual who contributes loses 16 relative to free-riding in that round (they give up 20 but receive only 4 for themselves from their own contribution). Because players see round summaries but cannot identify who punished or rewarded them, enforcement is impersonal and costly.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds tend to show moderate-to-high cooperation as many players experiment with contributing, but a few will free-ride (contribute 0) to exploit the public good.
- Cooperators who see free-riders earning more will occasionally punish (costly to punishers) or sometimes reward cooperators; punishment is typically sparse and targeted.
- Punishment tends to reduce subsequent free-riding by imposing steep losses on punished players; reward is used less frequently and usually in small amounts to reinforce cooperation.
- Near the final rounds, the incentive to defect increases (endgame), so we expect more defections in rounds 13–14, with some residual punishment or spite despite the lack of future rounds.
- Overall, cooperation rises and falls in response to observed payoffs and sparse enforcement: many rounds will show broad cooperation with occasional single or few defectors who are then lightly punished.

The transcripts below simulate these likely patterns: a majority of cooperative rounds, targeted and sparing punishment/reward, and a decline in cooperation toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20). Each round players decide simultaneously whether to withdraw all 20 coins (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared contributions are multiplied by 2.9 and divided equally, so cooperating helps the group but is individually costly if many others free-ride. After payoffs are revealed, players may spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the giver 1 and gives the recipient 1). Players see only their own round payoff and reward flows to/from themselves; they do not see who rewarded them or detailed peer choices beyond public contribution totals.

Likely unfolding and incentives:
- Early rounds: uncertainty; many players experiment. Some predisposed cooperators (higher education, older, or otherwise socially inclined profiles) will contribute to signal cooperation, while others test free-riding. Because rewards are costly and only positive, early rewards will be used sparingly to signal appreciation for cooperators and to seed reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players over 26 rounds. Each round each player either keeps all 20 coins (contribute 0) or leaves all 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns only about 0.2417 coins to each player — contributing is socially efficient but individually costly. Because players see only their own round summary and not who rewarded them, social enforcement is noisy: reward can encourage cooperators but cannot directly identify defectors. Typical dynamics: some players will start by cooperating, others will free-ride. Sparse, positive rewards are likely to be used to reinforce contributors; when contributions drop, rewards may temporarily help restore cooperation among a committed subset. Expect initial moderate cooperation, a slide as free-riding spreads, a midgame partial recovery led by a core of cooperators using rewards and brief coordination in chat, and an endgame decline as the horizon approaches (the incentive to defect increases near the final rounds).

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses either to keep their 20 coins or contribute the full 20 to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and then split equally across all players, so each 20-coin contribution returns 20*2.6/13 = 4 coins to each contributor (i.e., contributors get back 4 of the 20 they gave). Individually, contributing is costly (you pay 20 and only get 4 back), so the dominant selfish action is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, if many players contribute, the group does much better overall (all-contribute gives each 52). The reward stage lets players spend 3 coins per reward unit to give 4 coins to another player; rewarding is costly to the giver but increases total group payoff by 1 coin per unit sent. game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 14 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20), and voluntary costly rewards after payoff redistribution. Key forces that will shape play:

- Social preferences and conditional cooperation: many players with higher education and stable employment (CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, OWL, RABBIT, SNAKE, CROCODILE, etc.) are more likely to contribute consistently; others (SLOTH, DUCK, COW) may be more inclined to free-ride. Because contributions are not observable before decisions but are revealed each round, players learn about others' tendencies and adjust.
- Reciprocity via rewards: rewards are costly (3 per unit) but give substantial benefit to the recipient (4 per unit). In practice rewards are used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior (rewarding contributors) rather than to punish defectors.
- Endgame and uncertainty: knowing the game length (14 rounds) creates an incentive to defect in later rounds. Expect contributions and reward activity to be relatively high early/mid-game and decline toward the final rounds.
- Strategic heterogeneity: some players (postgraduate, full-time employed) are priors for stable cooperation; unemployed or not-in-paid-work players may be more variable. Players who start cooperating and receive few rewards may continue cooperating out of intrinsic motivation, while those who see widespread defection will begin to withhold contributions and rewards.

Given these dynamics, I predict an initially high level of cooperation sustained by sparse reciprocal rewards for contributors, followed by gradual unraveling as the horizon approaches. Rewards are reciprocal and limited (mostly 1-unit gestures), concentrated among regular contributors; near the end they taper off and contributions fall.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 anonymous players against a repeated social dilemma for 14 rounds. Each round every player decides whether to contribute their full endowment (20 coins) to a group pot (cooperate) or keep it (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly among all 13 players, so the individual private return from contributing 20 is 20 * 2.6 / 13 ≈ 4.0 (much less than the 20 cost). That makes free-riding attractive in any single round. However, because the game repeats and players can reward others (pay 3 to give 4 to a target per reward unit), cooperation can be supported early if participants expect reciprocity or use rewards to reinforce cooperators.

Expectations and likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' types tends to produce a mix of cooperation and defection. Many with prosocial priors (e.g., higher education, stable employment) will contribute initially; some will withhold to test others.
- Rewards will be sparse and targeted: cooperators who consistently contribute are most likely to get single-unit positive rewards from a few altruistic/reciprocal players. Because rewards cost 3, players use them selectively.
- Mid-game: if free-riding appears and rewards are insufficient, conditional cooperators will reduce contributions. Contributions typically decline over time as players learn others' tendencies.
- Endgame: backward induction and certainty about remaining rounds usually lead most players to defect in the final rounds, so cooperation collapses near the end (last few rounds).
- Individual variation: SLOTH and GORILLA appear inclined to cooperate throughout the early and middle game, and their consistent behavior makes them common reward targets. Some players (e.g., CHICKEN, SNAKE, CROCODILE) are more likely to defect early and remain free-riders; others (PARROT, MOOSE, DOG) may drift either way depending on observed cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and only two contribution choices each round: keep your 20 privately (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the group pot (contribute 20). Each coin left in the pot is multiplied by 2.9 and then split equally among all active players. Individually, leaving 20 in the pot reduces your private return (you forgo 20 but only get back about 4.83), so the one-shot incentive is to withdraw (defect). However, because the game is repeated and players can reward others (at a private cost of 1 per reward unit, which gives the recipient 1), we expect some players to try to sustain cooperation by using rewards to reinforce contributing behavior. Players do not see others' actions before deciding, and they only see their own round summary (their own net payoff, rewards given, and rewards received), so signaling via chat and rewards plays an important role.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players try contributing to establish a cooperative norm; those contributors receive reciprocal rewards from a few others. Free-riders who withdraw initially may be left unrewarded and possibly encouraged to switch.
- Middle rounds: if rewards successfully target cooperators, contribution rates rise and stay moderate-high. If free-riding persists or if contributors feel exploited, rewards will drop and cooperation can erode.
- Late rounds / end-game: forward-looking incentives weaken. Many players will reduce contributions approaching the final rounds, causing a decline in contribution rates. A small set of consistently cooperative players (or those socially motivated) may continue to contribute and sometimes give or receive small rewards.

Uncertainties:
- Who takes initiative with rewards early can tilt the group toward cooperation.
- Some players (by profile) are more likely to cooperate or be consistent (e.g., those with postgraduate degrees or older players who value reciprocity), but individual variation is high.
- Chat messages are sparse and have limited signaling power because players cannot link rewards to identities in the summary, but public chat can still coordinate behavior.

The transcript that follows predicts plausible choices, sparse chat, and modest use of rewards across 26 rounds. Contributors are shown in the exact avatar order provided. Reward lists include only non-zero edges.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game (26 rounds, 12 players) pits private temptation (keep 20) against group benefit (contribute 20 to a pot multiplied by 2.9 and shared). Because contributions are all-or-nothing each round, the strategic choice is binary: contribute (cooperate) or withdraw (defect). Defectors always earn more in the same round than cooperators, so without other mechanisms cooperation tends to erode across a finite horizon. However, the ability to send one-directional rewards (cost 1 to source, +1 to receiver) and occasional chat lets players signal, reciprocate, and try to sustain cooperation. Rewards are costly so they will be used sparingly — typically to reward reliable cooperators or to signal approval of pro-social behavior. Messages will be rare and purposeful (promises, calls-out, coordination).

I expect initial rounds to start relatively cooperative as players test the waters and try to build norms. Midgame will see falling cooperation as free-riding pays off, with a few players continuing to reward cooperators. A small revival is possible if a subset coordinates via chat and rewards, but the finite 26-round horizon will induce unraveling toward the end. Rewards will be used mostly to bolster cooperators (a few 1–3 unit gifts per round), not as mass redistribution. Chat will be sparse: short proposals, complaints, and occasional coordination.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and cheap but imperfect incentives to reward or punish after contributions are revealed. Each round players decide privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so cooperation benefits the group but individual incentives favor free-riding (keeping 20) unless social enforcement or reciprocity changes choices.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players tend to test cooperation norms. A sizable fraction will contribute initially, but a few will free-ride. Because punishments are costly to the punisher (3 coins) and particularly harmful to the target (11 coins per punishment unit), punishments will be used, but sparingly and targeted at repeat defectors. Rewards are also available (cheap to give compared to effect on recipient) and are often used selectively to encourage cooperators.
- Mid-game: conditional cooperators respond to observed group-level cooperation/punishment patterns. Persistent defectors are sometimes targeted and either change behavior or provoke occasional retaliatory or escalated punishment, but most players avoid frequent costly punishment. Rewards help sustain cooperation without continuous costly enforcement.
- Endgame (last rounds): forward-looking incentives decline, so some players experiment with defecting in final rounds. When punishments are less consequential (no future), there may be fewer punishments overall, although some players may still punish on principle. Overall, we expect overall cooperation to be high most rounds, punctuated by a few defectors who are punished occasionally; rewards are infrequent but used to reinforce cooperation.

Uncertainties:
- Who exactly will defect and when is hard to predict; individual profiles give priors but not certainties.
- Punishment and reward decisions are private; players cannot see who targeted them, only aggregate effects on payoffs and amounts used/received, which creates ambiguity about motives and reduces direct retaliation.
- Because punishments are costly to the punisher, enforcement is typically limited to a few moral enforcers rather than broad retaliation.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round players decide privately whether to contribute their whole endowment (20) to a common pot or keep it (0 contributed). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly; punishment and reward occur after returns are revealed. Typical dynamics here: early rounds tend toward conditional cooperation (many players contribute), with occasional free-riding; punishment is used sparingly to sanction obvious defectors and to sustain cooperation, and rewards are used even more sparingly to reinforce cooperation. Because the multiplier (2.6) shared among 13 players yields a relatively small per-person return from a single contribution, pure self-interest favors free-riding. Knowing the game has a fixed finite horizon (14 rounds) encourages a decline in cooperation toward the end (an “endgame” effect). Given the player profiles (many college-educated and employed participants mixed with a few younger/unemployed profiles), I predict fairly high cooperation early, occasional targeted sanctions for visible defectors, and a gradual erosion of cooperation in the final few rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round binary public goods game with 12 players. Each round each player either leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally. After payoffs are computed, players may spend coins to punish (cost 1 per unit to sender, deducts 2 per unit from target) or to reward (cost 1 per unit to sender, gives 1 per unit to target). Players do not see individual punish/reward decisions, only their own round summary. The game is finite (26 rounds), so strategic uncertainty and reciprocity motives change over time.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: Many players try cooperation (contribute 20) to test mutuality. Cooperators earn less privately than defectors unless enough others contribute, so opportunistic defection is tempting.
- Middle rounds: If persistent free-riding appears (same players defect repeatedly), cooperators face erosion of trust and contributions tend to fall. Sparse punishment by cooperators may try to discipline repeat defectors; punishment is costly and used sparingly.
- Endgame: As the horizon approaches, the incentive to avoid punishments weakens (backward induction), so contributions often decline further. Some targeted rewards late in the game might encourage brief re-cooperation, but we expect overall decline in contributions across rounds unless punishment is strong and frequent.
- Uncertainties: Players cannot identify who punished them, so reputation signals are noisy. Messages can encourage cooperation but have limited enforcement power; punishment is the main costly enforcement mechanism.

Transcript follows the strict scaffold below. I will keep chat sparse and punish/reward sparse and purposeful. Contributions are shown in the given avatar order. Punishment/reward tuples list only non-zero actions; positive units = rewards, negative units = punishments.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 anonymous players against the classic tension: contribute 20 of your 20 coins to a shared pot (which is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly) or keep your 20 privately. Contributing is collectively efficient but individually risky: if others free-ride you lose relative to them. Because the game lasts a known 14 rounds, we expect a mix of strategies: some players behave as steady cooperators, some as persistent free-riders, and a few as conditional cooperators who mirror the group's recent behavior. The opportunity to spend coins to reward others (3 cost -> 4 benefit per reward unit) allows cooperators to bolster each other, but rewards are costly and usually used sparingly. Early rounds normally show higher contributions (testing trust), midgame sees some decline as free-riding appears, and endgame often shows an additional drop as incentives to build reputation disappear. Given the player profiles, I predict a small core of consistent cooperators (e.g., PARROT, MOOSE, OWL, COW), a group of intermittent contributors (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, CHICK), and a set leaning to defect (SLOTH, DOG, RABBIT, SNAKE, CROCODILE). Rewards will be occasional, targeted at visible cooperators to sustain cooperation, and very sparse overall.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each round every player either leaves the whole 20 in the shared pot (contributes 20) or withdraws it for private use (contributes 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally. After redistribution, players can spend small amounts to punish (cost 1 to source, -2 to target) or reward (cost 1 to source, +1 to target). Players see only their own round summary (their net, what they spent on punishing/rewarding, what was deducted from them, and what reward they received), not who did the actions. Because the game is long (26 rounds), we usually see early exploration of cooperative norms, some enforcement (punish/reward) to stabilize cooperation, then an endgame tendency toward more private appropriation as the incentive to enforce declines. Punishment tends to be sparse (costly and anonymous) and rewards are usually occasional affirmations. Conditional cooperators often follow the group: if many cooperate, cooperation tends to stay high; if defecting spreads, cooperation collapses. Messaging is usually sparse and used to encourage or scold.

Below is a plausible transcript of the full game (round-by-round), showing sparse chat, contributions (0 or 20 for each player in the exact avatar order), punishment/reward edges as tuples (source, target, unit) where negative unit = punishment, positive unit = reward, and round summaries as seen by each player.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and a reward stage after contributions. Each round each player chooses either to contribute their full endowment (20) or keep it (0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so contributing helps the group but is privately costly; the dominant one-shot incentive is to free-ride. The reward stage (cost 3 per reward unit to giver, benefit 4 to receiver) gives players a tool to reinforce cooperation by selectively rewarding contributors. Because rewards are sparse and costly, expect them to be used mainly to encourage consistent cooperators or to welcome turnarounds, rather than to punish systematically. Early rounds typically show moderate-to-high cooperation as norms form; if a few players free-ride and are not rewarded, some conditional cooperators may withhold contributions in later rounds. With rewards available, cooperation can be sustained through the middle rounds, but an "endgame" effect is likely: in the final round many will defect to grab the private benefit, producing a drop in contributions. Throughout, rewards will be used sparingly (a few 1-unit gestures each round), usually directed at contributors and occasional new cooperators, and players will only see aggregate reward totals in summaries (they won’t see who rewarded them), which reduces targeted retaliation but still allows reputational incentives across rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game among 12 players. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the group pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute 0). The shared contributions are multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so when many people contribute, everyone benefits; when few contribute, defectors (who keep 20 privately) often earn more that round. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (cost 1 to sender, -2 to target) or reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target). Players cannot trace who punished/rewarded them after the round, and players see only their own net round payoff and their own punishment/reward activity.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties
- Early rounds: many players will try cooperating (contribute 20) to see mutual benefit; a few (SLOTH, SNAKE, CROCODILE) are more likely to defect early. Because defectors benefit immediately, cooperators may use sparse punishment to signal disapproval.
- Middle rounds: some conditional cooperators will switch if defection persists; punishment is used sparingly (costly to sender), rewards are occasionally used to encourage consistent cooperators. Group-level cooperation may remain moderate but tends to erode if many free-ride repeatedly.
- Endgame: as the horizon approaches (final rounds), backward induction and the lack of future retaliation usually cause more defection. In the final round players often withdraw privately (defect), since there is no future to enforce cooperation.

Given the player profiles, I predict conservatives of cooperation from GORILLA, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, DOG, and DUCK for many early rounds; SLOTH, SNAKE, and CROCODILE are more likely to defect early and intermittently. Punishment and reward actions are sparse and targeted to defectors or to signal appreciation to stable cooperators. Chat is also sparse and typically short appeals or acknowledgements.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with ten players who each decide each round whether to contribute their entire 20-coin private holding to a shared pot or keep it. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all ten players, so the per-contributor return is 3.8 coins times the number of contributors. When many people contribute, contributors can earn more than 20 (because 3.8*k can exceed 20), but when few contribute the per-contributor return falls well below 20. Players see only round-length summary payoffs (their own and everyone else's), not individual choices before deciding, but they can communicate by chat.

Likely unfolding: the game typically starts with a fair amount of cooperation (promises and reciprocation), so early rounds show many contributions. However, because defecting (keeping 20) often yields a higher round payoff than contributing when others contribute, free-riding is tempting and tends to spread once noticed. Conditional cooperators (those who pledged or tended to reciprocate) will reduce contributions when they see free-riding; strategic or low-trust players will defect early and consistently. Given a finite 15-round horizon, contributions often decline over time, sometimes collapsing to near-zero in later rounds. Occasional players may keep contributing out of stubbornness or fairness even when it becomes personally costly.

game_explanation:
This is a standard linear public goods game with 15 players. Each round every player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. Because the per-player marginal return on one coin contributed is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 < 1, contributing is privately dominated (you lose on each coin you contribute), but it benefits the group as a whole. Players therefore face a tension: the group best outcome is high contributions, but individual incentives push toward free-riding.

The reward stage allows costly positive transfers (each reward unit costs sender 4 and gives the recipient 5). Rewards can sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors, but they are costly to provide and rare in real experiments. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they may start more cooperatively (conditional cooperators) and then gradually reduce contributions if others free-ride. Given the player profiles (a mix of older/retired, full-time employed, unemployed, and younger participants), I expect an initial moderate level of contributions with a few generous contributors. Rewards will be sparse and targeted at consistently higher contributors (not punitive), so they will modestly slow the decline of contributions but not stop it entirely. Uncertainties include individual preferences (some players are more pro-social), strategic experimentation, and small random variation in how people respond to feedback.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a group benefit: any coin contributed is pooled, multiplied by 3.7, and split equally among all active players. With 15 players the personal return on one coin contributed is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.25, well below 1, so individually each player is better off keeping coins. However, if many contribute, everyone benefits collectively — so the social optimum is high contributions. The reward stage lets players spend remaining coins to give positive transfers to others (each reward unit costs 4 to the giver and gives 5 to the recipient). Because rewards are costly and anonymous (recipients don't know who rewarded them), they are used sparingly in practice: to thank clear cooperators or to try to sustain cooperation, not as a frequent mechanism.

Given the player profiles, I expect an initial mix: several educated, full-time employed players with pro-social priors (DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, OWL, CROCODILE, MONKEY, COW) start relatively high, while a few more conservative or low-effort players (SLOTH especially, and some part-time/less invested players) contribute little. Early rounds will show modest positive reciprocity among cooperators via one or two small rewards, which stabilizes cooperation for a few rounds. Over repeated rounds, free-riding pressure and the costliness of rewarding slowly erode contributions, so contributions drift downward across rounds; rewards remain infrequent and targeted to a handful of reliably high contributors.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 15 anonymous participants against the tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and collective benefits (contribute to a shared pot that is multiplied and split). The individual return per coin contributed is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247, so contributing reduces a player's private holdings on average; the socially efficient outcome would have much higher contributions, but the one-shot Nash equilibrium is to contribute 0. In repeated or iterated settings, typical dynamics are: initial moderate cooperation (people test others by contributing some amount), then conditional adjustments — cooperators reduce contributions if they see free-riding, while punishments and rewards can sustain cooperation by making free-riding costly or good behavior rewarding. Punishment is harsher on targets than it costs the punisher (sending one punishment unit costs 4 and removes 7 from the target), so targeted punishments can deter persistent free-riding but are used sparingly because they are costly. Rewards are also used sparingly (cost 4 to giver, +5 to receiver). Because players don't know how many rounds remain and can't identify who punished/rewarded them by name in the in-game summaries, behavior will be driven by observed summaries and a mix of reciprocity, fairness preferences, and occasional strategic enforcement.

Across nine rounds I predict:
- Round 1: Moderate average contributions as players "feel out" the group, few rewards for high contributors.
- Rounds 2–4: A couple of low contributors get targeted punishment from several cooperators; punishment yields small compliance increases. Some cooperators begin to reduce contributions if they see free-riding.
- Middle rounds: Contributions drift down overall, with occasional targeted rewards to stabilize high contributors and occasional punishment to discourage persistent defectors.
- Final rounds (8–9): Uncertainty about horizon can produce a mix: some players "cash out" by lowering contributions, while a subset maintain relatively high contributions expecting reciprocity; punishments continue but remain sparse.

game_explanation:
This public goods game is a repeated 15-round interaction where each player repeatedly faces the same trade-off: keep their 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or put the full 20 into the shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally across the 10 players, so a single player's contribution returns 1.9*20/10 = 3.8 coins to each player (including the contributor). That means contributing is socially efficient (group total rises) but individually costly: a contributor loses 20 and gets back 3.8, so contributing reduces the individual's pre-punishment payoff by 16.2 relative to keeping their 20.

Because players can punish after seeing contributions, cooperation can be sustained: a few reliable punishers can deter defectors by imposing costly fines (each punishment unit costs the sender 4 and reduces the target by 8). However, punishment itself is costly and may be used sparingly. Early rounds typically see higher cooperation due to uncertainty and reciprocal motives; persistent defectors attract targeted punishment from a small subset of players (here, PARROT and CHICK emerge as active punishers). Toward the end (final rounds), punishment incentives decline because future retaliation is no longer possible, so free-riding tends to increase.

Uncertainties include: whether occasional defectors will be punished enough to change behavior, whether punishers will sustain the cost of enforcement, and whether a few players will defect strategically near the end. Expect an initial wave of cooperation with targeted, low-intensity punishment of repeat defectors, occasional return to cooperation after punishment, and some decline in contributions and punishment frequency in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual self-interest against a group benefit. Each round players choose whether to place their whole 20-coin endowment into a shared pot (cooperate) or keep it (defect). The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among 10 players, so any coin contributed yields only 0.19 coins back to the contributor — contributing is socially efficient but individually costly. Punishment allows players to impose fines on others at personal cost (4 coins per punishment unit sent, 8 coins deducted per unit received). Because the game lasts 15 rounds and choices are simultaneous, we'll see a mix of motives:

- Early rounds: some players test norms. A few clear cooperators will contribute, and a few free-riders will refrain. Early punishments are used sparingly to signal that free-riding will be sanctioned.
- Middle rounds: if punishment successfully disciplines defectors, cooperation rises and punishment frequency drops. A core of contributers (often the more pro-social or invested players) sustains cooperation.
- Endgame (last rounds): incentives to punish weaken and some players revert to free-riding, so cooperation tends to decline. Punishment tends to be scarcer in final rounds, except occasional emotional retaliation.

Uncertainties: individual tendencies (age, occupation, and profile hints) are noisy priors — some players (e.g., PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, GORILLA) are modeled as more pro-social and more likely to punish; others (SLOTH, DUCK, DOG, CHICK) are more likely to defect at times. I predict modest, targeted punishment early to enforce cooperation, a sustained high-cooperation middle, and a decline near the end.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately decides whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and shared equally, so each coin contributed returns 1.9/10 = 0.19 coins to each player. Because the marginal per-capita return (0.19) is well below 1, a strictly self-interested player maximizes their own round payoff by not contributing (free-riding) regardless of others' choices. However, social preferences, conditional cooperation, and reputational/messaging dynamics can produce positive contributions early on or in response to appeals, and players can infer who contributed from the round payoff vector (contributors receive 20 less than non-contributors given the same pot).

Given these incentives and the 15-round finite horizon:
- Expect some initial cooperation from a subset of players (those predisposed to pro-social behavior), especially if they signal intention in chat.
- Once free-riding is visible (through payoffs that reveal who contributed), conditional cooperators will often reduce contributions in subsequent rounds. Expect overall contributions to decline or fluctuate.
- A few players (especially those more socially motivated or consistent) may continue to contribute sporadically. In the last round, classic game-theoretic reasoning predicts universal defection (no one contributes), although some players might still contribute out of spite or habit.
- Messaging can temporarily coordinate cooperation but tends not to fully overcome the incentive to free-ride across many rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with costly punishment. Each round each player can either contribute their full endowment (20) to the public pot or keep it (0). The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. After seeing payoffs, players may spend coins to punish others (each punishment unit costs 4 to the sender and removes 8 from the target). Players see round summaries including who punished whom and the net outcomes.

Likely dynamics: early rounds typically show a mix of cooperation and free-riding because players cannot see choices before contributing. When defectors are punished, many switch to cooperating in subsequent rounds; sustained punishment helps maintain high cooperation, but punishers pay costs themselves. Because punishment is costly and the game has a known finite horizon (15 rounds), punishment will be used sparingly and mainly as a corrective measure after clear defections. Expect cooperation to rise quickly once defectors are punished; occasional experimentation/last-round defection can reoccur, and punishment frequency will decline in fully cooperative phases and in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-player repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment and rewarding. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can spend coins to reward (cost 4 to giver, +5 to receiver) or punish (cost 4 to giver, -7 to receiver). Players do not see who targeted them. Because contributions benefit the whole group, the selfish incentive is to free-ride (contribute less) while still receiving the public share. However, rewards can reinforce cooperation and punishments can discipline low contributions. Typical dynamics: initial modest cooperation with some variation, sparse use of rewards to encourage high contributors and sparse punishments directed at persistently low contributors. Punishment and reward are costly to send, so they are used sparingly. Over rounds contributions may drift down absent strong or consistent sanctions, but occasional targeted punishments and reciprocal rewards can stabilize moderate cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with ten players, each choosing either to keep 20 coins or contribute 20 coins to a public pot each round. Every 20 contributed raises the group pot by 38 coins (because the pot is multiplied by 1.9), which is then split equally among the 10 players. Each player's private return from contributing (0.19 × 20 = 3.8) is far smaller than the private cost (20), so the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (keep 20). However, if everyone contributes, the group benefit is large — everyone ends up better off than if everyone free-rides.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often see some cooperation (people testing whether others will contribute). Conditional cooperators (those who match others' behavior) will try to coordinate via chat; selfish types will free-ride.
- When contributors observe that free-riders earned more in a round, they tend to reduce contributions in subsequent rounds; this causes cooperation to decline over time.
- Occasional short-lived recoveries are possible if a few players try to coordinate again through chat, but unless the group enforces cooperation, the endgame usually shows unraveling: many players defecting toward 0 contributions.
- Uncertainty about others' types, and the fact the game has a finite known horizon (15 rounds), makes late-round cooperation less likely. Expect sparse, targeted chat attempts to coordinate early/mid game and little chat late-game.

reflection:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with ten players, each choosing either to keep 20 coins or contribute 20 coins to a public pot each round. Every 20 contributed raises the group pot by 38 coins (because the pot is multiplied by 1.9), which is then split equally among the 10 players. Each player's private return from contributing (0.19 × 20 = 3.8) is far smaller than the private cost (20), so the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (keep 20). However, if everyone contributes, the group benefit is large — everyone ends up better off than if everyone free-rides.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often see some cooperation (people testing whether others will contribute). Conditional cooperators (those who match others' behavior) will try to coordinate via chat; selfish types will free-ride.
- When contributors observe that free-riders earned more in a round, they tend to reduce contributions in subsequent rounds; this causes cooperation to decline over time.
- Occasional short-lived recoveries are possible if a few players try to coordinate again through chat, but unless the group enforces cooperation, the endgame usually shows unraveling: many players defecting toward 0 contributions.
- Uncertainty about others' types, and the fact the game has a finite known horizon (15 rounds), makes late-round cooperation less likely. Expect sparse, targeted chat attempts to coordinate early/mid game and little chat late-game.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot public goods game where each round players privately choose how many of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot. The pot is amplified (×3.7) and split equally, so contributing benefits the group but is individually costly. After contributions are revealed, players may spend costly reward units (cost 4 to giver, +5 to receiver) to reward others; rewards are observable in totals but not traceable to a giver. Players do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation: many players contribute a positive share (conditional cooperators), while a minority free-ride or contribute little.
- Because contributions benefit everyone, conditional cooperators tend to match observed group behavior in later rounds. If average contributions drop, cooperation drifts downward across rounds.
- Rewards are used sparingly: because each unit is costly to the giver, players tend to reward only clear high contributors or occasionally reciprocate perceived kindness. Rewards can stabilize cooperation if visible and interpreted as approval, but anonymous source of rewards weakens targeted reciprocity.
- Uncertainties: players don’t know the horizon (how many rounds remain), cannot identify who rewarded them, and must balance present cost of rewarding vs. hoped-for higher returns in future rounds. Heterogeneous priors (age, employment, education) can produce different social preferences: some players (e.g., those with postgraduate or retired status) may be more consistently cooperative and more likely to reward, while others (unemployed, younger) might contribute less.

Given these priors, we expect an initial cluster of mid-to-high contributors, a few low contributors, sparse positive rewards targeted at the highest contributors, and a gradual decline in average contributions over the nine rounds as conditional cooperators react to observed average behavior.

game_explanation:
This 15-round public goods game pits individual incentives to free-ride against group incentives to cooperate. Each player chooses whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.9, and evenly split among the 10 players each round. If few players contribute, contributors typically earn less than non-contributors (because non-contributors keep their 20 plus share of the pot), so the temptation to defect is strong. Communication is allowed and can support coordination, but messages are public and players cannot condition decisions on unseen individual choices within a round. Early rounds often feature experimentation or attempts to build cooperation; as players observe peers' round payoffs they infer whether others contributed and may adjust strategy. Over repeated rounds, expect some conditional cooperators to contribute early, a steady drift toward more free-riding as defectors are rewarded, occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation via chat, and mixed behavior in the final round (some may contribute out of reciprocity or fairness, though self-interest predicts defection).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with 15 players, 20 coins per person each round, a multiplication factor of 3.7 on the group pot, and a punishment/reward stage after redistribution. Key incentives:
- Contributing increases the group pot which is split equally, so each coin contributed yields 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 of a coin back to each player (much less than the private cost of 1), so self-interest favors free-riding but group interest favors higher contributions.
- Punishment (cost 4 to sender, -7 to target) and reward (cost 4 to sender, +5 to target) allow peers to enforce norms. Because both are costly to send, expect sparse use — usually to signal disapproval of low contributors or to encourage high contributors.
- Players do not see others' choices before deciding and cannot identify who targeted them; they only see round summaries. Because players do not know when the game will end, behavior tends to be consistent early on and may drift later if enforcement is weak.

Likely unfolding given the profiles: initial round contributions cluster around moderate levels (10–15) with a few low contributors. Punishment and reward are used sparingly: a few players (especially those with postgraduate/experienced profiles) will send single-unit punishments to very low contributors and occasional single-unit rewards to high contributors. Where punishment is applied, low contributors tend to raise contributions in subsequent rounds; stable high contributors receive occasional rewards. Overall contributions fluctuate but remain in the moderate-to-high range when sanctions are present.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players who each have 20 coins each round and must decide how many coins (0–20) to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all active players, so any individual’s marginal per-coin return from contributing is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 — less than 1. That creates the classic tension: contributing is socially efficient but individually costly, so free-riding is attractive unless social enforcement (punishment/reward) or reciprocity sustains cooperation.

Because players do not know the number of rounds and can punish/reward after seeing contributions, we expect:
- Initial contributions to be moderate (many players hedging uncertainty).
- High contributors tend to be rewarded occasionally; low contributors may be punished sparingly.
- Punishment and reward are costly to send (4 coins per unit), and punishment is harsher on targets (-7 per unit) than reward benefits (+5), so senders will act cautiously and typically send only a unit or two, if at all.
- Over rounds, modest coordination and reciprocity will push average contributions up somewhat, but a few persistent low contributors may remain and attract intermittent punishment. Rewarding is used to reinforce cooperation, punishment used sparingly to deter blatant free-riding.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript following those expectations and using the provided player priors to guide likely behavior (e.g., more-educated or younger players tending to contribute more; a few older or less-engaged profiles tending to contribute less). Punishment/reward decisions respect players’ leftover budgets (they cannot spend more than 20 − contribution on these actions), and only non-zero edges are listed. Positive unit = reward, negative unit = punishment.

- High contributors tend to be rewarded occasionally; low contributors may be punished sparingly.
- Punishment and reward are costly to send (4 coins per unit), and punishment is harsher on targets (-7 per unit) than reward benefits (+5), so senders will act cautiously and typically send only a unit or two, if at all.
- Over rounds, modest coordination and reciprocity will push average contributions up somewhat, but a few persistent low contributors may remain and attract intermittent punishment. Rewarding is used to reinforce cooperation, punishment used sparingly to deter blatant free-riding.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript following those expectations and using the provided player priors to guide likely behavior (e.g., more-educated or younger players tending to contribute more; a few older or less-engaged profiles tending to contribute less). Punishment/reward decisions respect players’ leftover budgets (they cannot spend more than 20 − contribution on these actions), and only non-zero edges are listed. Positive unit = reward, negative unit = punishment.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a group benefit (contribute 20, it gets multiplied by 1.9 and shared). The dominant individual move is to keep your 20 and free-ride on others, but the group gets higher total payoffs if many contribute. Because players can punish others at a cost (4 coins per punishment unit for the punisher, 8 coins deducted per unit from the punished), we expect early rounds to be a test: some players contribute to signal cooperation, others test by withholding. When free-riding persists, some cooperators will use sparse, targeted punishment to enforce cooperation; if punishment is effective, cooperation will increase in subsequent rounds. Toward the very end of a 15-round game, backward induction pressures make some players reduce contributions (and punishment becomes less effective), so contributions often fall in the final rounds. Chat will be sparse and usually used to coordinate threats or call out defectors. Punishment will be used sparingly — enough to establish norms but not so much as to bankrupt punishers.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 15 players, each with 20 coins per round, against the classic social dilemma: contributing to the group pot raises everyone's payoff (the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally), but contributing is privately costly. Because players do not see others' choices before deciding and they don't know how many rounds remain, behavior will be shaped by initial priors about others, early signals (how others behaved and whether they punish/reward), and risk aversion.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation (many contribute around half their endowment) as players test others. A few low contributors (free riders) often appear immediately.
- Punishment and reward options are used sparingly. Punishments are costly to the punisher and therefore usually small and targeted (1–2 units) toward obvious free riders; rewards are also rare and usually small (1 unit) to reinforce cooperation.
- If low contributors persist and are not punished, cooperation tends to decline over rounds as the payoff from free-riding becomes tempting. If punishments are applied consistently and not too costly to enforcers, cooperation can be partially sustained.
- Because players cannot identify who punished or rewarded them, social enforcement is blunt: punishers try to deter by targeting low contributors, and rewarders try to encourage high contributors, but the lack of attribution means reputational dynamics are limited.
- Uncertainty includes: how strongly others value the public good versus short-term private payoff, and whether punishment/reward will be used enough to alter behavior. Expect some players to hold steady (consistent contributors), a subset to free-ride more over time, and occasional small-scale enforcing actions.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and must choose either to contribute all 20 to the public pot or to keep all 20 privately. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all 10 players, so a larger number of contributors increases the per-player return but each individual contributor bears the private cost of 20. After contributions are revealed and payoffs redistributed, players can punish others: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and removes 8 coins from the target. Players can also send chat messages at any time. Because the game has a known finite horizon (15 rounds), incentives shift over time: early and middle rounds favor building cooperation and using punishment to enforce norms; very late rounds often see more opportunistic defection because future punishment is less useful. Punishment is costly, so it is used sparingly — typically to discipline persistent free-riders rather than in every round — and only a few players are likely to take on the enforcement role.

Given the player profiles, I expect a mixed start (some cooperate immediately, a few try free-riding), then a handful of committed cooperators will punish occasional defectors until most players converge on cooperation. One player (GORILLA) is likely to test the system and be targeted repeatedly for punishment; other occasional experiments/defections happen later in the game. Chat will be sparse: a couple of suggestions to cooperate early, occasional threats or explanations, and short comments after punishments.

This is a typical public goods game with costly, targeted rewards. Individually, keeping coins dominates giving to the pot (each coin returned to an individual only yields ~0.247 coins), so there is a steady incentive to free-ride. However, many players start by contributing moderately out of fairness, confusion, or expectations of reciprocity. The reward stage allows cooperators to support each other by spending costly resources to boost recipients; because rewards are costly (cost 4 to giver, +5 to receiver per unit), they will be used sparingly. Early rounds will show moderate contributions with a few small positive rewards aimed at recognized cooperators. Over rounds, contributions tend to drift downward as players experience free-riding; selective rewarding can slow or partially reverse that decline but only if enough players are willing to incur costs. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no guaranteed "final round" behavior, but an indefinite-horizon uncertainty often leads to gradual decay rather than a sharp last-round collapse. Anonymity of reward sources (players cannot see who rewarded them) reduces direct tit-for-tat signaling; rewards will therefore be conservative and primarily targeted at consistently high contributors.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits ten players against a repeated social dilemma: contributing 20 coins increases the group's pot but lowers your private holding that round; keeping your 20 gives a higher private return unless others also contribute. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so each 20-coin contribution adds 3.8 coins to every player's round income — a net private loss for a contributor if others defect, and a net social gain if many contribute.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early uncertainty: players test others. Some will try contributing; some will free-ride to get a higher immediate payoff.
- Punishment acts as a costly enforcement tool: cooperators may punish visible defectors to encourage future contributions, but punishments cost the punisher 4 coins per unit and only remove 8 coins per unit from the target, so punishment is used sparingly.
- If punishment credibly follows defection, cooperation tends to rise in subsequent rounds; if punishments are few or too costly, defecting can persist.
- Near the end (final rounds) we expect some "endgame" unraveling as forward-looking incentives to sustain cooperation decline, so defections may increase and punishment decline (punishment is costly and yields no future deterrence).
- Messaging is sparse but can be used to coordinate or call out defectors; expect a few brief calls for cooperation and occasional protest messages after punishment.

This is a 15-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round each player can either contribute their whole endowment (20) or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Individually, keeping your 20 and letting others contribute yields a higher immediate payoff, but if many people contribute the group payoff rises. Because the game is finitely repeated and players see round-by-round payoffs of everyone, we expect some initial cooperation driven by goodwill or explicit signals, followed by a gradual decline as free-riding is tempting and observed. Occasional attempts to restart cooperation may occur mid- to late-game, but the final round is expected to have low cooperation because there's no future to influence.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players who each get 20 coins per round. Each round players choose how much of their 20 to contribute to a public pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After contributions are revealed and redistributed, players may spend coins to punish (cost 4 per unit to sender, -7 per unit to target) or reward (cost 4 per unit to sender, +5 per unit to target) others. Players do not see others' choices before deciding and cannot identify who targeted them later.

Likely dynamics: many players will start by contributing moderate amounts (conditional cooperators), some will free-ride or give very little. Punishment and reward are typically used sparingly: a few players (those who value fairness or high contributors) will punish persistent low contributors and occasionally reward high contributors to sustain cooperation. Because players don’t know the total number of rounds, behavior tends to be more forward-looking and conditional—cooperation may be sustained by selective rewarding, while persistent low contributors draw occasional punishments. Punishment is costly and tends to be used only against clearly low contributors; rewards are used selectively for consistently high contributions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with 10 players, 15 rounds, and simple binary choices each round (contribute 20 or 0). The group multiplier (1.9) is less than the group size (10), so each coin a player contributes returns only 0.19 coins to themselves via the public pot — individually it's always better to keep your 20 (free-ride), but if everyone contributed the group gets more total payoff (all contribute -> each gets 38, which is better than 20). That creates the classic social dilemma: cooperation is collectively efficient but individually costly.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players will try to signal cooperation (messages and initial contributions). Conditional cooperators (those who match others' past contributions) may contribute early.
- Mid game: because free-riding is individually profitable, we expect cooperation to decline as some contributors see they were exploited and switch to 0. Occasional "stalwarts" (more altruistic or trusting players) may keep contributing sporadically, and brief revivals can occur after messages promising mutual contributions.
- Late game: as the horizon approaches, contributions typically fall further; by the final rounds many players will defect (0), though a few may try last-minute cooperation or send normative messages. There is uncertainty because individual profiles differ: some postgraduates and older players may be more likely to try to maintain cooperation, while others respond quickly to exploitation.

Now the transcript, round by round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 15 players. Each round everyone privately has 20 coins and decides how many to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. The private marginal return on each contributed coin is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 — well below 1 — so a purely selfish player prefers to keep coins, but the group benefit of contributions is large. That creates the classic tension: individually incentivized to free-ride, collectively better off if many contribute.

Because contributions are revealed before punishment/reward, we expect conditional cooperation and slow dynamics: many players who are inclined to cooperate (higher education, younger, stable employment) will contribute moderately to highly early on, while a minority will contribute little. Reward and punishment are costly to the actor (4 coins/unit), so both are used sparingly. Rewards (benefit +5 to recipient) will be targeted at consistent high contributors to sustain cooperation; punishments (-7 to target) will be used occasionally against persistent low contributors to deter free-riding. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no predictable end-game spike in defection; contributions will typically trend slightly down as some players test free-riding, then rebalance via targeted punishments and rewards. Punishment may be targeted mainly at the obvious low contributors (repeated zeros or near-zero contributions), while rewards cluster on the handful of consistent high contributors.

In the transcript below I simulate nine rounds. Each round starts with an explanation of why I expect the contributions and subsequent sparse punishment/reward choices. Contributions follow the exact player order given in <PLAYERS>. Punishment/reward tuples are (source, target, unit) with positive = reward units and negative = punishment units. Only non-zero edges are shown.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone chooses either to contribute their full 20 coins to a group pot or keep the 20 privately. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so the group return per contributor is 3.8 coins to every player. Individual defectors (contribute 0) get the private 20 plus the group share, so short-run incentives favor free-riding. However, the ability to punish (cost 4 per punishment unit; each unit reduces the target by 8) creates a mechanism to enforce cooperation: cooperators can punish defectors, making defection costly. Because the game has multiple rounds, players who care about future rounds will punish to sustain cooperation, while end-game effects (near the last rounds) make punishment and cooperation less reliable.

Given the player profiles, I expect initially mixed behavior: some players contribute to signal cooperation (those who are more prosocial or disciplined), while a few will free-ride. Early targeted punishments against repeat free-riders should raise contribution rates by around round 3–4. Once cooperation becomes common, occasional one-shot defections occur and are lightly punished. Toward the final rounds, contributions will decline again as the incentive to sustain future cooperation weakens; punishment effort also declines slightly in the last round, though a few players may still punish out of spite or fairness preferences. Chat is typically sparse and used to exhort or report (e.g., "Please contribute" or "I punished X").
