game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with an added voluntary reward stage. Players get 20 coins each round, simultaneously choose integer contributions (0–20) to a shared pot that is multiplied by 3.7 and evenly split, then can spend costly rewards that transfer value (cost 4, benefit 5) to specific peers. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they treat each round as potentially lasting, which supports conditional cooperation: many will contribute a moderate positive amount early on, but some will reduce contributions if they see low contributions from others. Rewards are used sparingly in most lab/web experiments — typically to recognize high contributors (positive reinforcement) rather than to directly punish free riders (there is no direct costly punishment here). Given the provided profiles (many with higher education and stable employment), I expect overall contributions to start at a moderate-to-high level (near 10–15), then drift slightly downward as self-interest and uncertainty about others’ future behavior weigh in. Occasional targeted rewards will appear, usually 1 reward-unit at a time and directed to those who contribute relatively highly; a few players may give 2 units occasionally. Rewards will modestly stabilize cooperation but will not completely eliminate a gradual decline.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This public goods game repeatedly gives each player 20 coins and asks for a contribution to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the 15 players. After the redistribution, players can pay to punish or reward peers: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and reduces the target by 7 coins; each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and increases the target by 5 coins. Players do not see others' choices before deciding, cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, and do not know how many rounds will be played.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show moderate cooperation (many contribute about half or a bit more of their endowment) because of uncertainty and priors that others will cooperate. Some individuals (the “sloth” or unemployed profiles) may start lower, and highly educated/older profiles may contribute more.
- Rewards are used sparingly to reinforce high contributors; punishments are used sparingly as a deterrent to visible low contributions. Because punishment is costly to the sender, expect only a few one-unit punishments rather than many heavy sanctions.
- Over rounds, cooperation can stabilize if rewards/punishments create incentives to keep contributing; occasional defections (drops in contribution) can trigger a small number of punishments in the next rounds, after which the defector often raises contributions again.
- Because players cannot identify who punished them, the social signaling effect is imperfect; punishment still reduces a defector’s payoff and often elicits correction. Rewarding tends to flow to consistently high contributors (e.g., MOOSE, MONKEY, RABBIT).
- Uncertainty about total rounds can sustain cooperation longer than a known final round would, but small fluctuations (one or two players lowering contributions) are normal.

Below I simulate nine rounds consistent with these priors: modest-to-high initial cooperation, sparse rewards to consistent cooperators, occasional light punishments to deter low contributors, and gradual stabilization.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This 15-player public goods game sets up a classic tension: each player gets 20 coins and deciding whether to contribute any to the public pot is individually costly (you lose 1 coin per contribution) while the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all 15 players (each contributed coin returns ~0.247 to each player). The private monetary incentive is therefore to contribute 0, but social preferences (reciprocity, fairness, conditional cooperation) can lead many players to contribute positive amounts, especially early on when reputational concerns and uncertainty about others' behavior are still strong.

Because players cannot see others' contributions until after they decide and cannot identify who rewarded them later, reward behavior will be used sparingly and strategically: a reward costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient 5 coins, so rewards are an effective but costly instrument to reinforce cooperation. Expect an initial phase with moderate contributions from those inclined to cooperate (educated, employed, or reciprocity-prone profiles), some free-riding from less cooperative priors, and occasional targeted rewards to reinforce high contributors. Over repeated rounds (and without knowing how many rounds remain), contributions typically drift downward as conditional cooperators respond to perceived defection and the one-shot incentive to free-ride grows; rewards may slow the decline but are unlikely to fully sustain initial high contributions. Because rewards are costly, they will be relatively rare and concentrated on visibly high contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game where each player chooses either to contribute their entire 20 coins to the group pot or keep all 20. The group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players. Because each contributed coin returns only 0.19 to its contributor (1.9/10), individual incentives favor free-riding (contribute 0) while collective welfare is maximized if everyone contributes. In repeated play with visible summaries, we commonly expect an initial period with some cooperation (driven by social preferences, instructions, or goodwill), followed by declining contributions as players see free-riders earn more in the short run. Communication can sustain cooperation if credible promises or strong social preferences exist, but here chat is sparse and players vary in likely pro-sociality. So I predict moderate cooperation early (several players contribute in round 1), then a steady decline as contributors observe free-rider advantage; by the middle to later rounds most players will free-ride. Round-by-round I will show contributions and the round summaries (each player's net payoff for that round), keeping chat minimal and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments (20 coins each round), a multiplier (3.7) on the group pot and a costly peer-reward stage (each reward unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 5 to the receiver). Because the marginal per-capita return (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247) is well below 1, the individual monetary incentive in any single round is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, players often start with some cooperation (contributors expect others to contribute) and may use costly rewards to sustain cooperation: a 1-unit reward is net costly to the sender (−4) but increases the recipient by +5, so rewarders are implicitly willing to subsidize others to encourage contributions or to reciprocate. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, early rounds typically show more cooperation (players treat interaction as potentially long-term) and conditional cooperators will adjust contributions based on peers’ behavior in previous rounds. Rewarding is visible in the transcript but scarce in real games: sending rewards is relatively costly and therefore used sparingly, directed mainly at clear cooperators. Heterogeneous priors (the 15 player profiles) imply stable cooperators (e.g., older or more deliberative players) mixed with some low contributors (e.g., persistently inactive or opportunistic players). Expect modest-to-moderate average contributions to start, some decline as free-riding appears, and occasional targeted rewards to bolster high contributors and stabilize cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players, each given 20 coins per round and choosing either to contribute all (20) or nothing (0). The group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so the total group benefit from a full contribution is shared but the individual incentive is to free-ride: for any one player, withholding 20 yields a higher private payoff if others contribute. The social optimum (everyone contributes) would raise everyone's round payoff (38 each) above the all-defect payoff (20 each), but unilateral defection is individually tempting. Because the horizon is finite and known, backward induction predicts widespread defection over time. However, cheap-talk (messages) and social preferences can produce occasional and fragile cooperation early on or in focal rounds, especially from players who appear more pro-social. Expect some early attempts at cooperation from a few players, quick defection responses by many, and persistent low-level attempts from a subset (especially RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, DUCK), with cooperation generally declining and occasional short-lived blips when small groups try to coordinate. Chat will be sparse and mostly reactive: promises, pleas, and a few short explanations.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins, decides a contribution (0–20) to a pot that is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. The private marginal return from contributing is negative (each contributed coin returns 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 to the contributor), so the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Repeated interaction (even when the horizon is unknown) plus the ability to punish (costly to sender, costly to receiver) and reward (costly to sender, beneficial to receiver) can sustain higher contributions: some players will be conditional cooperators and increase contributions when others do, while a few free-riders may be punished. Punishment and reward are typically used sparingly because they are themselves costly (cost 4 per unit for the sender). Because players cannot see who punished or rewarded them, information is limited to totals; this increases uncertainty about who to target and tends to keep sanctions modest.

Given the provided player priors (diverse ages, education, employment), I predict initial moderate contributions with variation, a few targeted punishments toward clear free-riders, occasional rewards to consistent cooperators, and a modest trend toward stabilization of cooperation (not full cooperation) across rounds. Punishment will be rare and small (mostly single-unit punishments), and rewards likewise small.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game where each player must choose either to contribute their full 20 coins to a shared pot or keep their 20. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players, so each contributed 20 adds 38 to the pot and raises every player's round payoff by 3.8. That means an individual who contributes gets a share 3.8k (k = number of contributors) while a non-contributor gets 20 + 3.8k. Because non-contribution always leaves the private 20 intact, free riding strictly dominates contribution for an individual given others' choices; yet groups often see some level of cooperation early on because of social preferences, reciprocity, or successful coordination via chat. Over repeated rounds with a known endpoint (15 rounds), typical dynamics are:
- Initial rounds: some players try to coordinate (cheap talk) and several contribute; conditional cooperators contribute if they expect others to too.
- Middle rounds: if free riding is observed or inferred from payoffs, cooperation tends to decline; occasional messages may temporarily restore cooperation.
- End game (last rounds): defection becomes more common because future reciprocity is not available, often leading to many zeros in final rounds.

There is also uncertainty: players only see round payoffs (their own and peers'), not raw contribution labels directly. Payoff patterns let players infer others' behavior but not perfectly, which preserves some room for continued cooperation or strategic ambiguity. Expect sparse chat: occasional proposals to "all contribute" and a few complaints about free-riding; some players will attempt to re-start cooperation midgame but usually it drifts downward toward the last rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits a group of 10 players against the classic tension: contributing 20 coins to a shared pot benefits the group (the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally) but costs the individual 20 coins immediately, creating an incentive to free-ride. Because choices are binary (0 or 20) and contributions are private until revealed, early rounds typically serve as information-gathering: some players will test others by free-riding, and some will try to enforce cooperation by punishing defectors. Punishment is costly (4 coins per unit to the punisher, 8 coins deducted per unit from the target), so it will be used sparingly — enough to signal that defection has a cost, but not so much that enforcers ruin their own payoffs. Over repeated rounds with known horizon (15 rounds), we expect:

- Early rounds: a mix of cooperation and defection as players test norms.
- Midgame (rounds ~3–11): if punishers are effective, cooperation will rise and stabilize; occasional defections will be met with targeted punishment.
- Late game (final few rounds): incentives to punish and to cooperate weaken ("endgame unraveling"). Some players will begin to defect more often; punishment frequency drops because future deterrent value is limited.

Players know who punished them, so targeted retaliation is possible but costly. Chats will be sparse and used mainly to request cooperation or complain about punishments. Punishment decisions will target repeat defectors or obvious one-shot defectors, but remain relatively infrequent.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 20 coins each round) against a collective benefit (each coin contributed to the pot returns 3.7 coins, split equally across all 15 players). Because 3.7 is split across many people, each coin you give yields only about 0.247 coins back to you personally, so contributing is individually costly but socially efficient. The optional reward stage lets players spend 4 coins to give another player 5 coins — an expensive way to reward cooperation that can nonetheless sustain higher contributions if used selectively.

Given the player profiles and common behavior in repeated anonymous PGGs, I expect:
- An initial tendency toward modest cooperation from many players (conditional cooperators and those with higher education or civic preferences), with a few low contributors (free riders, low effort).
- Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted at the most consistent contributors (postgrads and those who repeatedly give above the group average) to signal appreciation and encourage continued cooperation.
- Over rounds, without strong punishment, average contributions will drift down slowly as players notice some low contributors. Rewards help slow the decline but are not numerous enough to fully sustain high contributions.
- Players do not know how many rounds there will be, so we should not expect a sharp end-game collapse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 players who each have 20 coins per round. Each decides privately how much to contribute to a common pot; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so contributing benefits the group but is individually costly. After contributions, players may spend coins to punish or reward others: each punishment unit costs 4 to the sender and removes 7 from the target; each reward unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 5 to the target. Players cannot see others' choices before deciding and they do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds often see moderate contributions as many players try to signal cooperation. Some players (those with pro-social priors) will contribute relatively high amounts; others (more self-interested or with less to gain from reputation) will give little.
- Punishment and reward are costly and used sparingly. Rewards tend to be used by cooperators to reinforce other cooperators; punishment is targeted at persistent low contributors ("free riders") to enforce cooperation, but only a few players will do this because it's costly.
- Over repeated rounds, absent strong, consistent punishment, average contributions typically drift downward as self-interested incentives to free-ride dominate. Players who are punished tend to raise contributions modestly; persistent non-contributors may be repeatedly punished by a few enforcement-minded players.
- Because players do not know how many rounds remain, end-game collapse (a sharp drop at the final round) may be muted; behavior will more likely show a gradual decline in contributions.
- Uncertainty about who punished/rewarded whom reduces targeted retaliation; interactions will show a small number of repeated edges (same sources acting multiple rounds) from players who consistently reward cooperators or punish free-riders.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players each round. Each player privately chooses how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 then divided equally. Because the per-coin private return from the public pot (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247) is well below 1, each player has a strong individual incentive to free-ride (contribute little or nothing) even though the group maximizes welfare if everyone contributes more. Uncertainty about other players' contributions and the absence of pre-play communication typically lead to moderate initial contributions and then a gradual decline as some players notice free-riding.

The reward stage allows positive reinforcement: players can spend 4 coins per reward unit to give 5 coins to another player. Rewards are costly to give and therefore typically used sparingly, often targeted at clear cooperators to encourage reciprocal cooperation. Because rewards are anonymous to recipients and players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect: moderate cooperation early, a few players who consistently contribute more and occasionally reward each other, and a slow erosion of average contributions as free-riding becomes visible. Occasional rewards should slow that decay among a subset of pro-social players, but overall contributions will likely drift downward over nine rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot public goods game with 15 anonymous players per round. Each player gets 20 coins, decides how many to contribute to the public pot (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally. After seeing contributions and payouts, players may spend coins to punish (each unit costs the sender 4 coins and removes 7 coins from the target) or reward (each unit costs the sender 4 coins and grants 5 coins to the target). Players see per-round summaries but cannot identify who acted.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- The individually optimal move in a single round is to contribute 0 (free-ride) because each contributed coin returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.2467 coins to the contributor — a private loss for any contribution. But group payoff is maximized by full contributions.
- Without enforcement, contributions tend to be moderate and drift down as some players free-ride. With low-cost, anonymous punishment/reward, cooperative players can deter free-riding by sanctioning it. Because punishing is relatively costly to the sender (cost 4 per unit) but inflicts larger damage on targets (7 per unit), punishments are used sparingly in real experiments — enough to influence behavior but not in large numbers.
- Rewards cost the same to send but give somewhat smaller direct benefit to recipients; rewards are also typically used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior.
- Players do not know the number of rounds; this tends to reduce end-game defecting effects and may sustain cooperation a bit longer.
- Given the provided profiles (mix of young postgrads likely pro-social, some older/more pragmatic players, a few with lower education or lower engagement), I predict initial contributions will be mixed-to-moderate (roughly 8–15), with a few low contributors. Small, targeted punishments will be used early to deter persistent low contributors (e.g., units = 1), and occasional 1-unit rewards will be given to consistently high contributors. Over rounds cooperation will tend to increase for many players but a few persistent low contributors will continue to receive occasional sanctions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their full 20 coins to a shared pot or keep all 20. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all 10 players. Individually, contributing is costly: regardless of what others do, you lose 20 coins and receive only the pot share, so contributing reduces your own round payoff by 16.2 coins relative to free-riding. Group payoff, however, increases with contributions: every 20 coin contribution raises total group payoff by 18 coins. That creates the classic tension: free-riding is the dominant strategy in the one-shot sense, but collective welfare is higher when people cooperate.

Typical dynamics in repeated PGGs: some initial cooperation (people test the waters; promises or social norms may encourage contributions), then gradual decline as free riders exploit contributors and conditional cooperators retaliate by reducing contributions. Messages can sustain cooperation if credible, but with a finite known horizon (15 rounds) cooperation tends to decay toward the end as backward induction and opportunism increase. Uncertainty remains about others' motives: some players may be consistently conditional cooperators, others pure free-riders, and a few may try to lead by example or send persuasive messages. Expect sparse chat (a few short pledges, appeals, or expressions of disappointment), an early-to-mid run of mixed cooperation, occasional small rallies, and a decline to low/no contributions by the final rounds.

Expect sparse chat (a few short pledges, appeals, or expressions of disappointment), an early-to-mid run of mixed cooperation, occasional small rallies, and a decline to low/no contributions by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and an added voluntary reward stage after contributions. Each round every player has 20 coins and decides how many to contribute to a shared pot. Because the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally among all 16 players, each coin contributed returns 4.5/16 = 0.28125 to each player — so contributors pay a private cost greater than their personal return. That creates a tension: group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes, but individually there is an incentive to free-ride.

The reward stage allows players to reward others at a cost (4 per reward unit) that benefits recipients (2 per unit). Rewards can help sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors and signaling appreciation or retaliation against low contributors. Rewards are costly, so they will be used sparingly and strategically. Early rounds will likely show moderate cooperation as players test each other (many contribute around the midpoint). Players who are visibly cooperative will occasionally receive a small reward, encouraging others to match them. Over the middle rounds, modest reciprocity and some reward signaling can raise average contributions. Toward the end of the 17-round horizon, standard endgame effects make contributions fall as players anticipate fewer future benefits from building reputation; similarly, reward use will drop.

Chat will be sparse and functional: short calls for cooperation, occasional thanks, and a few calls-out of consistently low contributors. Rewards will be focused on consistently high contributors and used infrequently (mostly 1-unit rewards), and only a few players will send rewards in any round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so group-level cooperation can raise everyone’s payoff but individual incentives favor free-riding. After seeing contributions, players can punish or reward others. Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per unit) and very costly to the punished (15 coins deducted per unit), so punishment is a powerful deterrent but used sparingly. Rewards cost the giver 4 per unit and give the recipient +2 per unit; rewards are less efficient for the giver but signal appreciation.

Given the mix of profiles, I expect a few patterns:
- Early rounds will see many players contributing moderately to highly (10–16), with a handful edging low. Because participants can see who punished/rewarded them later, a few players who start low may be targeted by occasional punishments, leading them to raise contributions.
- A small core of more cooperative, higher-education players (e.g., DUCK, RABBIT, OWL, SNAKE, COW) will often contribute at the top end and sometimes use rewards to reinforce others; punishments will be reserved for persistent low contributors (sparse and targeted).
- Mid-game cooperation will be relatively high thanks to occasional sanctions and reciprocal rewarding among cooperators.
- Toward the final rounds (especially rounds 14–17), contributions trend down as end-game effects kick in; punishment becomes rarer because punishing late is costly and provides little future benefit.
- Chat is sparse and strategic: brief encouragements, calls out of free-riders, or apologies.

The transcript below simulates these dynamics round-by-round for all 17 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players, known horizon and a costly reward stage after contributions. Typical dynamics: many players start moderately cooperative (contribute substantial fractions of their 20 coins) because contributions are socially efficient when multiplied and shared. Because players are anonymous within the round and iterated over known finite rounds, conditional cooperators will reduce contributions if they see free-riding; that typically produces a gradual decline in average contributions. The possibility to reward allows cooperators to give positive feedback to high contributors, which can slow the downward drift but does not eliminate it (rewards are costly to the giver and relatively small in effect per unit). Near the end of a finite game, forward-looking players have less incentive to sustain cooperation, so contributions usually fall further. Rewards tend to be sparse and targeted at clear cooperators or group leaders; chat is typically short and used to request cooperation, thank contributors, or call out patterns. There is uncertainty about individuals’ strategies (some are steadfast cooperators, some conditional, some persistent free-riders), so expect heterogeneous choices, some reciprocity via rewards toward consistent contributors, and an overall gradual decline in contributions with small amounts of rewarding throughout.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and an opportunity to punish or reward after seeing others' contributions. Incentives: contributing reduces your private holding immediately but increases the shared pot (multiplied by 4.5 and split equally). Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) and very harmful to the target (-15 coins per punishment unit). Reward is also costly to the source (4 coins per reward unit) but gives a modest benefit to the recipient (+2 coins per reward unit). Because the game is finite and players know the end round, we expect an initial modest-to-moderate level of cooperation, some escalation of cooperation if conditional cooperators and sanctioners are present, and a decline toward the end (last few rounds) as end-game incentives encourage freeriding. Punishments and rewards will be used sparingly: small, targeted punishments to deter consistent free-riders and occasional rewards to reinforce cooperators. Chat messages will be relatively rare and functional (calls to cooperate, short justifications for punishment).

game_explanation:
This is a finite (17-round) public goods game with 16 players. Each round each player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to put into a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally. Individually, every coin contributed returns less than its cost, so the single-round incentive is to contribute 0. However many people are conditionally cooperative: they contribute more if they expect others to contribute, and they may use costly rewards to reinforce cooperation or reward good contributors. Because rewards are costly (4 coins per reward unit) and give only 2 coins to the recipient, rewards will be used sparsely and typically only to signal approval of high contributors or to encourage reciprocation.

Given the players' profiles and standard experimental dynamics, I predict an initial burst of cooperation (many contribute moderately-high amounts) because players often try to establish cooperation early. A few free riders or low contributors (either by preference or experimentation) will appear early; this reduces others' incentives, and contributions will gradually decline over the mid- to late-game. Sparse reward actions by some pro-social players will slow the decline but won't fully prevent it. Chat will be used rarely and mainly to encourage cooperation or call out clear free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds. Players start each round with 20 private coins and simultaneously choose how many to contribute to a common pot; contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally. After seeing contributions, players can spend coins to punish or reward others (punish: source pays 4 per unit to reduce target by 15 per unit; reward: source pays 4 per unit to give target +2 per unit). Players see only their own round summary (their net payoff and what they spent/received), and can send messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as many test others' behavior and try to build reciprocity. Expect several players (those inclined to cooperate) to contribute above the midpoint; a few (free-riders) contribute very little.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly: rewards to acknowledge high cooperators and punishments targeted at persistent free-riders or very low contributors. Because punishment is costly to the punisher, it will be used selectively—mostly when norms are damaged.
- Over rounds we expect a modest mid-game decline if free-riding increases, with occasional punishments/rewards that nudge cooperation back up. Toward the final rounds, backward induction and diminished future returns will push average contributions lower; players will be less willing to spend on costly punishments late in the game.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: short appeals to cooperate, occasional apologies, and a few threats or promises to punish if others continue to free-ride.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with a strong multiplier (4.5) and a reward stage after contributions. Early rounds typically start with some optimism and moderate-to-high contributions from intrinsically cooperative or reputation-conscious players; others test the waters with low contributions. Because players can reward after seeing contributions, cooperators can selectively reward high contributors, which helps sustain cooperation among a core group. Rewards are costly to give (4 coins per reward unit) and yield limited benefit (2 coins per unit) to the receiver, so they will be used sparingly — primarily by players who value fairness or want to maintain cooperation.

Uncertainties that shape behavior: (1) how many players will consistently free-ride, (2) whether rewarders will persist in rewarding (costly tit-for-tat-like support), and (3) end-game effects (players often reduce contributions in final rounds because future reciprocity is not possible). Expect contributions to start moderately high and drift downward as free-riding appears, with intermittent spurts of cooperation if rewarders actively support contributors. Chat will be sparse and focused on encouragement, calls for fairness, or short complaints about low contributors. Rewarding will typically target the most consistent contributors, with one or two small gifts per round rather than broad redistribution.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round everyone privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. Because each coin contributed returns less than 1 coin to the contributor (4.5/16 = 0.28125), the individual incentive is to free-ride, but total group welfare is increased by contributions. Players see each other's contributions each round, so social information can create norms. The availability of costly rewards (pay 4 to give 2 to someone else) enables indirect enforcement: players can reward high contributors to reinforce cooperation, or simply withhold rewards to punish low contributors.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show moderately high contributions as players test each other and try to establish cooperation.
- Some participants (here SLOTH, CROCODILE, MOOSE) are likely to contribute little from the start; others (e.g., DUCK, PARROT, RABBIT, PINGUIN, MONKEY) will try to sustain higher contributions.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributions (a few 1-unit rewards here and there), not to directly punish—there is no costly punishment, only withholding reward.
- Over rounds, contributions tend to drift downward as free-riding becomes apparent; rewards can slow that decline but likely will not fully stop it. Near the final rounds, rational endgame effects and fatigue typically reduce contributions further and rewards largely disappear.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round everyone privately decides how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. Individually, each coin contributed returns less than 1 coin to the contributor (4.5/16 ≈ 0.28125), so the private incentive is to free-ride, while the group optimum is full contribution. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (cost 4 to sender, -15 to target per unit) or reward (cost 4 to sender, +2 to target per unit) peers.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions as players test each other; a few low contributors may be punished to signal norms.
- Middle rounds: with occasional sanctions and a few rewards, cooperation tends to rise as conditional cooperators respond to incentives.
- Late rounds: contributions often drift down again (end-game effects). Punishments and rewards may be used sparingly to express frustration or encourage improvement, but many players reduce contributions approaching the final rounds.
- Overall: punishment/reward tend to be sparse and targeted; chat is limited and mostly pragmatic (requests, complaints, short encouragements). Players see only their own detailed round summary (net payoff and what they paid/received).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players decide how many of their 20 private coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend costly reward units (each unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Players see everyone's contributions when choosing rewards, and they see their own round payoff and reward use/receipt in the summary; they do not see others' private payoffs.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players start cooperative (high contributions), some start low or free-ride. Cooperators often reward other cooperators, creating small clusters of mutual reinforcement.
- Midgame: as players observe persistent low contributors and the private cost of cooperation, conditional cooperators gradually reduce contributions. Cooperation tends to decay as free-riding spreads, unless rewards are strong and targeted enough to sustain it.
- Late rounds: with fewer future consequences, contributions tend to drift to low levels. Some steady cooperators keep contributing out of intrinsic preferences, reciprocity, or to signal fairness, and these players occasionally exchange modest rewards.
- Chat is sparse and used for short pledges, calls for cooperation, or brief thanks/complaints. Rewards are used sparingly — mainly to acknowledge persistent contributors.

This is a 17-round public goods game among 16 players. Each round players choose how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (costly to sender, very costly to target) or reward (costly to sender, modest benefit to target) others; these post-decision actions can sustain cooperation but are themselves costly. Because players know the game length, there is pressure toward lower contributions as the end approaches (end-game decline), but early and midgame punishment/reward can stabilize higher contributions if some players commit to sanctioning free-riders and rewarding cooperators. Communication is allowed but limited; typical behavior in such settings is sparse chat, moderate initial cooperation that gradually declines, with a few targeted punishments of persistent low contributors and occasional rewards to recognize high contributors. I'll simulate plausible individual differences (some consistently cooperative players, some pragmatic/moderate, a few persistent low contributors) and sparse, targeted sanctioning and rewarding across the 17 rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game lasts 17 rounds with 16 players each endowed with 20 coins per round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and divided equally, so an individual gets back only 4.5/16 ≈ 0.281 coins per contributed coin — a private incentive to free-ride, but a social incentive to contribute. Because contributions are revealed before the punishment/reward stage, players can condition punishments/rewards on others' contributions. Punishment is costly (4 coins per unit for the punisher, deducts 15 coins per unit from the target) and hence tends to be used sparingly and selectively against blatant free-riders; rewards are also costly to give (4 coins per unit) but return smaller bonuses (2 coins per unit to the target), so they are used infrequently as positive reinforcement.

Given the profiles, I predict an initial phase of moderate-to-high contributions as many players try cooperation. A few early free-riders (or low contributors) will be punished by several cooperators; that will cause some conditional cooperators to increase contributions. Over the mid-game, cooperation generally holds but with gradual drift and occasional defections that provoke targeted punishment. Toward the final rounds, standard endgame effects appear: contributions fall as the incentive to invest in future rounds disappears, and punishments largely stop because they are costly and offer little strategic benefit late in the game. Chat will be sparse and used mainly for short coordination appeals, apologies, or to call out obvious free-riders.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally. After returns are distributed, players can spend coins to send positive rewards to others (each reward unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver). Players can also chat at any time. The main incentives: contributing increases the group payoff but is costly to the individual, so free-riding is tempting. Rewards provide a way to incentivize cooperation by rewarding high contributors (and are costly to the rewarder), which can sustain higher contributions if enough players use them. Uncertainties include how many players will persistently free-ride (e.g., contributing 0) and how willing others are to pay to reward cooperators. Over repeated rounds we expect some clustering into cooperators who contribute moderately-high amounts and occasionally reward, and some persistent low contributors (free-riders). Communication and visible reward receipts (players see who rewarded them) will help sustain cooperation for many players; near the end, contributions may either rise (end-game cooperation) or fall (end-game defection) depending on whether social norms and reciprocity have become established. Rewards are likely to be used sparingly and targeted at reliably high contributors.

game_explanation:
This 16-player public goods game creates a classic social dilemma: each coin you keep yields one private unit, while any coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and then split equally across all players, so one coin contributed returns only 4.5/16 ≈ 0.28 to its contributor. The individually dominant strategy is to contribute nothing, but the group optimum is full contribution. Over 17 rounds we therefore expect some mix of conditional cooperation (players who contribute more when others do), free-riding (players who contribute little and benefit), occasional sanctioning (punishments targeted at low contributors) and sparse rewarding (to reinforce cooperators). Early rounds will likely show a moderate level of cooperation from several industrious players (postgrads, those who signalled commitment), while a few participants (younger, unemployed, or low-engagement profiles) may contribute little. If free-riding becomes visible, some cooperative players may punish to deter it — but punishments are costly and tend to be used sparingly. Rewards may appear occasionally for consistently high contributors. Toward the final rounds, contributions tend to decline (end-game effect), unless sanctions/rewards sustained cooperation. Chats are expected to be few and focused (requests to contribute, mild admonitions, or thank-yous).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds, contribution decisions (0–20) each round, and optional costly punishment (-15 to target per unit, costs 4 to sender) and costly rewards (+2 to target per unit, costs 4 to sender). Early rounds typically see moderate-to-high contributions as players test each other; a few low contributors often get punished or rewarded to shape behavior. Punishment is used sparingly because it is costly to the punisher, and rewards are even rarer (also costly) but can be used to reinforce cooperation. Over middle rounds, conditional cooperators tend to increase contributions if others are cooperative and decrease if others free-ride. Toward the final rounds, the incentive to enforce cooperation weakens (backward induction), so contributions usually drift downward and sanctions become less frequent. Chat is generally sparse and used for short coordination, praise, or complaints.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 17 known rounds, a standard multiplication/redistribution, and a costly positive-reward stage. Players start each round with 20 coins, choose contributions (0–20) privately, then the group pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players can spend 4 coins to give 1 reward unit that grants 2 coins to another player. Rewards are visible (who gave them), and summaries show each player's own net payoff and reward flows for that round. Knowing the game length creates an end-game incentive to free-ride: toward the last rounds we should see contributions fall as forward-looking incentives disappear. Early rounds typically see relatively high contributions (trust/hypothesis of cooperation), followed by gradual decline as some players test or exploit others. Because only positive rewards are allowed, players use them sparingly to reinforce/reciprocate high contributors rather than punish defectors. Rewarding is costly, so it tends to be used by those who have spare coins (low contribution) and by cooperators who want to promote cooperation; reward reciprocity (rewarding those who rewarded you) can stabilize contributions mid-game. Chat is usually sparse and used to encourage cooperation or thank rewarders. Expect: fairly high average contributions in rounds 1–4, a gradual decline in the middle rounds, some pockets of sustained cooperation from a few consistently cooperative players (e.g., MOOSE, RABBIT, CHICK, PINGUIN), and a clear drop to low or zero contributions by the final rounds. Rewards will be intermittent, targeted at high contributors, and decline in frequency toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a standard public goods game with 16 players, 20 coins each round. Every coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally among all players. That means each coin a player contributes directly returns only 4.5/16 = 0.28125 coins to them; individually, the dominant action is to keep coins (contribute 0). Group welfare is maximized when everyone contributes, so there's a classic tension between private incentive to defect and collective benefit from cooperation.

Because the game lasts 17 rounds and players see round summaries (their own payoffs, rewards given and received, and who rewarded them), we expect:
- Early rounds: some players will try cooperating (moderate-to-high contributions) to test others, especially those who look conditional or prosocial. Others (low contributors) will test free-riding.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators will adjust to observed cooperation; if rewards are used to reward cooperators, they can sustain higher contributions. Rewards are costly to the giver (4 coins per reward unit) and modest for the recipient (+2 coins per unit), so they are used sparingly and mainly as signals/reciprocity.
- Late rounds: standard end-game effect — many players gradually reduce contributions toward zero as the horizon approaches. Rewards can slow but generally cannot prevent a downward trend unless many players coordinate to sustain cooperation.

Uncertainties: exact individual motives (altruism, conditional cooperation, indifference) cause heterogeneity. Rewards are private costly transfers and will be used rarely — mainly to encourage consistent cooperators or to thank specific players. Chat will be sparse and occasionally used to call out free-riders or ask for cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with post-decision punishment and reward. Typical dynamics: early rounds show varied contributions as players test each other's cooperativeness. Low contributors (free riders) often prompt targeted punishment by cooperators; punishment is costly to both parties and thus used sparingly. Rewards are less costly to targets and sometimes used to reinforce high contributors. Over repeated rounds, punishment can stabilize higher cooperation, but toward the very end of the game contributions tend to drift downward (end-game effect) as the incentive to maintain reputation weakens. Expect a mix of mid-level cooperation early (around half the endowment), occasional targeted punishments of clear free-riders, selective rewards to consistent cooperators, and some decline in cooperation in the final rounds. Chat will be sparse—with a few appeals or remarks after surprising behavior.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and costly punishment and reward. Individually, contributing any coin to the pot reduces a player's private payoff (the private return per coin is 1 while the private benefit from the public pot share is only 4.5/16 ≈ 0.281). So the dominant motive is to free-ride. But because players can punish or reward after seeing contributions, and because punishments are effective (costly to target: −15 per punishment unit) many players will use targeted sanctions to sustain higher contributions early and mid-game. Expect:

- Early rounds: a mix of cooperators (higher-contributing, often those with more education or pro-social priors) and some free-riders. A few players (enforcers) will punish clear free-riders to signal that defection is costly.
- Middle rounds: cooperation stabilizes at a higher level if punishers act credibly. Rewards will be rarer (they cost the sender same as a punishment unit but return less to the target), but occasional rewards acknowledge high contributors.
- Late rounds: as the end approaches, the incentive to punish wanes (punishers prefer to conserve private payoff when the future is short), and contributions typically decline. The final round often sees widespread free-riding and little punishment.

Uncertainties: who will be the persistent low contributors (targets) and whether punishers coordinate. Also, individual taste for fairness, willingness to pay to punish, and risk-aversion will create variation. Chats are typically sparse and strategic (requests, thanks, short reproaches).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and a reward stage after contributions. Each round each player decides how many of their 20 private coins to contribute to a public pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly. Rewards are costly to send (4 coins per reward unit) but give a smaller benefit to a target (2 coins per unit). Because the group-level multiplier (4.5) and equal splitting often make cooperating (high contributions) socially valuable, we should expect an early tendency toward moderate-to-high contributions from some players. But the private incentive to free-ride (keep your 20) will push some players to contribute less, especially if they are uncertain about others' choices.

The reward stage allows players to selectively reward cooperators, which can sustain cooperation if used sparingly by many players. In practice, rewards are used rarely (to save sender resources) and targeted at clear high contributors. Over 17 rounds we expect:
- An initial phase of moderate-to-high contributions with a few high contributors attracting small rewards.
- A mid-phase of some decline as free-riding appears; targeted rewards try to prop cooperation up.
- Later rounds show mixed responses — some players increase contributions anticipating end-game reciprocity, others reduce (end-game defection) — with rewards concentrated on consistent cooperators.
- Because reward sends are costly, only a few reward transfers per round are predicted. Chat messages will be sparse and mostly short statements (encourage, thanks, or brief strategy signals).
- There is uncertainty: some players (per their profile) may be more likely to cooperate (e.g., experienced, postgraduate educated players), while others may be cautious or opportunistic. Rewards help signal approval and encourage contributors, but cannot directly punish defectors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game without communication and without a known horizon. Each player starts the round with 20 coins placed in a shared pot and can either withdraw all 20 (keep them privately) or leave them in the pot (contribute 20). The pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally among all players. Because the per-player return from one player's 20-coin contribution is (230/number_of_players) and the cost of contributing is 20, contributing is individually dominated: a defector (withdraw 20) always ends the round with 20 more coins than a cooperator, all else equal. That makes free-riding attractive.

However, people vary: some will be altruistic, conditional cooperators, or stubborn cooperators; others will be selfish or opportunistic. Because players observe only payoffs after each round — and those payoffs let them deduce who contributed (contributors earn exactly 20 less than defectors in the same round) — cooperation can persist for a few rounds but typically declines as conditional cooperators see many others free-ride. Given the player profiles (a mix of experienced, highly educated, retired/not-in-work and younger players), I predict an initial minority of contributors (altruistic and conditional cooperators), followed by a steady decline as contributors learn they're being exploited. By the middle rounds most cooperation will collapse, and by the late rounds nearly everyone will defect, each keeping 20 coins.

predicted_rounds.explanation: At the start, some players with stronger pro-social priors (postgraduate education, retirement/not-in-work, or older players) are likely to try contributing. Others (more opportunistic or younger players) will withdraw immediately. No one sees others' choices before acting, so initial variance is likely: a minority contributes while the majority defects. After the round, payoffs will reveal who contributed.

Players see the round 1 payoffs and can exactly infer who contributed. Some initial cooperators (seeing many defectors) will reduce or stop contributing; a small core of conditional cooperators may try again if they see enough others cooperating. Expect a drop in contributors, but a handful who value the public good or misinterpret others' incentives may continue.

Seeing that only a minority contributed in round 2 and defectors earned more, many conditional cooperators will switch to withdrawing. Expect continued decline: only a few stubborn cooperators remain (those with strong prosocial motives or misunderstanding incentives).

The pattern of exploitation is now clear: defectors earn a steady 20 coins more than cooperators. Many conditional cooperators will switch to withdrawing to avoid being exploited. A couple of persistent cooperators may briefly continue out of principle, but the group is moving toward universal defection.

At this point most players have learned that contributing is personally costly and that few others reciprocate. Only the most committed cooperator (if any) will continue to contribute. Expect a near-collapse of cooperation.

Facing persistent exploitation and the small public return from a lone cooperator, even the last cooperator is likely to switch to withdrawing. Rational players will defect; payoff reports will show everyone receiving the same private payoff (20) with no public return. From here on, universal defection is expected.
With everyone having observed round 6 payoffs showing no public return, there is no incentive to contribute. Expect continued universal defection.

Given persistent zero public return, cooperation remains off the table for self-interested players. Universal defection continues.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a binary public goods game: each player effectively chooses whether to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment (contribute = 20) or keep it privately (withdraw = 0). The sum of contributions is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. That makes free-riding individually attractive: any player who keeps their 20 gets that private benefit plus an equal share of the public multiplier, so defecting typically yields a higher round payoff than contributing when others contribute at least somewhat.

Because players do not see others' choices before deciding, they act on priors about others' cooperativeness and on feedback from round summaries. The summary shows each player's net payoff (so participants can infer who earned more and who likely contributed), and there is no communication. Typical dynamics in such settings are: relatively high cooperation at the start (hopes, conditional cooperation, social norms), followed by a decline as some players see defectors earning more and switch to free-riding. A minority of players who are "always cooperative" or who value the public good more may keep contributing; conditional cooperators tend to converge toward defection once they see cooperating is costly.

Given the provided player profiles, I model a mix of behavioral types:
- Always-cooperators (steady contributors): DUCK, RABBIT, FROG, COW, WHALE, HORSE.
- Always-defectors (steady withdrawers): GORILLA, CHICKEN, SNAKE, CROCODILE, ELEPHANT, PARROT.
- Conditional players (start cooperating, then react to payoffs): SLOTH, DOG, MOOSE, OWL, CHICK, PINGUIN, MONKEY. They begin by contributing but, seeing defectors earn more, most switch to defect over the first few rounds.

Expect cooperation to begin relatively high and then fall within the first few rounds, stabilizing at the contributions of the always-cooperators (6 players) for the later rounds in this simulated run.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players each round choosing whether to withdraw the whole 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). Each coin put into the pot is multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally among all 19 players. Individually, withdrawing is strictly attractive: keeping a coin yields you 1 privately, while a contributed coin returns only 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 to you—so contributors lose about 0.395 per coin relative to keeping it. Socially, however, contributions are efficient (every contributed coin generates 11.5 total group coins), so group payoffs rise when people cooperate. The game therefore sets up the classic free-rider tension: cooperation benefits the group but is costly to the individual.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here:
- Players do not know how many rounds remain, so there is no predetermined "last round" to defect in; that favors either steady conditional cooperation or cautious defection depending on risk tolerance.
- There is no communication, so players can only respond to observed payoffs (which reveal others’ behavior indirectly).
- The summary after each round shows everyone’s net payoff for that round, so players can infer who contributed (contributors receive 20 less than defectors, all else equal) and update beliefs.

Given the profiles, I predict a mix of motivations:
- Players with postgraduate degrees or older age (DOG, PARROT, OWL, CHICK) are more likely to cooperate initially or be conditional cooperators.
- Some younger or less-educated players (GORILLA, MONKEY, CHICKEN) are more likely to defect early, attracted by private gains.
- Many players will be conditional cooperators: they start cooperative or uncertain but shift toward defection when they see many free-riders. Over 10 rounds we should see initial cooperation dampen as defectors gain short-run advantage; a small core of steady cooperators (often those with stronger prosocial priors) may persist.

Now the simulated round-by-round transcript (choices are either 0 or 20; 20 = leave the 20 coins in the shared pot = cooperate; 0 = withdraw 20 = defect). Contributions are listed in the exact avatar order given.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a 19-player public goods game with a strong individual incentive to free-ride: each player chooses either to keep 20 coins (contribute 0) or to leave the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). All contributions are multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally across the 19 players. Contributing 20 increases the common pot by 230 coins, which raises each player's share by 230/19 ≈ 12.11 coins. Because a contributor forgoes 20 private coins to produce only about 12.11 back to themselves via the public fund, unilateral defection (keeping the 20) is privately profitable while damaging collective payoff.

Punishment can deter free-riding: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and reduces the punished player's payoff by 3 coins. Punishment is therefore costly and typically used sparingly in real experiments. Because players do not see who punished them and rounds are of unknown length, punishers pay a short-term cost to signal enforcement and to try to sustain cooperation; defectors face the risk of being targeted and losing more than the punisher spends, which can shift behavior toward cooperation over rounds.

Likely unfolding: early rounds start mixed (some cooperate, some defect) as players experiment. Defectors are occasionally punished by cooperators; those punishments are small but visible in round summaries. Over several rounds, punishments induce more cooperation and the group tends toward higher contribution rates. Because identifications are anonymous and the end is unknown, cooperative norms maintained by modest, targeted punishment can persist through later rounds. Punishment remains relatively sparse — used mainly to signal disapproval of clear free-riding rather than to impose large losses.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-player public goods game with a simple binary contribution decision each round: each player's endowment is effectively 20 coins in the shared pot and they can either keep (withdraw) the pot (contribute 0) or leave it untouched (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 11.5 and shared equally, so individual incentives favor free-riding (take the private benefit and still get a share of others' contributions). However, the punishment stage allows players to impose small costs on defectors at a personal cost: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and reduces the target by 3 coins. Punishment is anonymous (players see amounts punished and amounts deducted but not who did the punishing), and there is no known end round, so players may use punishment to sustain cooperation over time.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often have a mixture of cooperation and free-riding as players test others. Some players, often with stronger pro-social priors or greater patience, contribute and occasionally spend small sums on punishment to sanction clear free-riders. Because punishment is costly, it tends to be used sparingly and targeted at persistent defectors. Over repeated rounds we expect cooperation to rise as punishments discipline free-riding, though a few stubborn free-riders may persist and attract occasional punishments. Because players cannot identify punishers, punishment both enforces cooperation and creates some uncertainty (players infer who might have punished based on who contributed and who punished in the past).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with one key structural feature: each player faces 20 coins that start in the shared pot and must either leave them all in the pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them all for private use (contribute 0). Contributions are multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally among the active players (here, 19 players). The individual incentive is clear: withdrawing the 20 (i.e., defecting) always yields 20 coins more for you than contributing, because your private withdrawal is added on top of the equal share from the multiplied public pot. Numerically, each coin contributed returns 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 to each contributor personally — far less than the coin's private value — so free-riding is the dominant strategy.

Despite that, uncertainty about the number of rounds and the visibility of peers' round payoffs can sustain some cooperation early on. Players who are pro-social, inexperienced, or testing the environment (or who misunderstand payoff math) may contribute in early rounds. Others will quickly infer from visible round payoffs that defectors obtain strictly higher round payoffs and shift toward defection. Without communication and with the strong short-term incentive to withdraw, the likely dynamics are: a mix of cooperation and defection early, rapid learning and a drift toward defection over many rounds, occasional isolated or experimental cooperative moves by a few players, and repeated-payoff evidence pushing most toward consistent defection. Some players (the “stubborn cooperators”) may continue contributing sporadically despite the personal cost.

Below I simulate a plausible 10-round run given those incentives and the provided player profiles. I use the avatar order provided for all contribution lists. Each round shows the predicted contributions (20 = contribute, 0 = withdraw) and the round summary showing each player's net payoff (private withdrawal plus equal share of multiplied public pot). All monetary values are rounded to two decimals.

Late in the sequence, an individual (DOG) tries a lone contribution — perhaps a stubborn cooperator or someone probing for reciprocity. A single contribution yields a small share for everyone, but the cooperator's net is still below defectors'.

The total contributed is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally, so any individual who contributes loses private value relative to withholding (a contributor gives up 20 but receives only a fraction of the multiplied pot). Players do not know the total number of rounds, which supports sustained enforcement early on but also creates uncertainty: some players will experiment (cooperate or defect) to see others' responses; others will respond to punishment by switching to contribution. Over 10 rounds we typically see an initial mix, possible short-lived increase in cooperation if punishment is used effectively, then a gradual decline as the temptation to free-ride persists and punishment is costly. Punishment events are usually rare and directed at clear, repeated defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players per round. Each player starts with a 20-coin endowment placed in a shared pot and must choose to withdraw (take) all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0 to the public good) or withdraw nothing (leave 20 in the pot; contribute 20). The pot of contributions is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. Because each player's private withdrawal decision changes only their own private holding while the multiplied pot is shared, the individual incentive is always to withdraw (take) the 20 coins: taking yields the same public share but an extra 20 coins privately, so defection strictly dominates contribution in one-shot incentives.

Despite the dominant incentive to take, players often start with some cooperation for behavioral reasons (trust, altruism, reciprocity, experimentation). Because players do not observe others' choices before deciding but do observe payoffs afterwards (their own and peers'), the game gives information over time that reveals how many people contributed (inferred from the round payoffs). Since contributors always end up with 20 coins less than defectors in the same round (they get the same public share but no private 20), contributors will tend to earn less than defectors in each observed round. Over repeated rounds without a known end, this typically leads to declining cooperation: initial voluntary contributions may occur, but as players see contributors getting lower payoffs than noncontributors, they update toward withdrawing. Given the profiles supplied, I predict a modest number of contributors in round 1 (some older or highly-educated players inclined to try contributing), followed by a gradual decline in the number of contributors across rounds, reaching all-defection by the final rounds.

Uncertainties: individuals vary in intrinsic cooperation, risk preferences, or experiment length beliefs, so some players may persist in contributing for many rounds even when they are economically disadvantaged that round. The specific trajectory below reflects a plausible path: moderate early cooperation that decays as payoff information accumulates.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each player effectively chooses whether to contribute all 20 coins (contribute = 20) or withdraw them for private use (contribute = 0). The total contributions are multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. Because each contributed 20 returns about 12.11 to each player (if they were the only contributor), contributing is collectively beneficial (total group gain > cost) but individually costly: a contributor gets less private payoff than a withdrawer in the same round. That creates a classic tension: some players (especially those with stronger pro-social priors or education-related tendencies) may try to contribute early; many others will free-ride. Players see round payoffs for everyone after each round, so contributions are effectively revealed via payoff differences, and behavior will tend to drift toward withdrawal over time. However, occasional stubborn cooperators or late attempts to rebuild cooperation are plausible because players do not know when the game ends.

Below I simulate 10 rounds. For each round I give a short explanation of expected dynamics, the predicted contributions (in the exact avatar order), and the round summary showing each player's net payoff (rounded to two decimals). The share calculations used: total_contribution * 11.5 / 19. Individual net payoff = (20 if withdrew, 0 if contributed) + share.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start with no history. Some players with stronger pro-social priors (postgraduates, older players, or those who tend to cooperate in similar tasks) are likely to contribute. Others will try to free-ride. I predict a modest initial cooperation cluster (several contributors) and a majority of withdrawers exploiting that cooperation.

Players saw clear payoff differences: withdrawers earned about 20 more than contributors. Conditional cooperators start to defect; a smaller core of contributors remains (those who value the public good or hoped to seed cooperation). I predict a drop in contributors.

Seeing many players free-riding in round 2, more cooperators dial back. A small set of committed cooperators may persist for a while (some experimenters or conditional cooperators trying to stabilize cooperation), but numbers fall further.
Conditional cooperators respond to the pattern of many withdrawers; cooperation continues to shrink. A couple of players with stronger norms might still contribute, but contributions are low.
With diminishing returns and visible exploitation, most contributors give up. One committed player may still contribute out of principle or habit, but cooperative momentum is mostly gone.
At this point most players have learned that contributing is punished by free-riding. Cooperation collapses: nobody contributes; everyone keeps their private 20.

Even though cooperation collapsed, a few players may try to reintroduce contributions out of norm-driven motives or experiment. Expect at most one isolated contributor; others will continue to free-ride.

A small coordination attempt by one or two players (perhaps those who have been cooperative earlier) may occur, but most players stick to withdrawal. Expect a tiny uptick in contributions relative to rounds 6–7.
The small attempt in round 8 didn't entice others; free-riding remains dominant. Expect another round of universal withdrawal.

Even though players do not know the horizon, some may attempt a last-minute contribution (habit or hope). A few committed contributors might reappear; most will still withdraw. I predict a small cooperation attempt from familiar cooperators.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each round there are 20 coins in the shared pot and each player decides whether to withdraw them for private use (resulting in a contribution of 0) or leave them in the pot (contribution of 20). The shared pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. Because the per-coin return to the group is less than 1 for the individual (a full 20 contributed yields each player only 12.105... back), the one-shot dominant individual incentive is to withdraw (defect). However, some players may have pro-social preferences or may try cooperating when the horizon is unknown. With no communication and full visibility only after the round, we expect some initial cooperation from a subset of players (altruists or conditional cooperators), visible payoffs will show defectors getting higher round payoffs, and cooperation will generally unravel over rounds as contributors observe they earn less than withdrawers. Uncertainty about the number of rounds can sustain cooperation briefly, but absent coordination we expect contributions to decline and eventually disappear.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start without history and may rely on social preferences and priors. Some profiles (e.g., players with college or postgraduate education, retirees/homemakers, or those not currently employed) are slightly more likely to try contributing initially; others default to withdrawing. Because players don't know the number of rounds, a modest number may test cooperation this first round. I predict a moderate initial level of contributions from several pro-social players; most will withdraw.

Players see the round 1 payoffs: contributors earned substantially less than withdrawers. Many contributors revise downward (switch to withdrawing) to avoid being exploited; a handful of persistent cooperators (those with stronger pro-social priors) may continue to contribute to test or signal. Expect a drop in contributors.

The pattern continues: contributors are earning less than withdrawers, so more players switch to withdrawing. A small core of consistent cooperators (driven by strong social preferences or stubbornness) may remain. Expect only a few contributors.

With contributions now small, the advantage of withdrawing remains clear. A couple of players may still contribute out of habit or strong preferences, but numbers will continue to fall. Expect only 2 contributors.
Most players have now observed the payoff gap. Only a single persistent cooperator (or an experimental holdout) may keep contributing; others withdraw. Expect 1 contributor.

The single remaining contributor sees a clear disadvantage relative to withdrawers; they may persist once more out of preference or may still defect next round. Here I predict one last solitary contribution (stubborn pro-social player), but the incentives push toward universal withdrawal.
By now the pattern is clear: contributing yields a lower payoff than withdrawing, so remaining contributors stop. Expect all players to withdraw (no contributions).
With universal withdrawal established and no incentive to unilaterally return to contributing, the group remains at zero contributions.
No new incentives arise to change behavior; cooperation remains absent.
By round 10 the dominant strategy is clear and no-one has incentive to contribute unilaterally; everyone withdraws.

game_explanation:
This public goods game is a classic social dilemma: each round every player either keeps the full 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leaves the full 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally across the 18 players, so each contributed 20 yields 230 total to divide, giving about 12.78 back to each player. Contributing is socially efficient (the group gains), but individually costly (one loses 20 privately and receives only ~12.78 back), so the one-shot incentive is to withdraw (defect). The punishment mechanism lets players impose costs on others at personal cost (2 coins per punishment unit to the source, 3 coins taken from the target per unit), which can sustain cooperation over repeated interaction even when punishers are themselves paying a cost.

Given players do not know how many rounds remain and cannot identify who punished them, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: uncertainty leads many to defect; a subset of more prosocial or reputation-minded players start by contributing.
- Punishment will be used sparingly to sanction clear free-riders. Because punishment is costly and anonymous, only a few players usually pay to punish, targeted at persistent defectors.
- Over rounds, sanctions and conditional cooperation typically increase contribution rates. Occasional defections or “relapses” can occur and attract isolated sanctions.
- Because players cannot see exactly who punished them, punishers may continue only if they believe the punishment influences group behavior; otherwise punishment use tapers off as cooperation stabilizes.
Below is a predicted transcript for rounds 1–10 showing these plausible dynamics.
