predicted_rounds.explanation: First round uncertainty: many players are likely to defect by default; a minority of more prosocial or status/age-associated players (e.g., GORILLA, PARROT, OWL, SNAKE, COW, ELEPHANT) are expected to contribute. Some of the cooperators will use low-level punishment to signal that defection will be costly, but punishment is sparse because it carries a personal cost.

The first round's punishments are visible in summaries (who was punished, and how much was deducted), so some punished defectors may switch to contributing to avoid future costs. Cooperation is expected to rise modestly. Punishment remains targeted but limited to a couple persistent defectors.

Seeing more contributors, a few marginal players join cooperation (e.g., DOG). A small number of defectors remain; targeted punishment of the persistent free-riders continues but is restrained so punishers don't overpay.

Some previously-punished defectors (e.g., DUCK) respond by cooperating. A handful of players still resist cooperating; cooperators continue light, targeted enforcement directed at those remaining defectors.

Cooperation grows as many players have experienced the cost of being punished. Only a small subset remain defecting (those who either tolerate punishment or haven't been effectively targeted). Punishment events are few and concentrated.
Two or three holdouts remain. Punishers continue to target persistent defectors but still do so sparingly. Some holdouts may change after sustained penalties.
Sustained mild sanctions and the visibility of group benefits encourage near-universal cooperation. At this stage we expect most players to contribute; punishing becomes unnecessary and is paused.

With cooperation high, a slight temptation or slip can cause an occasional unilateral defection. When that happens, a couple of previously active punishers may impose a small penalty to deter relapse. Otherwise cooperation remains the norm.
The punished player (SLOTH) returns to cooperating after the isolated sanction. The group largely sustains cooperation, and punishment activity drops to zero.

Players do not know when the experiment ends, so cooperation remains high. However, occasional opportunistic defections (e.g., one player testing the payoff) can occur in late rounds; these typically elicit a single small punitive response from a neighbor or long-term cooperator.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game combines a stark binary choice each round (contribute all 20 coins to the public pot or withdraw all 20 for private use) with costly peer punishment. Incentives: if a player contributes, everyone benefits from the multiplied public pot (11.5×) split equally; if a player withdraws, they privately keep the 20 but reduce the group payoff. Punishment allows cooperators to deter free-riding, but it is costly (each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target), so players typically use it sparingly and selectively.

Given the player mix and uncertainty about the total number of rounds, I expect:
- Early rounds will start mixed: a majority of conditionally cooperative players will contribute, while a minority will free-ride.
- Targeted, light punishments will be used against clear defectors. Punishment is often sparse (one or two small units from a couple of cooperators) rather than heavy collective sanctioning.
- Punishment will cause many defectors to switch to contributing in subsequent rounds. After a few rounds of successful sanctioning, contribution rates should rise and often stabilize near full cooperation.
- Occasional lapses (temptation to defect) will occur later in the sequence; cooperators may respond again with light punishment. Because players do not know when the game ends, there is not a strong deterministic last-round collapse, but some noise in the final rounds is common.

All these tendencies will be reflected in the transcript below: a noisy start with a handful of defectors, a few targeted punishments, rising cooperation, occasional isolated defections and sparing punishment responses.

Punished players are likely to move toward cooperation; some early testers (who weren't punished) may continue to defect. Cooperators who were harmed by free riders will punish remaining defectors but still sparingly.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods environment with 19 players. Each round every player privately chooses to either keep the 20 coins for themselves (contribute 0) or leave those 20 coins in the group pot (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split evenly among all 19 players. Because the per-player return from one player's 20-coin contribution is (11.5*20)/19 ≈ 12.11, each individual gets less than the 20 they would keep by withdrawing; withdrawing (not contributing) is the strict individual best response in any single-shot round. However, the multiplier is large enough that collective welfare is much higher when many people contribute.

Given players do not see others' choices before deciding and they do not know how many rounds the game will last, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about horizon and reciprocity/conditional cooperation tendencies mean some players will try contributing in round 1 (hoping to trigger mutual cooperation).
- After the first summary, contributors can be easily identified (they earn exactly 20 fewer than defectors that round), so many conditional cooperators will switch to withdrawing in subsequent rounds because they observe they were exploited. Stubborn or strongly prosocial players (or those who misinterpret payoffs) may continue to contribute for some rounds.
- Over time, contributions tend to fall toward full defection: free-riding is individually profitable and information revealed each round makes exploitation visible, accelerating the collapse of cooperation. With no group chat and no known end, cooperation is fragile and typically decays.

I will simulate a plausible trajectory across 10 rounds, starting with moderate cooperation and declining to near-universal withdrawal by rounds 7–10. Each round I show the reasoning, the contribution vector in the exact avatar order, and the round summary (each player's net payoff for that round).

Because withdrawing gave a clear 20-coin advantage, several first-round contributors are likely to switch to withdrawing. game_explanation: This public goods game pits 19 anonymous players against a tension between private extraction (taking the 20 coins) and contributing the whole pot (leaving 20 in the public pot). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and shared equally, so cooperation benefits everyone, but a single defector can gain privately if others contribute. The punishment stage allows contributors to impose costs on perceived free-riders at personal cost. Because punishment is costly, players tend to use it sparingly to establish norms: a few early punishments of clear free-riders can sustain high cooperation afterward. Uncertainties include individual preferences (some players may be more inclined to free-ride or to punish), the anonymity of punishers (so retaliation is noisy), and the fact that players don't know the number of rounds (reducing classic end-game collapse). Expect: a mix of contributions early, a few targeted punishments to discourage freeloading, then generally high cooperation with occasional lapses that are lightly sanctioned.

Punishments and the established norm keep cooperation high; the group contributes fully and no one is punished.
Continuing the pattern, players maintain cooperation. Because players do not know when the game ends, there is no final-round rush to defect; no punishments occur.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with a binary decision each round: each player either leaves all 20 of their coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute 0). The sum of contributions is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all active players. Because the private withdrawal (if you take 20) does not reduce your share of the multiplied pot, withdrawing is the individually dominant action: whatever others do, taking the 20 and keeping the shared payoff gives you 20 more than contributing. Socially, however, contributions increase the total multiplied pot a lot (230 per extra full contribution), so group welfare is maximized when many contribute.

Uncertainty and learning: players do not see others' choices before deciding, but after each round they see numerical round payoffs for themselves and for every peer. Because withdrawers end up 20 higher than contributors (everyone gets the same share from the multiplied pot), players can infer who withdrew and who contributed from the summary. That makes behavior observable indirectly and allows conditional strategies to develop: some players may start cooperative (hoping others will too), then switch to withdrawing after observing the payoff advantage of free-riding. Player profiles provide priors about motivations: postgraduates and older players (e.g., CHICKEN, RABBIT, HORSE, ELEPHANT) are more likely to be willing to sustain contributions; unemployed or pragmatically employed players may prefer immediate private cash (e.g., DUCK, OWL). Over ten rounds, I expect an initial mixed level of cooperation (some contributors), followed by gradual erosion of contributions as players learn free-riding is individually profitable, leaving only a small core of persistent cooperators for a few rounds before full defection.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players enter without information about group play. A modest cluster of likely cooperators (postgraduates and some college-educated players) try contributing on round 1; others withdraw to secure private cash. Because rewards are large when many contribute, optimistic or pro-social players (CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, RABBIT, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, ELEPHANT, HORSE) contribute; the remainder withdraw. Players will see round payoffs and infer who free-rode.

After seeing round 1 payoffs, many players notice withdrawers earned 20 more. Some first-round contributors who saw little reciprocal support (or who are pragmatic) switch to withdrawing. A smaller core (CHICKEN, PARROT, RABBIT, COW, MONKEY, HORSE) persist in contributing to test whether a stable cooperative cluster can form.

Round 2 confirms the private advantage of withdrawing. Several marginal cooperators drop out; a small set of committed contributors (CHICKEN, RABBIT, ELEPHANT, HORSE) continue, but the total contribution level falls further. Observers will see contributors' payoffs drop relative to withdrawers.

Contributors are now a small minority. Conditional cooperators see their payoff shrinking relative to withdrawers and many abandon contribution. A persistent subset (CHICKEN, RABBIT, HORSE) stick with contribution for social preference reasons or optimism that others might return.

The pattern from round 4 reinforces the lesson: withdrawing yields 20 more than contributing. A small group maintains contributions out of intrinsic motivation or habit (same trio continues), but no broad recovery occurs.

Two of the remaining cooperators tire of losing relative to withdrawers and stop contributing. Only the most persistent pair (RABBIT and HORSE) remain as contributors this round; the share drops accordingly. Observers will see contributors now earn substantially less than withdrawers.

With contributions yielding very low returns, most contributors give up. Only a single stubborn cooperator (HORSE) contributes this round, possibly due to a strong pro-social preference or a testing tactic. The group payoff is very small; withdrawers enjoy higher payoffs.
By now the pattern is clear: withdrawing is always individually better. The last cooperator stops contributing; everyone withdraws. Group earnings equal private withdrawals only.
With everyone withdrawing last round and the private incentive confirmed, full defection persists.
No new information suggests a reversal; all players withdraw again. The group remains at the individually stable equilibrium of full defection.

The group pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players, so a single contribution increases everyone’s payoff by 230/19 ≈ 12.11 coins. That means: if you are deciding unilaterally, defecting (withdrawing 20) always gives you 20 extra private coins relative to contributing, so one-shot incentive is to defect. But if many people cooperate, everyone (including contributors) gets a large share — all-contribute yields 230 coins each, far higher than all-defect (20 each).

Given no communication and unknown horizon, we expect initially many players to try cooperation (especially those with more cooperative priors), but because defectors earn more in each round, free-riding spreads. Some players (e.g., profile-indicated high education or older players who appear more conditional-cooperative) will try to sustain cooperation for moral or long-term payoff reasons and may re-enter contribution when group returns fall. The visible round-by-round payoffs (each player sees peers' net payoffs after each round) create feedback: defectors are often rewarded immediately, leading to waves of defection; occasional resurgences of cooperation can occur when a critical mass of contributors reappears or some players attempt to “restart” cooperation. Over ten rounds we therefore expect early-to-mid cooperation, a decline as defection spreads, and fluctuations thereafter as some players attempt to re-establish contributions.

Some (by name or profile—e.g., SLOTH, CHICKEN, PARROT, PINGUIN, HORSE) withdraw immediately to test the rewards of free-riding. The immediate payoff advantage for defectors will be visible in the summary, setting the stage for further switching.

Players see defectors earned ~20 more than contributors in round 1. Some conditional cooperators switch to defect; a core of contributors remains (those with stronger cooperative priors). Expect about a dozen contributors now.

Seeing defectors continue to earn more, more players switch to withdrawing. Only a smaller core of cooperators remains (those prioritizing the public good or trusting others). Expect around 8 contributors.

As the pattern of higher immediate returns from defecting is reinforced, only a small committed minority contributes (often older/higher-education profiles who keep trying to maintain the public good). Expect ~5 contributors.

Seeing low group returns, some players with cooperative priors (or who prefer trying to restore higher group payoffs) attempt to re-establish contributions. A modest resurgence occurs — a few more players contribute again. Expect ~6 contributors.

The modest return to cooperation begins to look promising to others — a few additional players rejoin contributors (conditional cooperators responding to the improved per-player share). Expect ~7 contributors.

As the visible group payout improves (per-player share is becoming substantial), more players re-enter contributions hoping higher group returns will persist. Without communication, re-entry is partial but noticeable. Expect around 10 contributors.

Seeing a clear improvement in payoffs when many contribute, several more conditional cooperators (and some earlier defectors) switch back to contributing. This creates a larger cooperative cluster. Expect around 13 contributors.

Players still don’t know the final round. The recent success of cooperation convinces many to keep contributing; a small set of consistent defectors remain (testing and free-riding). Expect a fairly large cooperative majority (~15 contributors).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each round everyone faces a binary choice: contribute the full 20 coins to the shared pot (cooperate) or withdraw 20 coins for private use (defect), so each player's contribution is either 0 or 20. All contributions are pooled, multiplied by 11.5, and then split equally among the 19 players. Individually, keeping the 20 coins always gives a private advantage of 20 over contributing, because everyone receives the same share of the amplified pot regardless of their own choice. However, socially the group does best when more people contribute (total payoff rises with more contributors).

Because players do not see others' choices before deciding and cannot communicate, behavior will be guided by priors about others, observed round payoffs, and simple conditional rules (“if many people contributed last round, I’ll contribute; if not, I’ll free‑ride”). In an environment with an unknown number of rounds, we typically expect some initial cooperation from those with prosocial priors (players with higher education, older players, or those not currently in paid work who may be more willing to contribute), followed by a gradual drift toward defection as the private incentive to free‑ride becomes clear from the payoff summaries. However, a small core of steady cooperators can persist throughout, producing fluctuations rather than a monotonic collapse. Each round's summary shows every player's net payoff, so players can infer how many others contributed and update strategies accordingly.

Below is a simulated transcript of rounds 1–10 following these likely dynamics: moderate initial cooperation, a decline as free‑riding is rewarded, occasional rebounds when a few stable cooperators persist or briefly return to contributing, and persistent heterogeneity across players driven by the provided profiles.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round: no prior observations. Some players with prosocial priors or strong reciprocity tendencies (older, higher-education, or not in paid work) are likely to contribute out of fairness or expectation that others will do the same. Others—young, unemployed, or less trusting players—are more likely to take the private 20 immediately. With no communication and symmetric payoffs, we expect a modest majority to try contributing, but several free‑riders will also appear.

Players saw round 1 payoffs and noticed defectors earned 20 more than cooperators. Some initial cooperators who value reciprocity may continue contributing (they expect others like them to stay cooperative), but a number will switch to defect to exploit the advantage. Expect a drop in contributions, but a core of conditional cooperators will remain.

Seeing the clear advantage to defect, more players switch to keeping the 20. A smaller set of steady cooperators (those who prefer fairness or are optimistic about conditional cooperation) remain contributing. Expect further decline in contributions and lower per‑person shared returns.

As players update, expected cooperators are now a small minority. Those who continue to contribute may be driven by intrinsic preferences or mistaken expectations that others will return to cooperation. Most players will defect to secure the immediate +20 advantage.

Contributors are now rare. Many players have converged to defect because it’s consistently profitable. A few stubborn or prosocial players (often older or highly educated in the profiles) may still contribute, but overall group payoff is low.

Some players (perhaps CROCODILE, a postgraduate who returned to contributing) try to boost the public pot again, hoping to encourage reciprocation or out of intrinsic cooperation. This produces a small uptick in contributions and shared returns, but defect still pays more per round.

A small cluster of steady cooperators (including MONKEY this round) coordinate only implicitly via payoffs: they resume contributing in hopes of higher group returns. This yields a modest improvement in shared income, and some players may briefly switch depending on the relative appeal of 20 private vs. the improved public share.

The slight rebound in round 7 shows that cooperation can re-emerge, but many players still prefer the sure private 20. A reversion toward more defection is likely when the incentive to free-ride remains salient.

With the unknown horizon, some players try again to re-establish cooperation while others continue to defect. Expect another small cluster of contributors, similar to round 7, producing a modest temporary improvement.

By round 10, patterns are established: a small core of contributors remains (often the same few profiles), while most players defect each round to secure the private 20. Cooperation can persist sporadically, but defection continues to dominate individually.

game_explanation:
This public goods game gives each player a binary choice each round: take the full 20 coins for private use (defect) or leave them in the shared pot (cooperate). The group return is large (the shared pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally), so when many players cooperate everyone benefits, but each individual who defects gets the private 20 immediately while still sharing in others' cooperation. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding and do not know how many rounds remain, we expect a mix of behavior: many players will try cooperation early on, hoping to gain from mutual contributions; a few will free-ride occasionally to test or exploit cooperators. Punishment is available but costly (2 coins to punish, 3 coins removed from the target per punishment unit), so players punish sparingly and typically only when they observe defection. Because punishers are anonymous, defectors learn only that they were punished, not who punished them; that tends to produce targeted but limited retaliation or quick correction by punished players rather than long, costly vendettas.

Likely unfolding: initial rounds show substantial cooperation with a handful of free-riders. Cooperators use small, targeted punishments to discourage free-riding; punished players often switch back to cooperating in subsequent rounds. Occasional experiments (one-off defections) appear throughout the game as players probe whether cooperation is still being enforced. Punishment instances are sparse and focused on clear defectors rather than broad retribution.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start with little information about others. Many will try cooperating to test whether mutual cooperation pays off. Some risk-averse or opportunistic players will defect immediately. Early punishments are likely targeted at visible defectors as a signal to enforce norms.

Players see summaries from round 1 and can respond. Those who were punished may switch to cooperating; cooperators continue contributing but still watch for defectors. A couple of players may continue to defect if they think occasional free-riding will go unpunished.
With most players cooperating, remaining defectors face more pressure. A lone defector may be punished multiple times; cooperators mostly keep contributing to maintain group gains.
Punishments in earlier rounds can deter repeat defection, but opportunistic players may test the norm. Here one player tries free-riding and is likely to be targeted by a few punishers.
After enforcement in prior rounds, most players return to cooperation. With cooperation high, there is little incentive to punish, so expect a round with no punishment.
Sustained cooperation makes a brief defection attractive to some players. A single defection will typically attract a couple of small punishments rather than broad retaliation.
Punished defectors often revert to cooperation. Seeing the cost of punishment and most others cooperating, players typically return to contributing.
Occasional one-off defections continue as players probe whether enforcement remains. Those who defect are usually hit with a couple of small punishments.
After being punished, most one-off defectors switch back to contributing. Cooperation is restored and no punishment is necessary.
Even late in the sequence, without knowledge of the final round count, players sometimes try a last-minute defection. Expect a small number of punishments targeting that defector.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players, binary contribution choices each round (either contribute the full 20 or withdraw it all), an unusually high multiplier (11.5) on the public pot, and the option to punish at a cost. Because players do not know the number of rounds and cannot communicate, typical dynamics are: (1) modest-to-strong initial cooperation driven by uncertainty and conditional prosocial preferences, (2) some free-riding from selfish players, (3) limited, targeted punishment by some cooperators to discourage defectors, (4) adaptation over rounds — punished defectors sometimes switch to cooperating while some cooperators may “give up” and start defecting if they see many free riders, and (5) gradual erosion of cooperation toward the end as forward-looking incentives decline (the “endgame” effect), producing more defection and less punishment. Punishments are costly for enforcers, so they tend to be used sparingly and directed mainly at repeat defectors. Player profiles (age, education, employment) act as soft priors: older and higher-education players may be somewhat more likely to cooperate and to punish defection; younger or more opportunistic profiles may defect more often. I will simulate these tendencies across 10 rounds, keeping punishments modest and plausible.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round uncertainty usually produces relatively high cooperation but a small set of definite free-riders appear. Some cooperators may use punishment to signal that defection will be costly, but punishment is kept light because it is costly to send.
Some punished defectors may respond by cooperating; others may persist. Overall cooperation edges up slightly as a couple of early defectors switch to avoid repeated penalties.
Cooperation largely holds but a few players test free-riding again. Punishment remains targeted at repeat defectors rather than broad retaliation.
Some players who saw low immediate returns or who are opportunistic begin to defect; cooperators continue to punish select repeat defectors to try to sustain contribution levels.
As some players perceive free-riding is profitable or punishment insufficiently deterring, defection increases. Punishment remains present but scarce because senders bear a cost.
Some punished players change strategy (cooperate); others persist. Punishers persist in targeting visible repeat defectors but remain cautious because punishment is costly.
Mid-game dynamics: cooperation slips further as more players experiment with defection when enforcement appears sporadic. Punishments stay targeted at the most persistent defectors.
Cooperation continues to erode; a few more players defect (endgame expectations begin to matter even though the horizon is unknown). Punishment remains limited and selective.

Approaching the later rounds, many players who prefer the private payoff defect; a few cooperators continue to punish repeat defectors out of norm enforcement or spite, but such punishments are sparse.

Final-round unraveling is common when players suspect or prefer defecting with no future enforcement. Punishment declines further because there is little to gain from enforcing future cooperation; a final spiteful punishment may still appear.
